File.Name: da23f_da23-008.jpg
Notes: 10448612794 This is a droopy-looking plant of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, growing up and over a shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see it has red berries on the umbrella-like "perfoliate" last leaves. The berries are dull red instead of brilliant like they should be. Compare this photo from last year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 October 2013)
ScientificName: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da24a_da24-001.jpg
Notes:
10467921353 "They're young. They're in love. They munch leaves by the river."

Here are a sweet pair of Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) by the river today - at a place that still holds some water. That "forked horn" buck on the right survived the hunting season. I tried to slowly move my tripod closer, but then they were gone. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2013)

I went back to the river today to revisit a favorite place of mine that was burned by the White Fire last May. Some plants are regenerating, but everything is waiting for rain.

File.Name: da24b_da24-004.jpg
Notes: 10467691433 I believe these are new flowers of native Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae. It's a leafy shrub that grows 5-8 feet tall with yellow composite flowers that have ray-flowers. It's quite pretty and rather uncommon on the dry wash by the river. I thought this population was burned out by the White Fire last May, but a few shrubs survived. Other burned plants are stump-sprouting. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2013)

File.Name: da24c_da24-020.jpg
Notes: 10467416455 These are still new yellow composite flowers of native Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing along the river. It's a slender plant with long slender leaves and small ray flowers that favors marshy places. It doesn't look anything like a classic goldenrod. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2013)

File.Name: da24d_da24-007.jpg
Notes:
10467289974 This is a stump-sprouting - and flowering! - native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family growing on burned soil by the river. If you look close, you can see a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly working the flowers on the right. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2013)

I tried to get photos of the butterflies today, but it was too windy.

File.Name: da24e_da24-008.jpg
Notes: 10466978214 This is a grand ol' Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family which was burned in the White Fire last May. The tree has a bit of green in its leaves, and I hope it makes it. Most of the landscape is pretty moon-like. In the foreground, you can see a few other survivors - stump-sprouting Bicolored Yerba Santa on the left, flowering Scale Broom on the right, and a Chaparral Yucca in the left-front. I hope this will be full of life once our winter rains finally come. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2013)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Bicolored Yerba Santa; Scale Broom; Chaparral Yucca

File.Name: da24f_da24-024.jpg
Notes: 10466880475 Of course even Lichens burn in a wildfire. Rocks get singed too. This lichen was in the path of the White Fire last May. I wonder if it will regenerate when the rains come? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 24 October 2013)

File.Name: da25a_da25-011.jpg
Notes:
10484781706 The predator becomes prey!

This adult predator Assassin Bug in the family Reduviidae of the old insect order Hemiptera has become prey for a white Crab Spider (Thomisidae). See this photo of an assassin bug as predator. I found this scene today on a plant of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek. I reckon this is one of my favorite photos. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

Spring is full of life, but in some ways autumn is my favorite time as a photographer. It's a time when you have to hunt for photo-ops as a naturalist, using everything you know as your guide. I set out with my camera every day expecting to find nothing, and so far I've been lucky enough to capture these amazing scenes. Now, what about tomorrow...
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Thomisidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Thomisidae; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: da25b_da25-022.jpg
Notes: 10484368526 This is a cute little furry Jumping Spider in (I think) the genus Phidippus of the family Salticidae. It's perched on a stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It seems to have an odd attachment to this odd "stone house" - a nest that looks like it was made by a terrestrial Caddisfly. See this photo from last year. I guess it's the pupal case of a moth. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

File.Name: da25c_da25-026.jpg
Notes: 10484207134 Here is a female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working new autumn flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. I've been seeing them fluttering by, but I haven't gotten a photo since July. The male butterflies get all the glory, but the ladies are quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)
ScientificName: Colias eurydice; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Colias eurydice; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Lessingia filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: da25d_da25-016.jpg
Notes:
10483940074 This is a "Stink Bug" in the family Pentatomidae of the old insect order Hemiptera. Maybe it's a Green Stink Bug in the genus Chinavia of the tribe Nezarini? This one has its beak stuck in the phyllary of a female pistillate flower of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

These bugs have remarkable beaks. I'm not sure of the anatomy, but I think the thin front stylets are sheathed by the sturdy rear labium. It's interesting to see them in separate pieces like this.
CommonNames: Stink Bug; Green Stink Bug; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Nezarini; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; beaks; stylets; labium
associatedTaxa: Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Chinavia; Nezarini; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; beaks; stylets; labium

File.Name: da25e_da25-001.jpg
Notes: 10483707684 This little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) is catching some morning sunshine on a cool morning. It's resting on a beautiful old Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae) stump on the edge of the garden. It reminds me of our cats laying in the sun. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: da25f_da25-039.jpg
Notes: 10483598175 This is another large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our house. I got too close and it's scooting away from me up its web. See this photo of a different spider from two days ago. Hooray, we have at least two of these large spiders! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

File.Name: da26a_da26-001.jpg
Notes:
10503884304 Here is a sweet female Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) high-stepping through the native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family above our garden. I got this photo from our upstairs porch with my 105 mm macro lens, which also works great as a moderate telephoto. Maybe she hasn't noticed me yet? This was my first photo this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)

Today was a another beautiful day in paradise, though it was breezy all morning when I was out getting photos. We have the chance of another small rain on Monday.
CommonNames: Southern Mule Deer; Giant Rye Grass
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae

File.Name: da26b_da26-014.jpg
Notes:
10503896913 This is another odd-looking Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera. I found it today on a leaf of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. They have a striking shape and remarkable speckled eyes. Compare this photo from a few days ago to see how much their color varies. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)

I don't think of these as autumn insects, so I checked my archives to just when I've gotten photos before - October, July, and April. I rarely find them, but that's no sign of how common they are. They are small and easy to overlook.
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: da26c_da26-017.jpg
Notes: 10503573673 This is another odd-looking Chaparral Monkey Grasshopper (Morsea californica) in the Eumastacidae family of the Orthoptera. I found it today on a leaf of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. Along with a striking shape, they have remarkable speckled eyes. Compare this photo from a few days ago to see how much their color varies. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)
ScientificName: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae
associatedTaxa: Morsea californica; Eumastacidae; Orthoptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae

File.Name: da26d_da26-027.jpg
Notes: 10503244245 These are pom-pom-like seed heads of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family. This one is growing over a shrub of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They are prettier in early summer - or is it just the autumn lighting? Compare this photo of the same seed heads. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da26e_da26-021.jpg
Notes:
10503002865 This is a remarkable gall of the Spined Turban Gall Wasp (Antron douglasii) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on the underside of a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They look like little mushrooms growing from the oak leaf - I can even see rounded "gills", like a Chanterelle! They are really the "nests" of tiny wasps - made by the oaks. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)

I know, I've shown this same gall before - most recently in this photo. It's so striking that I don't mind showing it again. It seems to be developing more color as it matures.
CommonNames: Spined Turban Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Antron douglasii; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da26f_da26-009.jpg
Notes:
10502814883 These are galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The mushroom-like overlapping base of the gall distinguishes them from similar galls. They are growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. They produce real "oak honey" on their surface, which attract ants (family Formicidae, Hymenoptera) which might protect them from other wasp predators. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)

I know, I've shown these same galls before in this photo. If you compare the two photos, it's interesting that most of the leaves at the end of the stem have died and fallen away. Galls are "nutrient sinks" that divert nutrients from the leaves beyond. They do cause some damage to the shrub or tree.
CommonNames: Clasping Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; ants
ScientificName: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; do
associatedTaxa: Disholcaspis prehensa; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; do

File.Name: da27a_da27-014.jpg
Notes:
10526105375 "Leaving the nest..."

Here are new seeds of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family going off to meet their destiny. The dandelion-type seeds are definitely dispersed by the wind. There's some debate about whether the flowers are also wind-pollinated. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2013)

Today was another beautiful day with no indication of an approaching storm as forecast. It has gotten colder and the wind is starting to pick up, so maybe that's a sign.

File.Name: da27b_da27-022.jpg
Notes:
10526102693 I'm pretty sure this the gall of a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella or kin) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the order Lepidoptera. They cause these swollen stems on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's interesting how the stipules of the leaves become exaggerated on these galled stems. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2013)

I know this photo isn't quite sharp, but I like the light and composition more than my sharper photos.
CommonNames: Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth; Greenbark Ceanothus
ScientificName: Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: da27c_da27-011.jpg
Notes: 10525462346 The blisters on this leaf are different galls on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I don't find it in my gall book, but there are definitely critters in there. (San Marcos Pass, 27 October 2013)

File.Name: da27d_da25-003.jpg
Notes:
10525025536 I'm pretty sure this is the work of a real "leaf roller gall" (my name) on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, both below the leaf (left) and above (right). I gently rotated the leaf on the left onto it's back. Note that the leaves roll under rather than over the leaves like the critters in my [Previous] photos. See this photo from last spring. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

You can also see the "hairy armpits" of the oak leaf I've turned over. That's one sure-fire way to always recognize Coast Live Oak, even when it grows like a scrub oak.

File.Name: da27e_da25-006.jpg
Notes:
10524665064 This is a "rolled leaf" on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. There's a critter in there, but is it a gall or a mechanically rolled leaf? The leaf looks discolored like a gall, and see my [Previous] photo of maybe the same critter. I'm still not sure if this counts as a gall. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

"Leaf rollers" and "gall insects" really have a similar strategy - to have the plant provide food and shelter. The more I think about it, the more similar they seem. Both kinds of insect get shelter and food from the host plant. And how can one folded leaf provide all the sustenance that an insect larvae needs without some help? Now that I think about it, how does the insect fold the leaf UP rather than DOWN in its natural direction? Is it a gall?

I may have a photo of the same leaf taken a year ago. See this photo. That's one thing about hiking the same trails over and over again - even single leaves get familiar!

File.Name: da27f_da25-007.jpg
Notes: 10524602953 This is an old "rolled oak leaf" on native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's rolled onto the topside of the leaf. You can look into it, and it sure looks like abnormal leaf growth inside. I suspect that it's a gall. See my [Next] photo of maybe the same critter. (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

File.Name: da28a_da28-002.jpg
Notes:
10547495383 This is a sweet little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae, order Diptera) perched on a twig of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family on the wild edge of our garden this morning. It was cold for us, now - in the low 40s - and it let me get close before flying away. (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2013)

We did have a bit of rain today as promised, but just a bit - less than half of 1/10th inch. The winds haven't kicked up yet, and there's still some drizzle in the air.

File.Name: da28b_da26-003.jpg
Notes:
10546950154 Here is another photo of the sweet female Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) that I found above our garden two days ago. She is a lovely creature, though she has a "deer in the headlights" look after noticing me taking her picture from the upstairs porch. After that, she looked like my [Previous] photo! (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)

This is an interesting bit of woodland - I can see stump-sprouting live oak, wild blackberry, snowberry, giant rye grass, coffeeberry, and hummingbird sage - all growing in a tangle.

File.Name: da28c_da26-004.jpg
Notes:
10546584324 Invisible deer!

This is a sweet female Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) being invisible behind a native shrub of Coffeeberry (Rhamnus californica) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Talk about camouflage! I'm sure I've walked right past many of them over the years without noticing - maybe not within spitting distance, but for sure within throwing distance. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Rhamnus californica; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: da28d_da28-003.jpg
Notes:
10546089426 This is an old gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls are "polythalamous" - hosting many wasp larvae, and you can see some exit holes where the critters got away. Or did something else get in? (San Marcos Pass, 28 October 2013)

These large galls are about the same tangerine-size as the classic "oak apple galls" on Valley Oak and Scrub Oak, but they have a different texture and life cycle. The cynipid wasps responsible make two different galls in a single season - see this photo. The wasps that form the other galls appear to be parthenogenic and only form the one gall.

I've been looking for new galls in Live Oak this summer, but I haven't found them. Then I noticed this gall a few days ago in the oak tree about three feet from where I sit on our upstairs porch! It took a drizzly day to get the right photo.
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Valley Oak; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da28e_da26-032.jpg
Notes: 10545957946 I believe these are the knobby galls of the Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp (Andricus chrysolepidicola) in the family of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). They are growing on a stem of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. They are very hard wood, and I wonder if they would make good pipes? (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)
CommonNames: Irregular Spindle Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasps; Scrub Oak
ScientificName: Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus chrysolepidicola; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: da28f_da26-020.jpg
Notes: 10545699674 This is an odd one. It's definitely a native Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria) in the Asteraceae plant family, but which one? It has the stature of the Small Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria exigua), only one foot tall or less, and this is the plant which grows nearby. But it has more petals and the short peduncles of Rod Wirelettuce(Stephanomeria virgata). Maybe it's been browsed by the horse that sometimes walks this same trail? I don't know what it is, but it's rather pretty and has lots of late-season flowers for the wild bees. It seems unusually green. (San Marcos Pass, 26 October 2013)
ScientificName: Stephanomeria; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria exigua; Stephanomeria virgata
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria exigua; Stephanomeria virgata

File.Name: da29a_da29-024.jpg
Notes:
10566064946 I believe this is a female Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis or P. albescens) butterfly. It has golden wings. Maybe I have my genders wrong - compare this photo.

This one is working still-flowering yellow composite flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). Their host plant is the native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family, which grows nearby. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2013)

Darn, I got called for Jury Duty tomorrow. Now who would schedule a court case to begin (nearly) at Halloween? Well it's a duty, I believe in the system, and I have no good excuse - except for missing a few days for new photos! Fortunately, the recent archives are in good shape. I've served on two juries so far, and I recommend the experience.
Locality: nearly) at Halloween?  Well it's a duty

File.Name: da29b_da29-031.jpg
Notes:
10565659916 I guess this is a Pallid-winged Grasshopper (Trimerotropis pallidipennis) - a Band-winged Grasshopper, subfamily Oedipodinae, in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera. It's well-camouflaged against the soil. There were quite a few of these today. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2013)

My back is slowly getting better, so I can actually bend down to photograph these critters!

File.Name: da29c_da29-006.jpg
Notes:
10565516554 This little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) is posing for me on an old stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia, Fagaceae). It's shedding it's old skin, but it's also remarkably well camouflaged. (San Marcos Pass, 29 October 2013)

I only find these small lizards on these chilly mornings. It takes longer for the BIG critters to warm up than the small ones. It's simple geometry. A lizard that's twice as large in length has four times the surface area to collect sunlight, but eight times the bulk to warm up. (Think of cubes.) I guess that's why cold-blooded lizards don't get too big.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: da29d_da29-014.jpg
Notes: 10565231845 This is another stump-sprouting native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family - in full flower after being burned out by the White Fire last May. Other plants have finished flowering. This should give new life to the insects, but I didn't see any visiting the flowers. I reckon flowers and insects have gotten out of sync. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2013)

File.Name: da29e_da29-013.jpg
Notes: 10565308313 This is looking towards the second crossing of Paradise, on the Santa Ynez River - with storm clouds. The cottonwood and sycamore trees are starting to show some fall color. The tilted landscape is the result of tectonic forces along the Santa Ynez Fault which runs right through here. There are interesting caves in those rocks! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2013)

File.Name: da29f_da29-032.jpg
Notes:
10564884454 This is a heavily galled seed head of native Bicolored Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon crassifolium var. nigrescens) in the Hydrophyllaceae or Boraginaceae plant family. The galls are open at the top. They are not listed in my book on plant galls, and I don't know whose galls they are. I also find them on Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the mountains - see this photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2013)

The aromatic leaves of this fine shrub make healing tea, incense, and a smoke that lives up to its common name. I really like this plant!
CommonNames: Bicolored Yerba Santa; Trask's Yerba Santa
ScientificName: Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Eriodictyon traskiae
associatedTaxa: Eriodictyon crassifolium; nigrescens; Hydrophyllaceae; Boraginaceae; Eriodictyon traskiae

File.Name: da30a_da29-019.jpg
Notes:
10584458153 This is a little pollen-dusted Spotted Cucumber Beetle (Diabrotica undecimpunctata) in the Chrysomelidae family of the order Coleoptera. It's a common garden pest, but it also shows up in the woods. This one is on still fresh looking yellow composite flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2013)

Two photos (from yesterday) are just fine tonight. I'm fried after sitting through a day of jury selection. I'm still waiting to be called into the jury box. The judge thought they will be done with jury selection by noon tomorrow, but I have my doubts. I know, it's a duty of citizenship - but I'd rather be out getting new photos!

File.Name: da30b_da29-011.jpg
Notes: 10584258243 This is the perfect rosette of a native Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plant in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. It seems to do tricks to your eyes if you stare at it, like old op-art. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 29 October 2013)

File.Name: da31a_da23-003.jpg
Notes:
10600930293 This is another large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our house - captured "en silhouette". Happy Halloween! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

This will have to do for scary Halloween photos this year. All spiders are scarce in this drought year, and I've been on Jury Duty for the past two days so I can't get out to look for more. Yep, I am on a Jury, but as an alternate - which means I listen to the case but don't get to vote on the final verdict, unless someone else gets sick. It is an interesting case, and it should be done next week. *Sigh* It's a duty of citizenship. The good news is that there's no court on Friday, so I'm free to get new photos for the next three days, until Monday.

File.Name: da31b_da25-038.jpg
Notes: 10600594295 This is another large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our house. It is pretty spooky when you inadvertently walk through one of their webs at night - Where's the spider! (San Marcos Pass, 25 October 2013)

File.Name: db01a_db01-008.jpg
Notes:
10618062174 Here is another Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia virgulti aka Apodemia mormo virgulti) butterfly working still-flowering yellow composite flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2013)

I'm posting this photo really to just prove that we still have butterflies in November! I also saw Common Buckeyes, Painted Ladies, Common Checkered-skippers, and Northern White-skippers. There are butterflies where there are flowers, but both are getting scarce.

I'm free until Monday, when my jury duty resumes. I'll try to make the best of my time!

This is growing as a parasite on a tree Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family that is not suffering from drought-stress. See my [Previous] photos. Notes: 10618062483 This is dead-looking Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) in the Viscaceae plant family that's growing as a parasite on this Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) tree in the Platanaceae plant family. When the host dies, the parasites follow. I guess this tree is done for. The mistletoe may well be an indicator of trees in stress. I reckon this is caused by drought-stress after this very dry summer. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2013)

Look at that dead-looking Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) in the Viscaceae plant family that grows as a parasite. When the host dies, the parasites follow. It may well be an indicator of trees in stress. I reckon this is caused by drought-stress after this very dry summer. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2013)

That dead-looking Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) in the Viscaceae plant family grows as a parasite, and when the host dies, the parasites follow. It still has some green leaves, but I fear it's fading with that dead-looking Big-leaved Mistletoe (Phoradendron macrophyllum) in the Viscaceae plant family that grows as a parasite. When the host dies, the parasites follow. I reckon it's drought-stress after this very dry summer. See my [Next] few photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 November 2013)

File.Name: db02a_db02-021.jpg
Notes:
10636680665 This is last night's web of the large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our house. Its web is connected to our house on the left and a Coast Live Oak tree about 15 feet away on the right. You have to get the sun and the background just right to photograph these webs, and not over-expose. These spiders very fastidiously roll up their old web every night and make it new again. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2013)

Autumn is the hardest tine of the year to get new photos - after most insects and flowers and before the mushrooms. It's a time when you have to hunt for photos as a naturalist, using everything you know as your guide.

File.Name: db02b_db02-005.jpg
Notes: 10636323046 This is an odd "blister" on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe it's a fungus, and you can maybe see some dark spore-bearing structures on the reticulated surface within. I guess this is how oak leaves get "skeletonized". (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2013)

File.Name: db02c_db02-013.jpg
Notes: 10636181666 These are ripe nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2013)

File.Name: db02d_db02-015.jpg
Notes: 10635954336 This is sucker growth on a tree of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, showing signs of drought-stress after this very dry summer. The foliage above looks better, but the leaves are unusually thin with some dead leaves showing. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2013)

File.Name: db02e_db02-008.jpg
Notes: 10635799025 This is a Coffee Fern (Pellaea andromedifolia) in the Pteridaceae plant family at the end of the dry season. I sure hope it comes back to life when the rains finally come. It looks pretty sad right now. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2013)

File.Name: db02f_db02-018.jpg
Notes: 10635679056 This is a frond of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family, showing the last of its green as we move deeper into autumn. It's an unlikely summer fern in our summer-dry mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 2 November 2013)

File.Name: db03a_db03-027.jpg
Notes:
10660940294 This is a little "parasitic wasp" on the interesting surface of a mass of Crystalline Gall Wasps (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. "Parasitic wasp" is not a taxonomic division, and I have no idea what kind of wasp this is. I have many photos of the galls, start here. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2013)

This wasp is tiny, and this photo is an 800x1066 crop from my original photo. Just the body, without antennae or ovipositor is 500 pixels long in that original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the wasp is 23.4x500/4800 = 2.4 mm, less than 1/10 inch. Like I said, this wasp is tiny!

If you've been as confounded as me about the recent changes in taxonomic names of plants you know and love, don't miss this video - Hitler Rants on Taxonomists!
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: db03b_db03-024.jpg
Notes:
10660403256 Get set, go!

This is a little "parasitic wasp" on the interesting surface of a mass of Crystalline Gall Wasps (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. The photo on the left shows the wasp getting ready, and the photo on the right is the "money shot". Some little Andricus crystallinus wasp larvae just met its destiny. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2013)

"Parasitic wasp" is not a taxonomic division, and I have no idea what kind of wasp this is. I have many photos of the galls, start here. Hairs and bristles and ants provide some protection for the gall larvae, but they remain easy targets for predators that can find them.

They seem to suck the nutrients from the leaves they inhabit. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2013)

File.Name: db03d_db03-014.jpg
Notes:
10659804745 Here is a new "oak apple" gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's two galls growing side by side? It looks squished vertically, but this is how I found it. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2013)

I'm confused by the timing of these galls. The ones on Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) seem done, but these are just beginning. See this photo.

Notes: 10659725953 This is a furry little gall on the three-veined leaves of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This gall is not mentioned in my book "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, UC Press, 2006), so I don't know what it is. We have just two blue-flowered Ceanothus shrubs in our mountains - this one with three-veined leaves and Ceanothus spinosus with a single vein. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2013)

Notes: 10659382743 This is an old "foliose gall" on a stem of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. I believe it's the gall of the Ceanothus Bud Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi) in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera, though this gall doesn't seen to be growing on any sort of flower bud. File.Name: db04a_db04-040.jpg
Notes:
10683093495 This is one wall of the fantastic "Mural Room" aka the "Board of Supervisors Room" at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. The courthouse was dedicated in 1929 after a great earthquake destroyed the old building - and much of Santa Barbara - in 1925. It's considered "Spanish Colonial Revival" style. It's a magnificent building.

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course! There's no longer a court in this room - if there ever was - but the nearby courtrooms are almost as nice, and less distracting. There are murals covering all the walls of this room. (Santa Barbara, 4 November 2013)

Flash is not allowed, so I used my relative fast 50mm f/1.8 lens, and the doors and seats as my tripod. I'll see if I can photograph all the murals over the week my trial is expected to last. I'm pretty fried after a day in court, so these photos are pretty much straight from the camera. And I'll just post two photos a day.

File.Name: db04b_db04-021.jpg
Notes:
10682841186 This is the astounding painted ceiling of the "Mural Room" aka the "Board of Supervisors Room" at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. I believe (from the web) that the ceilings were painted by Giovanni Battista Smeraldi, who was born in Palermo, Italy and immigrated to the United States in 1889. The courthouse itself was dedicated in 1929 after a great earthquake destroyed the old building - and much of Santa Barbara - in 1925. It's considered "Spanish Colonial Revival" style. It's a magnificent building.

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course! There's no longer a court in this room, but the nearby courtrooms are almost as nice, and less distracting. (Santa Barbara, 4 November 2013)

File.Name: db05a_db05-001.jpg
Notes:
10700912293 The halls of justice are usually a placid place - unless a tour group or a new jury is coming by. This is looking down the first floor of the Santa Barbara Courthouse this morning. The courthouse was dedicated in 1929 after a great earthquake destroyed the old building - and much of Santa Barbara - in 1925. It's considered "Spanish Colonial Revival" style. It's a magnificent building. (Santa Barbara, 5 November 2013)

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course! The good news is that the trial may be over tomorrow. I'm just an "alternate juror" ready to step in if someone gets indisposed. If the trial ends tomorrow, I'll be done - until they call me the next time, next year.

File.Name: db05b_db05-002.jpg
Notes:
10700436086 Yesterday I showed the front panel in the fantastic "Mural Room" aka the "Board of Supervisors Room" at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. This is the back panel, where the mural begins. *Sigh* The victors write the history, and this mural starts the history with the building of the Mission of Santa Barbara. Look in the people in the open door at lower right for scale. That lady seems to have her mouth open. It's appropriate.

The courthouse was dedicated in 1929 after a great earthquake destroyed the old building - and much of Santa Barbara - in 1925. It's considered "Spanish Colonial Revival" style. It's a magnificent building.

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course! There's no longer a court in this room - if there ever was - but the nearby courtrooms are almost as nice, and less distracting. Flash is not allowed inside, so I used my relatively fast 50mm f/1.8 lens, and the back of a chair as my tripod. (Santa Barbara, 5 November 2013)

File.Name: db06a_db06-009.jpg
Notes:
10720069126 I suppose since I've been stuck in this building all week on jury duty, I should have at least one photo of the front of the Santa Barbara Courthouse. I'd be nice to have a wider lens than my 50mm, but this is what I got. That red mass below the clock is a huge Bougainvillea plant that's growing two stories tall! (Santa Barbara, 6 November 2013)

I stayed up last tonight to post these courthouse photos because my jury duty is all done! I was an alternate juror, so I don't have to go to deliberation - and I'm glad. Tomorrow, I get to go back to the woods to find what I can find.

File.Name: db06b_db06-022.jpg
Notes:
10719787684 This is the view looking north-west from the view tower of the Santa Barbara Courthouse. I live and take most of my photos near the lowest dip in the mountains on the left. You can also see the Santa Barbara Mission in this photo. To tell the truth, I rarely go to the city of Santa Barbara, except for the occasional music show and beer run - and jury duty. (Santa Barbara, 6 November 2013)

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course, or in this case from the courthouse!

File.Name: db06c_db06-006.jpg
Notes:
10719549805 This is looking up into the painted cupola of a small dome at the Santa Barbara Courthouse. It's easy not to notice as you walk up the steps at the entrance. The design looks quite Moorish, but that's appropriate in this "Spanish Colonial Revival" style building. (Santa Barbara, 6 November 2013)

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course!

File.Name: db06d_db05-032.jpg
Notes:
10719510236 This is part of the wonderful painted ceiling in the courtroom at the Santa Barbara Courthouse during my jury duty this week. The court bailiff didn't like me photographing the courtroom, so this is my best - and only - photo. I spent quite a few minutes staring at this ceiling and wondering what the beasts are along the beam on top. I believe (from the web) that the ceilings were painted by Giovanni Battista Smeraldi, who was born in Palermo, Italy and immigrated to the United States in 1889. (Santa Barbara, 6 November 2013)

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course!

File.Name: db06e_db06-027.jpg
Notes:
10719186715 This is the works of the "Seth Thomas tower clock" in the clock tower of the Santa Barbara Courthouse, surrounded by the wonderful mural about the history of time keeping. It looks like a helicopter! I love these old mechanical clocks, though I don't know quite what I'm looking at. This one has been ticking the time and decades since its installation in the late summer of 1929. See this website for the Bisno Schall Clock Gallery. (Santa Barbara, 6 November 2013)

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course!

File.Name: db06f_db06-030.jpg
Notes:
10718829263 This part of an impressive mural hidden in the clock tower of the Santa Barbara Courthouse, all about time. As I understand, it's a new work, done by Ed Lister in 2010-2012, and is part of the Bisno Schall Clock Gallery. It's a wonderful mural about the obscure subject of horology, the art of making clocks. (Santa Barbara, 6 November 2013)

It's interesting to see Einstein standing by a train with relativity equations floating around in this mural. Einstein used trains to explain relativity, but he also thought deeply about clocks and train schedules when he was a young worker in the Berne Patent Office. Peter L. Galison has written a book about this, Einstein's Clocks, PoincarÈ's Maps (2003). There is an interview with the author here.

What do you do when you're stuck on a jury in court all week? You photograph the courthouse of course!

File.Name: db07a_db07-014.jpg
Notes:
10734981666 I believe this is a female Northern White-Skipper (Heliopetes ericetorum) butterfly working the yellow composite flowers of Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. The host plant for these butterflies is the native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family, which grows nearby. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 November 2013)

The Common Checkered-Skipper (Pyrgus communis or P. albescens) butterfly is very similar, and maybe I've been confusing them. Compare this photo.

It was joy to be out in the woods today getting new photos - after several days of being stuck on a jury in Santa Barbara. Photographing the courthouse is fun for a change, but I'd rather be in the woods!
Locality: Pyrgus communis or P. albescens) butterfly is very similar

Notes: 10734698655 This is a flower head of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family that's going to seed. *Sigh* It's the end of the season for these butterfly-friendly flowers on the dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. There are still a few new flowers, especially on plants that have stump-sprouted after the White Fire last May. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 November 2013)

Notes: 10734656703 This is a galled and/or fasciated flower head of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). The lower flower buds seem too large, and the upper flower buds seem too small! This is on a stump-sprouting plant that was burned by the White Fire last May. File.Name: db07d_db07-018.jpg
Notes: 10734234005 There are still new composite flowers of native California Brickellbush (Brickellia californica) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family growing on rocky banks along the river. It's not a butterfly favorite, but I have seen Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterflies working the flowers recently. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 November 2013)

File.Name: db07e_db07-027.jpg
Notes: 10734355313 The deciduous trees along the Santa Ynez River are starting to show some fall color. The golden trees in the center are Fremont Cottonwood (Populus fremontii) in the Salicaceae plant family, flanked by a couple of browner Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa) trees in the Platanaceae plant family. That's a native Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum) in the Asteraceae plant family that's gone to seed in the lower-left. I got this photo near the 4th river crossing at Paradise. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 7 November 2013)

File.Name: db07f_db04-003.jpg
Notes:
10734120133 I believe this is a dead Hermit Thrush (Catharus guttatus in the bird family Turdidae. I found it's legs up and head buried in this succulent Jade Plant (Crassula ovata, Crassulaceae), and I moved it a bit to show it's face and breast. Maybe it hit the upstairs window and fell into this shrub below? Sorry, little bird. (San Marcos Pass, 4 November 2013)

I got this photo a few days ago when I was just starting jury duty, and didn't want to post it. I'm not sure of the etiquette of posting photos of dead things, but it is a very nice photo of an interesting bird. I've been seeing quite a few of them lately after a long absence. Do they migrate?

File.Name: db08a_db08-021.jpg
Notes:
10751008526 I'm pretty sure this is a Dragon Lubber Grasshopper - Dracotettix monstrosus in the subfamily Romaleinae of the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera. I love the Latin name! I found this lovely critter today on the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)

I went back to the river today to avoid the crowd over the upcoming holiday weekend. It was another beautiful day in paradise. We're still waiting for real rain.

File.Name: db08b_db08-035.jpg
Notes: 10750818875 This is a rather large Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) trying to work a spent flower of native Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae plant family. This flower is too old, and the other is too new. It's tough being a bumblebee! It was a challenge to get this photo - with the wind and the heavy bee pulling down the flowers. I took *many* photos to get this keeper. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)

File.Name: db08c_db08-033.jpg
Notes: 10750761736 Here are new flowers of native Palmer's Goldenbush (Ericameria palmeri var. pachylepis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a leafy shrub that grows 4-8 feet tall with yellow composite flowers that have small ray-flowers. It's quite pretty and rather uncommon on the dry washes by the river. It's one of those plants that keeps the insects going in autumn. See my [Next] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)

File.Name: db08d_db08-006.jpg
Notes: 10750548315 This is a beautiful new flower of native Dunn's Lobelia (Lobelia dunnii var. serrata) in the Campanulaceae plant family. It's an unusual plant in our mountains because it favors perennial creeks that flow in our dry summer. It don't know what it's doing here on the dry river bed, but here it is. Blue is an unusual flower color in November! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)

File.Name: db08e_db08-011.jpg
Notes: 10750349924 Here are late-season composite flowers of native Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash along the river. I hardly recognize it, because it's growing from a stump-sprouting plant after the White Fire last May. Usually the flowers are on taller stalks and the bottom leaves are already shriveled by the time the plant flowers. This photo and this photo are more typical. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)

File.Name: db08f_db08-030.jpg
Notes: 10750252286 These are still new yellow composite flowers of native Southern Goldenrod (Solidago confinis) in the Asteraceae plant family growing along the river. It's a slender plant with leafy stems and small ray flowers that favors marshy places. It doesn't look anything like a classic goldenrod. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)

File.Name: db09a_db09-001.jpg
Notes:
10768726064 Here are new seeds of California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family getting ready to blow away in the breeze this morning. It's only when they get these dandelion-like seeds that these plants look like genus Epilobium. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2013)

It was breezy today, probably "fog winds" caused by the turbulent air at the upper side of the temperature inversion that forms above the marine layer gloom. We never had the gloom today, but we were close.
ScientificName: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Epilobium
associatedTaxa: Epilobium canum; Zauschneria californica; Onagraceae; Epilobium

File.Name: db09b_db09-009.jpg
Notes: 10768595546 Here are leaves of native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family showing some fall colors. Note that many trees are skipping the fall color phase and just turning brown in this drought year. I've seen this on native sycamores and cottonwoods too. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2013)

File.Name: db09c_db09-002.jpg
Notes:
10768322666 I found this oddball new violet flower on a dead-looking plant of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale) in the Asteraceae plant family this morning. I don't know what it's doing flowering in November on a plant that looks long dead, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2013)

Yes Sal, I haven't forgotten. I'll get seeds in the mail this week.

File.Name: db09d_db09-005.jpg
Notes: 10768153595 This is a single stalk of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family, catching a beam of light in the woods this morning. It's growing in a tangle of (mostly) native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. These woodland plants look very different from the large clumps I see along the mountain roads, almost a different species. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2013)
ScientificName: Leymus condensatus; Poaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae
associatedTaxa: Leymus condensatus; Poaceae; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae

File.Name: db09e_db09-003.jpg
Notes: 10767883545 This is just a fly (order Diptera) - but it is different and rather pretty with those orange antennae and bristly orange bottom. I believe it's a Scavenger Fly aka Flesh Fly in the family Sarcophagidae. This one is perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It was breezy today, and this was as sharp as I could get in the few seconds I had for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 9 November 2013)
CommonNames: fly; Scavenger Fly; Flesh Fly; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: db09f_db08-018.jpg
Notes: 10767726815 This is another photo of the same remarkable Dragon Lubber Grasshopper that I showed in my [Previous] photo. It's Dracotettix monstrosus in the subfamily Romaleinae of the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera. It's well named, both common and Latin! I found this lovely critter yesterday on the dry wash of the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)

File.Name: db10a_db10-024.jpg
Notes:
10790155646 This is a beautiful golden Syrphid Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) - more of a "hover fly" than a "flower fly". These flies hover and perch on the leaves of this native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family, and chase away all other insects that come close. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2013)

I know the focus isn't perfect. These little flies are very aware of intruders, and it was breezy again today. I was happy to get this photo as sharp as it is.

File.Name: db10b_db10-002.jpg
Notes:
10790005544 This is a tiny net-winged Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera. I believe it is an Issid Planthopper in the genus Neaethus of the family Issidae. It's perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2013)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, and the hopper is 510 pixels long in the original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the hopper is 23.4x510/4800 = 2.5 mm, less than 1/10 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!
CommonNames: Planthopper; Issid Planthopper; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Neaethus; Issidae; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: db10c_db10-006.jpg
Notes:
10789368705 I believe this is the terminal bud gall of a Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on the new bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe it is the unisexual gall of a critter that also has sexual galls in the spring. Nature is complicated like that! (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2013)

I had to double-check the timing of these interesting galls - and sure enough, I've always found them before in November according to my records. They are not common, and I've only noticed them 3 times - photos here, here, and here, all at Fotolog where I used to post before I came to Flickr.
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: db10d_db10-016.jpg
Notes: 10788999205 This is native Autumn Willowherb (Epilobium brachycarpum) in the Onagraceae plant family going to seed in the canyon. The flowers are tiny, but you can see a family (or genus) resemblance to California Fuschia (Epilobium canum). These are both dry-land Epilobiums, as opposed to the usual plants that like water. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2013)

File.Name: db10e_db10-008.jpg
Notes: 10788826036 I believe this is the native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family, which does very well in our canyon. It looks a lot like native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens, Poaceae), but it doesn't have jointed stems and the flowers are completely different. This amazingly tough grass-like plant grows both in woodland and chaparral. (San Marcos Pass, 10 November 2013)
ScientificName: Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae
associatedTaxa: Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Agrostis pallens; Poaceae

File.Name: db10f_db08-013.jpg
Notes: 10788846723 This is a whole flowering plant of native Hairy Golden Aster (Heterotheca sessiliflora ssp. echoides) in the Asteraceae plant family on the dry wash along the river. Compare this photo which looks quite different. Hairy Golden Aster was formerly named Chrysopsis villosa. This one is stump-sprouting after the White Fire last May. Usually the flowers are on taller stalks that are almost leafless by now. I'm posting this now so that a friend can see the whole plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 8 November 2013)
ScientificName: Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Asteraceae; Chrysopsis villosa
associatedTaxa: Heterotheca sessiliflora; echoides; Asteraceae; Chrysopsis villosa

File.Name: db11a_db11-008.jpg
Notes:
10810601715 This is a pollen-dusted Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) digging into a new flower of late-season native Fort Tejon Milk-Aster aka Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in the Asteraceae plant family in the woods. The long pistils of this flower appear to be tied into a knot! Pollen abounds, but this bee seems interested in the nectar at the base of the flowers. The Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) flowers in my [Previous] photo look similar, but they are smaller and have fewer petals. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 November 2013)

*Sigh* Today was another beautiful day in paradise, though there were some high clouds and wind. There's no rain in the forecast. Maybe this extended dry spell in autumn is a good thing. We've had early rains for the last two drought years, so maybe this late dry spell is a good sign? Yep, I'm grasping for straws.
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Fort Tejon Milk-Aster; Chicory-leaved Stephanomeria; Rod Wirelettuce
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Stephanomeria cichoriacea; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa

File.Name: db11b_db11-022.jpg
Notes: 10810339856 These are pretty new flowers of Rod Wirelettuce (Stephanomeria virgata ssp. pleurocarpa) in the Asteraceae plant family. These flowers are unusually violet. The plant looks kind of weedy from a distance, but it is a native that deserves some respect for flowering in autumn when little else can. You can see a family resemblance with the Fort Tejon Milk-Aster (Stephanomeria cichoriacea) in my [Next] photo. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 November 2013)
ScientificName: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea
associatedTaxa: Stephanomeria virgata; pleurocarpa; Asteraceae; Stephanomeria cichoriacea

File.Name: db11c_db03-008.jpg
Notes: 10810426213 This is a wonderful seed head of Douglas' Groundsel (Senecio flaccidus var. douglasii aka S. douglasii) in the Asteraceae plant family. The long skinny seeds are remarkable. I took this photo more than a week ago, but it's one of those photos I don't want to disappear into the archives. (San Marcos Pass, 3 November 2013)
ScientificName: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Senecio flaccidus; douglasii; S. douglasii; Asteraceae

File.Name: db11d_db11-024.jpg
Notes: 10810059284 These are new cones, dripping with resin, on a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family. It's a three-needle pine. The huge cones can weigh as much as 10 pounds, according to Wikipedia. I don't recommend camping under one of these trees! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 11 November 2013)

File.Name: db11e_db11-028.jpg
Notes: 10810080703 This is the heart-shaped seed of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. The seed is still in the pod. It's one of those plants that is "rarely common" in our mountains. See my [Previous] photo, and follow the links. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2013)

File.Name: db11f_db11-030.jpg
Notes:
10809931403 These are summer-dry stalks of native Soap Plant or Amole (Chlorogalum pomeridianum) in the Liliaceae plant family, now moved to the Agavaceae. They stand taller than I am! I have a photo of the same plants in flower last June here, and a closer photo of the flowers here. It's one of those plants that is "rarely common" in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 11 November 2013)

The plants were useful for the Chumash people for food, soap, fiber, glue, and ... fish poison! The bulbs were crushed and stirred into pools on a creak, and the fish floated to the surface. Apparently the soapy saponins in the root messed with the fish's gills and didn't affect the edibility. I've always wanted to test that trick.

File.Name: db12a_db12-039.jpg
Notes:
10830007136 This is the historic bar at Cold Springs Tavern on the pass, with beautiful native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family showing some fall colors above. There's a huge light-skinned Western Sycamore (Platanus racemosa, Platanaceae) here too, but the foliage is all coming from the dark-skinned maple to the left of it, and through the roof! (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2013)

This mountain tavern is a rockin' place on weekends, especially on Sundays when Tom Ball and Kenny Sultan have the stage. See this video on YouTube. Many people, many motorcycles... I avoid this place on the weekends.

I got my start teaching at Cold Springs Tavern! Forty years ago, we used to live in a rustic cabin a few miles up the road. I was working on a PhD thesis in philosophy at UCSB, and I would occasionally walk down to the tavern to have a few, and stumble home. One night I met a bunch of friendly folks with odd names like Bullroar, Snakeman, and Chemise. They all worked at a place called the Outdoor School near Lake Cachuma, taking 6th graders on nature walks. They needed a substitute for the next week, and I agreed. On my way to the campfire circle, I picked the nickname "Treebeard" which I had ever since. I led hikes at the Outdoor School for 10 years, and finally left to Dunn Middle School where I taught science for another (almost) 30 years.

Like I said, it's a historic place!
ScientificName: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae
associatedTaxa: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Platanus racemosa; Platanaceae

File.Name: db12b_db12-045.jpg
Notes: 10829852933 This is the creek that flows by Cold Springs Tavern on the Pass. Flowing creeks are rare in this drought year. Maybe that's the reason why this creek has California Newt (Taricha torosa) salamanders. See this old photo (from Fotolog) - and old photo because my beard is still brown! I believe this is the only creek on the north-facing slopes that has these salamanders. Also see this photo from a wetter year. There are a few other creeks that still have water in them, but I won't say where. (San Marcos Pass, 12 November 2013)

File.Name: db12c_db12-019.jpg
Notes: 10829596054 There are still new flowers and buds on native Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family growing along the river. This is a real workhorse of a shrub that just keeps producing new flowers. They are beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2013)

File.Name: db12d_db12-020.jpg
Notes:
10829325786 Here is another Behr's Metalmark (Apodemia virgulti aka Apodemia mormo virgulti) butterfly working still-flowering yellow composite flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2013)

I'm really posting this just to prove we still have butterflies in the woods. One of these photos will be the last butterfly of the year, probably when the last flower goes to seed.
CommonNames: Behr's Metalmark; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma
ScientificName: Apodemia virgulti; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia
associatedTaxa: Apodemia virgulti; Apodemia mormo virgulti; Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia

File.Name: db12e_db12-018.jpg
Notes: 10829223226 Here is another photo of flower heads of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family going to seed. *Sigh* It's the end of the season for these butterfly-friendly flowers on the dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. There are still a few new flowers, especially on plants that have stump-sprouted after the White Fire last May. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2013)
ScientificName: Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia
associatedTaxa: Isocoma; Asteraceae; Isocoma menziesii; Isocoma acradenia

File.Name: db12f_db12-002.jpg
Notes: 10829322373 This sure looks like a stump-sprouting Carmel Valley Malacothrix (Malacothrix saxatilis var. arachnoidea) in the Asteraceae plant family, but I'm not sure what it's doing here in Santa Barbara County. It looks like the common Malacothrix saxatilis var. tenuifolia except that the leaves are white-tomentose. This one was burned by the White Fire last May. I have photos of the the flowers here and here. It's an unusual native plant. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 12 November 2013)
ScientificName: Malacothrix saxatilis; arachnoidea; Asteraceae; Malacothrix saxatilis; tenuifolia

File.Name: db13a_db13-014.jpg
Notes:
10846720996 This photo shows the brilliant geometry of feathery seed plumes of native Mountain Mahogany (Cercocarpus betuloides) in the Rosaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2013)

*Sigh* Today was another beautiful day in paradise. It was warm, almost 80 on the porch when I got home today, and hardly a breath of wind all morning. I'm tired of these perfect days!

File.Name: db13b_db13-023.jpg
Notes: 10846803223 This is looking into a seed head of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family. It looks so much like the receptacle of a composite flower, but it's a different family. Last summer's seeds are going free. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2013)

File.Name: db13c_db13-010.jpg
Notes: 10846361635 I first thought that these are odd nests or pupal cases on leaves of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. But nope, they are galls of the Intermediate Oak Disc Gall Wasp (Paracraspis guadaloupensis), another gall-making galls of Cynipid Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae of the Hymenoptera). Once I noticed them, I found quite a few more. I guess those are exit holes where the critter inside got out. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2013)
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Intermediate Oak Disc Gall Wasp; Cynipid Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Paracraspis guadaloupensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae; Paracraspis guadaloupensis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: db13d_db13-027.jpg
Notes: 10846386644 I guess this is the pupal case of some unfortunate insect that has been parasitized, with the exit hole of the parasite in plain view. Or is that an exit hole? I found this on a three-veined leaf of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's not a gall. I tried to brush it off and it came away clean, like it was glued onto the leaf. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2013)

File.Name: db13e_db13-004.jpg
Notes: 10846398533 This is a whole line of furry little galls along the leftmost vein of a three-veined leaf of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. These galls are not mentioned in my book "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, UC Press, 2006), so I don't know what they are. They appear to have "hatched". (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2013)
CommonNames: Hairy Ceanothus; Field Guide to Plant Galls

File.Name: db13f_db13-005.jpg
Notes: 10846270763 Here are a pair of furry little galls on a three-veined leaf of Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. This gall is not mentioned in my book "Field Guide to Plant Galls" (Ron Russo, UC Press, 2006), so I don't know what it is. Note how the galls occur on the main veins of the leaf, and compare my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2013)
CommonNames: Hairy Ceanothus; Field Guide to Plant Galls

File.Name: db14a_db14-007.jpg
Notes:
10863299993 This is a different Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera perched for a moment on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I first thought it was a Leafhopper (family Cicadellidae) because of its shape, but the antennae are below the eyes. It hopped away before I could get better focus. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2013)

The wind picked up last night after I finished posting. It was an an unusually dry and warm tropical wind that seemed to come from the south, where the cold ocean is. Do upslope winds heat by compression like downslope winds? We're still under a wind advisory, but it's not happening tonight, yet.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cicadellidae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Cicadellidae

File.Name: db14b_db14-024.jpg
Notes:
10863040823 Here are two photos of an odd "thing" I found on a branch of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family yesterday. At first I thought it might be a seed "glued" to the stem. If you look at the left photo, it sure seems to have a "seed germ" stuck to the bottom. But if you look at the photo on the right, you can clearly see that it's a gall that's growing from the branch. The "seed germ" is just a bud of the shrub that's been nudged over. The gall doesn't easily rub off like a seed would. Maybe it's the dried out gall of the the "Dried Peach Gall Wasp" (Disholcaspis simulata) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera)? (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2013)

I found this gall yesterday, but I didn't like my photo. I went back to the same place today for a re-shoot.
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Dried Peach Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis simulata; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: db14c_db14-020.jpg
Notes: 10862650286 This is a different terminal bud gall of the Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on the new bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These galls look so perfect, like celestial orbs. See this photo for more info. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2013)
CommonNames: Live Oak Bud Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercusagrifoliae; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
