File.Name: db14d_db14-025.jpg
Notes: 10862681354 These are the last real flowers on the California Buckwheat (Eriogonum fasciculatum) in the Polygonaceae plant family that grows by our mailbox. The flowers fade into a rich chocolate-brown at this time of year. I hate to see them go. This humble shrub supports much life throughout our dry summer. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2013)

File.Name: db14e_db14-005.jpg
Notes:
10862483084 This is an old flower head - now a seed head, though you hardly notice the difference - of native Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek. There's another plant in the shade behind. It's a nice native plant, and we have a few patches in our garden at home. (San Marcos Pass, 14 November 2013)

It's very easy to over-expose photos of this plant. The exposure on this photo is 2 2/3 stops less than my camera thought was right in center-weighted mode.

File.Name: db14f_db13-022.jpg
Notes: 10862245926 This is a mass of dusty-looking seed heads of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha) in the Ranunculaceae plant family. They're not really dusty, that's just how they look. See my close shot two photos [Back]. They are growing over a scrubby Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's a different scrub oak with mostly smooth-edged leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 13 November 2013)
CommonNames: Chaparral Clematis; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak
ScientificName: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Clematis lasiantha; Ranunculaceae; Quercus chrysolepis; Fagaceae

File.Name: db15a_db15-011.jpg
Notes:
10878852493 These are multiple trunks coming from an ancient burl of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. The red peeling bark is manzanita-like, very beautiful. The trunks in the foreground have real bark, which is unusual in our mountains. The burl is huge. I'll bet this tree has been through a lot. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2013)

The fog monster visited last night - it was gloomy, breezy, and cold all morning. The clouds finally cleared out, but it stayed breezy and cool. The clouds have already moved back in tonight.

File.Name: db15b_db15-009.jpg
Notes: 10878454645 Here are ripening berries of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. The berries are edible, though a bit mealy. The trees really belong further north, but here the are in our mountains. They're having a tough time in this drought year, and only a few trees have berries. The trees are very beautiful. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2013)

File.Name: db15c_db15-032.jpg
Notes: 10878682543 Here are almost ripe berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The plants are also called Christmas Berries. The ripe berries are edible, but astringent. If you make an infusion in boiling water and add a bit of honey, they make a delicious drink. They are having a surprisingly good year, despite our drought. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2013)

File.Name: db15d_db15-018.jpg
Notes: 10878515973 These are pretty new autumn flowers of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. Any native plant that can flower at this time of year - in our summer-dry climate, in a drought year - has my respect! (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2013)

File.Name: db15e_db15-020.jpg
Notes: 10878206874 These are ripe berries on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. Actually, these berries are past ripe, into the mealy stage. They are edible, and make a decent tea when mashed with hot water. The "ssp. mollis" refers to the long white hairs on the petioles, barely visible here. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2013)

File.Name: db15f_db15-023.jpg
Notes: 10877911946 This is what's left of the once pretty leaf galls on a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. Compare this photo. The galls are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera. They don't seem to do the leaves any good. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2013)
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: db16a_db16-020.jpg
Notes:
10895329165 Here is a line of Cattails (family Typhaceae) advancing into still water, with reflections. I don't really know aquatic plants very well, but maybe these are the Narrowleaf Cattail (Typha angustifolia)? This is a place on the Santa Ynez River that still has water. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 November 2013)

I went back to the river today for the umpteenth time... Well, that's what I do. I hike the same trails week after week looking for something new, and I usually find it!

File.Name: db16b_db16-015.jpg
Notes: 10895354254 I was happy to see this green fuzz on branches of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family that was burned by the White Fire last May. This tree looked quite dead, but it has new life! It's not stump-sprouting, so I guess it's limb-sprouting. Life does come back! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 November 2013)

File.Name: db16c_db16-025.jpg
Notes:
10894890045 Well blow me down... (as Popeye used to say)

This is a flowering native Narrowleaf Bedstraw (Galium angustifolium) in the Rubiaceae plant family that seems to be stump-sprouting in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. I'm not sure if these flowers are staminate or pistillate on this dioecious plant. The plants usually flower in late spring or early summer, so this was a surprise. Wildfires do mess with the wild plants' flowering schedule. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 November 2013)

We have many different species of Galium in our mountains, and they are a favorite of mine, though the flowers are quite plain and hard to tell apart. Most plants have leaves in whorls of four and four-petaled flowers like this one.

File.Name: db16d_db16-009.jpg
Notes: 10894733106 This is the stump-sprout of a Chaparral Mallow (Malacothamnus fasciculatus) shrub in the Malvaceae plant family in a place that was burned by the White Fire last May. I'm glad to see it. There used to be a great shrub here that was 10 or 12 feet high, and the fire took it out. There was only one of these shrubs in this place, but maybe it will come back. See this photo for the pretty flowers. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 November 2013)

File.Name: db16e_db16-030.jpg
Notes: 10894849673 I believe this is new foliage of the Santa Barbara Milkvetch (Astragalus trichopodus var. phoxus) in the Fabaceae plant family, with those long pinnate leaves. It looks tropical. This plant used to be known as a locoweed, but there's nothing about it that will get you high or crazy. This one is "stump-sprouting" after being lightly toasted by the White Fire last May, on the edge of the wildfire. The foliage is beautiful. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 November 2013)

File.Name: db16f_db16-011.jpg
Notes: 10894452466 These are toasted Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plants in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. They were burned by the White Fire which passed through this place last May. Some will make it, and some won't. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 November 2013)
ScientificName: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae
associatedTaxa: Yucca whipplei; Liliaceae; Hesperoyucca whipplei; Agavaceae

File.Name: db17a_db17-005.jpg
Notes:
10916996235 This is a little spider wrapping its prey, which is maybe a tiny Leafhopper or Planthopper insect. (See this photo.) I guess from its web and shape and eyes that it's a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2013)

This page on Spider Eye Arrangements from BugGuide is really useful. I keep it bookmarked.

It was cloudy and gloomy at home, but a few miles down the road towards the valley it was clear. We're heading up to Oregon for our traditional Thanksgiving holiday at the end of the week, so expect me to become increasingly distracted as the week goes on.

File.Name: db17b_db17-012.jpg
Notes: 10917010433 Here is a last flower (left) and new seeds (right) of native composite Sawtooth Goldenbush (Hazardia squarrosa) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. It's one of our late-season composite shrubs that keeps the insects going at the end of their time before winter sets in. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2013)

File.Name: db17c_db17-016.jpg
Notes:
10916353125 I believe this is the "fruticose" Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) growing on a dead twig of a different Scrub Oak - Quercus wislizenii var. frutescens in the Fagaceae plant family. I reckon the "gold eyes" are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. They can't reproduce the lichen - only the fungus. They are more common in the Santa Ynez Valley where they get more fog drip. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2013)

I really want to photograph mushrooms right now - it's that time of year. If the rain won't come to us, we'll go to the rain! We're heading up to Oregon for our traditional Thanksgiving holiday at the end of the week.
ScientificName: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; apothecia
associatedTaxa: Teloschistes chrysophthalmus; Quercus wislizenii; frutescens; Fagaceae; apothecia

File.Name: db17d_db17-019.jpg
Notes: 10916531443 Here is a mass of galls of the Crystalline Gall Wasp (Andricus crystallinus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera growing on a leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. These have surprisingly rich color for this time of year. You can see the the mass is actually made up of individual gall capsules. They are beautiful and strange. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2013)
ScientificName: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus crystallinus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: db17e_db17-010.jpg
Notes: 10915902916 I'm not sure whose hairy galls these are on the underside midrib of this leaf of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family, but I have no doubt they are the galls of Cynipid Gall Wasps (Cynipidae, Hymenoptera). Quite beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2013)

File.Name: db17f_db17-008.jpg
Notes:
10915727754 I believe this is a mature gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't see any exit hole, so I reckon the wasp larvae is still in there. These gall are very beautiful when fresh, with yellow spots instead of black. See this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2013)

It seems odd to think of galls as having seasons and good or bad years, but this has not been a good year for these galls and their wasps. I've only seen a few.
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: db18a_db18-014.jpg
Notes:
10936379006 This butterfly had me guessing for a while, but I'm sure it's just another Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus). It's tattered and threadbare and missing most of its tails, and it's unusual because it's showing its upper wings, with some blue-green colors. See my [Previous] photo. This one is perched on late flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, but what species?) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 November 2013)

It was cloudy and gloomy at home this morning, so I went back to the river - where it was still cloudy, but less gloomy. It sure feels like rain, but the NWS Area Forecast Discussion says not much if any. *Sigh* Still waiting.

File.Name: db18b_db18-009.jpg
Notes: 10936207745 This butterfly had me guessing for a while, but I'm sure it's just another Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus). It's tattered and threadbare and missing most of its tails. Compare this photo (from Oregon) to appreciate just how tattered this butterfly is! This one is perched on late flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, but what species?) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 November 2013)

File.Name: db18c_db18-004.jpg
Notes: 10936288494 This is a lovely Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on late flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 November 2013)
CommonNames: Painted Lady; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma

File.Name: db18d_db18-006.jpg
Notes: 10936112306 This is a handsome Wild Bee (Anthophila aka Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on still-flowering golden flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. I'm still not sure if the plant is the Coastal Goldenbush (Isocoma menziesii) or the Desert Isocoma (Isocoma acradenia). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 November 2013)
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Goldenbush; Coastal Goldenbush; Desert Isocoma

File.Name: db18e_db18-021.jpg
Notes: 10936018914 Here are new "flowers" on Coastal Sagebrush (Artemisia californica) in the Asteraceae plant family. Look close at the stalk in the center to see them. It seems kind of late in the year for these shrubs to be in full flower, but here they are. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 November 2013)

File.Name: db18f_db17-025.jpg
Notes:
10935469025 I found these ripe berries of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family in the woods yesterday. The berries are large and only contain a single large nut, unlike the Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) which contains multiple nutlets. (See this photo.) Their secret is that the skin of these large seeds is delicious if you peel it off and chew it slowly. They have a lemony flavor that is very nice. (San Marcos Pass, 17 November 2013)

The shrubs have few berries in this drought year. What's more disturbing is that they have no flower buds. This is a plant that usually forms buds early in order to flower early in December or January. This might be a hard year for the hummingbirds that seem to depend on the flowers in the winter.

File.Name: db19a_db19-001.jpg
Notes:
10954951206 This is another female Southern Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus - maybe O. h. fuliginatus?) that I found today near a "wet place" in the mountains - maybe a spring, or more likely a leaking water tank somewhere up the hill. But look close on the deer's shoulder, just screen-left of its neck - there is a Western Scrub-Jay (Aphelocoma californica). I've never seen this behavior before. It sure looks like the bird is picking parasites from the deer, like oxpecker birds or helper fish. Sorry I can't make the photo any clearer - it's a tight crop from a distance. This place looks unnaturally green for this time of year. Maybe it's a spring, or more likely a leaking water tank somewhere up the hill. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 November 2013)

Today started gloomy, then cleared up a bit, then got gloomy again. There's now a 30% chance of a bit of rain on tonight and tomorrow. This morning, it was a 50% chance. I'll be happy if we get anything.

Our DSL internet connection has been down today, but the phone service was fine. I reset the router to no effect. I finally checked the router from my computer, and sure enough the router had reset its password and wireless settings. Live and learn, and try to remember the lessons until the next time!
ScientificName: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Aphelocoma californica
associatedTaxa: Odocoileus hemionus; O. h. fuliginatus; Aphelocoma californica

File.Name: db19b_db19-013.jpg
Notes: 10954812196 These are odd insect galls on a leaf of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I believe these galls are made by tiny Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) flies in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. The non-detachable galls go completely through the leaf with the upper surface shaped like a volcano. I don't notice these galls very often, even when I look for them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 November 2013)
CommonNames: White Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera

File.Name: db19c_db19-015.jpg
Notes:
10954773924 This is a large cluster of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family growing near a "wet place" in the mountains - maybe a spring, or more likely a leaking water tank somewhere up the hill. The leaves have a strong camphor smell. They make good smudge sticks and have a reputation for quieting the dead - a claim I can't argue with. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 November 2013)

The seedlings look completely different. See my [Previous] photo.

File.Name: db19d_db16-002.jpg
Notes:
10954604266 These are tiny seedlings of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family, growing in a large pot in the garden. The secret to starting the seedlings seems to be to use fresh seed from this year. See my [Next] photo for the mature plant. It's interesting that the seedlings look nothing like them! (San Marcos Pass, 16 November 2013)

*Gulp* I had to use flash for this photo in the shade. I tried to get a photo with natural light, but it didn't take.

File.Name: db19e_db19-007.jpg
Notes: 10954701743 This is the "microverse" of tiny lichens growing on a dead twig of native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. That's a deciduous oak, and the lichens seem to favor them . I don't know what they all are, but I believe that's a Gold Eye Lichen (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) growing on the left. See my [Previous] photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 November 2013)
CommonNames: lichens; Blue Oak; Gold Eye Lichen

File.Name: db19f_db19-009.jpg
Notes: 10954633713 This is a pretty group of "fruticose" Gold Eye Lichens (Teloschistes chrysophthalmus or kin) growing on a dead twig of native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. This is a deciduous oak, and the lichens seem to favor them. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 November 2013)

File.Name: db20a_db20-024.jpg
Notes:
10971684403 This is a little caterpillar posed on the sleeve of Julie's blue jacket this afternoon. I hit the books, and I believe it's the caterpillar of a California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly. See this photo of the adult. It's remarkable that the butterflies are so common all summer long, and I think this is the first caterpillar I've seen. The caterpillars feed on oak leaves, and there are lots of those in the woods. This photo looks downright psychedelic with the blue background of Julie's jacket. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)

We only had 1/100 of an inch of rain last night, but we've had 1/3 inch this evening so far. This is our biggest rain event of the year so far!

File.Name: db20b_db20-rainfall chart.jpg
Notes:
10971212966 I think many locals are wondering whether less rain in the fall means more rain overall. It's a nice thought in this dry autumn of a drought year.

I collected data about San Marcos Pass rainfall from the Santa Barbara County Hydrology website. I got the data into Excel, and did quick and dirty spreadsheet. Then I collected the data for Sep-Nov rainfall vs. total rainfall. I'm sorry to say that there is no correlation at all. The graph looks like a shotgun blast. Some years with no early rain have been wet, and some have been dry. I added a trendline, but it doesn't mean anything.

This weather year will be what it is, wet or dry. Be that as it may, there's always something interesting happening in the woods. I just hope it's mushrooms and wildflowers and not oak trees dying. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)

File.Name: db20c_db20-011.jpg
Notes: 10971103486 Here are a scant few raindrops on leaves of Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. This is an uncommon plant in our canyon, though it is more common in the hills above. The leaves have a silky-white texture that my photo doesn't capture. This one is growing in the creek bottom. It won't last if we get real rain this year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)

File.Name: db20d_db20-013.jpg
Notes: 10970993904 I believe this is a gall of the Willow Apple Gall Sawfly (Potania californica) in the family Tenthredinidae of the order Hymenoptera, on a narrow leaf of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family. Sometimes a gloomy day bring out the colors. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)
CommonNames: Willow Apple Gall Sawfly; Arroyo Willow
ScientificName: Potania californica; Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae
associatedTaxa: Potania californica; Tenthredinidae; Hymenoptera; Salix lasiolepis; Salicaceae

File.Name: db20e_db20-016.jpg
Notes: 10970790316 This is a fresh gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. I find these galls in both red and green forms, but this one is both at once! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: db20f_db20-001.jpg
Notes: 10970590575 This is fall foliage of a large deciduous native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family, showing some fall color in the woods on this gloomy day. Our woods are mostly evergreen in the mountains, but there are a few plants which give us a taste of fall. This is a remarkable shrub that has wonderful spring flowers. See this photo for more info. That's a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family in the background. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Arbutus menziesii; Ericaceae

File.Name: db21a_db21-004.jpg
Notes:
10987342703 Here are aging fronds of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the Polypodiaceae plant family, showing the color gradation as we as we move deeper into autumn. It's an unlikely summer fern in our summer-dry mountains. Sometimes it takes a drizzly day to bring out the colors! (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2013)

We had an honest inch of rain in our rain gauge this morning! This is most rain we've had since last March. It was cold and gloomy all day. I've been busy running errands getting ready for our Thanksgiving trip to Oregon, the land of lichens and mushrooms. We hope to leave on Saturday morning, but we'll see if it happens. I did look casually for mushrooms today, but it's still too soon. I'll look again tomorrow.

File.Name: db21b_db21-009.jpg
Notes: 10987072744 This is a universe of crustose and foliose lichens growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I really don't know our lichens at all, but maybe this is the year I try to learn them. How quickly the lichens appear after a rain! We had an honest inch of rain in our rain gauge this morning. This is most rain we've had since last March. The woods are coming alive again. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2013)

File.Name: db21c_db20-007.jpg
Notes: 10986802456 We can't be too picky about what counts as fall color in our mountains! These are deciduous leaves of Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis or kin) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family, and they do have some color. They are growing in a thicket of native wild blackberry and honeysuckle - the understory of our woods. Good luck walking through there! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)

File.Name: db21d_db18-033.jpg
Notes: 10986536105 It's always a surprise to find native grasses in an otherwise weedy field! I believe this is Nodding Needle Grass (Nasella cernua or Stipa cernua) in the Poacea plant family, with twisted awns. At least I've found that one nearby. See this photo, and the one before it. Perhaps the native grasses are more common than we think? (San Marcos Pass, 18 November 2013)

File.Name: db21e_db20-018.jpg
Notes: 10986593514 I believe this is the integral stem gall of a Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth (Periploca ceanothiella or kin) in the family Cosmopterigidae of the order Lepidoptera. They cause these swollen stems on Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. See my [Previous] photo for another moth gall. I wonder how the tiny moths chew their way out of the galls - they have the wrong kind of mouthparts! (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)
CommonNames: Ceanothus Stem Gall Moth; Greenbark Ceanothus
ScientificName: Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Periploca ceanothiella; Cosmopterigidae; Lepidoptera; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: db21f_db20-017.jpg
Notes:
10986313695 I believe this is the integral stem gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae (Lepidoptera). The moth causes an abrupt swelling on the stems of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The moth must be tiny to fit through that exit hole. I don't know anything else about it. (San Marcos Pass, 20 November 2013)

Now that I think about it, just how does a tiny moth chew its way out of that gall? They have the wrong kind of mouthparts!
CommonNames: Baccharis Stem Gall Moth; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: db22a_db22-004.jpg
Notes:
11002515514 These are our first mushrooms of the new rain year! Well, maybe I'm cheating a bit since they are in the garden which we've been watering, but it's interesting that they picked today to fruit - the day after our first real rain of the year. There's *something* about real rain. I'm not sure what this mushroom is, but I guess it's some kind of Inocybe. It has a silky cap and gills with whitish edges from cystidia. This one had no particular smell. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2013)

We're heading up to Oregon tomorrow morning on our annual Thanksgiving trip to share time and dinner with our kids and grandkids - just when things are starting to get interesting in the woods at home. Oh well, Oregon is the land of lichens and mushrooms. I'll be back in a couple of weeks, hopefully with many photos.

File.Name: db22b_db22-006.jpg
Notes:
11002382893 Just add water...

These are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants greening up after our first real rain of the season a day ago. They are growing on a rocky sandstone bank near the creek in our canyon. They turn completely black in dry weather - which has been since March this year. Compare this photo and this one. They are real "resurrection plants". That's a white lichen, not bird poop. (San Marcos Pass, 22 November 2013)

File.Name: db22c_db21-006.jpg
Notes: 11002238853 Here's another photo of foliose lichens growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I really don't know our lichens at all, but maybe this is the year I try to learn them. How quickly the lichens appear after a rain! We had an honest inch of rain in our rain gauge yesterday morning. This is most rain we've had since last March. The woods are coming alive again. (San Marcos Pass, 21 November 2013)

File.Name: db22d_db19-006.jpg
Notes:
11001815626 I was trying to get a photo of these odd insect galls on a leaf of native White Sage (Salvia apiana) in the Lamiaceae plant family, and I didn't realize until today that I might also have a photo of the emerging gall-producing insect! I believe these galls are made by tiny Sage Leaf Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia audibertiae) flies in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera. That critter doesn't look like a fly, but I can make out antennae and legs. Maybe it's a parasite? The critter does look kind of like a tiny Lace Bug in the family Tingidae of the order Hemiptera. I wish I had noticed at the time so I could have gotten a better photo. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 19 November 2013)

The non-detachable galls go completely through the leaf with the upper surface shaped like an open volcano. I don't notice these galls very often, even when I look for them.
CommonNames: White Sage; Sage Leaf Gall Midge; Lace Bug
ScientificName: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Tingidae; Hemiptera
associatedTaxa: Salvia apiana; Lamiaceae; Rhopalomyia audibertiae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Tingidae; Hemiptera

File.Name: db22e_db18-017.jpg
Notes: 11001632945 This is another photo of a Gray Hairstreak (Strymon melinus) butterfly on late flowers of native Goldenbush (genus Isocoma, but what species?) in the Asteraceae plant family on a dry bluff above the Santa Ynez River. The upper wings are a beautiful dark gray, but they usually hide them when they perch. See this photo and the one [Previous] to that from the same day. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 18 November 2013)

File.Name: db22f_db15-008.jpg
Notes:
11001411645 Here are new flower clusters of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. They are the first new flowers of any native plant in the new rain year, coming even before the rains. You can see a bit of the beautiful manzanita-like shredding bark on the stems. (San Marcos Pass, 15 November 2013)

Some of the flower clusters are already looking a little "long in the tooth", but other plants haven't started flowering yet. This is actually one of our longest flowering natives, from September to April on different plants in different years - seven months!

File.Name: db23a_ab26-039.jpg
Notes:
11019465396 Here is Julie preparing a freshly harvested mushroom bounty for our Thanksgiving table in Oregon - way back in 2010. We're hoping for another mushroom harvest like this when we (finally) get up to our Oregon cabin this year! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 26 November 2010)

Clockwise from the lower left, here are Lobster Mushrooms (Hypomyces lacifluorum), White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus), Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes), and two piles of "ordinary" Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius). A good day's harvest!

We had every expectation of being on our way to Oregon today, but we got word from my son in Portland this morning that they have friends staying at the cabin until Monday. No problem. It's a two day drive, so we'll leave tomorrow on Sunday. I thought a photo from the archives of what we're looking forward to would be appropriate. I hope you all enjoy your Thanksgiving. I'll be back in a couple of weeks, hopefully with many new photos.
Locality:
Cantharellus cibarius). A good day's harvest!

We had every expectation of being on our way to Oregon today
CommonNames: Lobster Mushrooms; White Chanterelles; Black Chanterelles; Horn of Plenty; Chanterelles
ScientificName: Hypomyces lacifluorum; Cantherellus subalbidus; Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus cibarius
associatedTaxa: Hypomyces lacifluorum; Cantherellus subalbidus; Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus cibarius

File.Name: db23b_db23-017.jpg
Notes:
11019176366 This is a gnarly bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the rain gutter of our house. This one is perched at home, but you don't want to walk through one of their webs at night - Where's the spider! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2013)

I'm not proud that I got this photo with flash, but it was hidden in a dark place where the sun never shines. At some point these spiders will withdraw as dark lumps for the winter.

File.Name: db23c_db23-001.jpg
Notes: 11018954765 These are beautiful leaflets on a frond (or two) of native Coffee Fern (Pellaea andromedifolia) in the Polypodiaceae plant family - with raindrops. I guess this fern gets its name from the color, not the taste. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2013)

File.Name: db23d_db23-003.jpg
Notes: 11018901604 Here are tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plants growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. I guess they are the California Liverwort (Asterella californica). They're coming alive after our inch of rain two days ago. See this photo of them with umbrella-like sporophytes last year. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2013)

File.Name: db23e_db23-008.jpg
Notes: 11018713026 These are crustose lichens growing on a branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The pretty tan and red structures are the apothecia (spore-bearing structures) of the fungal part of the lichen partnership between fungus and algae. I really don't know our lichens at all, but maybe this is the year I try to learn them. (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2013)

File.Name: db23f_db23-004.jpg
Notes:
11018573374 Here's another photo of (mostly) foliose lichens growing on twigs of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I really don't know our lichens at all, but maybe this is the year I try to learn them. How quickly the lichens fill out after a rain! (San Marcos Pass, 23 November 2013)

We had every expectation of being on our way to Oregon today, but we got word from my son in Portland this morning that they have friends staying at the cabin until Monday. No problem. It's a two day drive, so we'll leave tomorrow. I'm already packed, so there nothing to do today except go out to look for a few new photos!

File.Name: dc08a_dc06-031.jpg
Notes:
11279937456 These are Snow Geese (Chen caerulescens) taking wing at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. This is a wonderful refuge located south of Willows, California. Look at that solid white line of resting geese across the middle of my photo in the background. There are many thousands of the birds! I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge. See my [Previous] two photos, which form a sequence. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, California, 6 December 2013)

We stopped by the wildlife refuge on our way home from our cabin in Oregon where we've been for the past two weeks. It was pleasant until the day after Thanksgiving, but it's been very cold since then. We left the cabin on Thursday, the day before snow and very cold weather would have closed us in. See this photo from Curtis Irish a bit north of us! It's been cold at home too, in the low 20s last night, and probably colder on Friday night. I'll have the first of many mushroom photos from our trip starting tomorrow.

Sorry I missed the Flickr Black Day protest. I've been away, I didn't know. Is the "new photo experience beta" finally being forced on us? *Sigh* I left Fotolog when they made similar changes last spring.

Look at that solid white line of resting geese across the middle of my photo in the background. There are many thousands of the birds! I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos, which form a sequence. I think this is my favorite of the three photos. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, California, 6 December 2013)

Notes: 11279431864 These are Snow Geese (Chen caerulescens) taking wing at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. This is a wonderful refuge located south of Willows, California. Look at that solid white line of resting geese across the middle of my photo in the background. There are many thousands of the birds! I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge. See my [Next] two photos, which form a sequence. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, California, 6 December 2013)

File.Name: dc08d_dc06-042.jpg
Notes:
11279018246 Here is an adult Bald Eagle (Haliaeetus leucocephalus) just leaving its perch at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. This is a wonderful refuge located south of Willows, California. We were in our car, but close. I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge. See my [Previous] two photos, which form a sequence of the bird taking flight. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, California, 6 December 2013)

There was some excitement last summer when a pair of these birds successfully raised a youngster - a first for the refuge. (See this source for more info.) I assume this bird is a winter visitor from the north.

File.Name: dc08e_dc06-043.jpg
Notes: 11279032363 Here is an adult Bald Eagle (Haliaeetus leucocephalus) in the air just after leaving its perch at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. This is a wonderful refuge located south of Willows, California. We were in our car, but close. I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge. See my [Next] and [Previous] photos, which form a sequence. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, California, 6 December 2013)

Notes: 11278518965 Here is an adult Bald Eagle (Haliaeetus leucocephalus) in flight at the Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge. This is a wonderful refuge located south of Willows, California. We were in our car, but close. I got this photo with my 105mm macro lens along the auto tour in the refuge. See my [Next] two photos, which form a sequence. (Sacramento National Wildlife Refuge, California, 6 December 2013)

File.Name: dc09a_dc09-011.jpg
Notes:
11299547805 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

I went into the frosty woods at home today looking for mushrooms, and the last thing I expected to find was a butterfly! But this Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly is "puddling" - taking moisture - on fallen leaves! I didn't find any mushrooms, or even any flies. I'm surprised it was active. It continues to be very cold, in the upper 30s when I got this photo at midday. That's warmer than yesterday! (San Marcos Pass, 9 December 2013)

I went to all the usual places, and there are no mushrooms in the woods yet. Maybe it's too cold for them? We had 1/2 + 1/3 inch of rain while we were away in Oregon, bringing our total to 1.8 inches for the rain year, just 29% of normal. It doesn't look good for the new year, but early rain totals are deceiving. See my early rain chart.

File.Name: dc09b_db28-023.jpg
Notes:
11299408793 The bounty of the woods!

This is the second basket of mushrooms that we filled for our Thanksgiving Day feast. Here are Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes), regular Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius), White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus), and Hedgehog Mushrooms (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum). We cooked them all up for dinner, see this photo for the details. Delicious! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2013)

The Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty were easily our favorite mushrooms at our Thanksgiving feast, followed by the Hedgehog Mushrooms. Unfortunately, we don't find either of them back at home. I'll have photos of them as they grow tomorrow.
CommonNames: Black Chanterelles; Horn of Plenty; Chanterelles; White Chanterelles; Hedgehog Mushrooms; Black Chanterelles; Horn of Plenty; Hedgehog Mushrooms
ScientificName: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantherellus subalbidus; Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum
associatedTaxa: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantherellus subalbidus; Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum

File.Name: dc09c_db28-016.jpg
Notes:
11299049164 This is the first mushroom basket we collected on Thanksgiving Day, with a forked horn deer skull that we collected on the same day. Julie noticed the skull and the scattered skeleton the day before, but we waited to collect it with the grandkids. A good day's harvest, but see my [Next] photo for the second basket! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2013)

The mushrooms are Hedgehog Mushrooms (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum), Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius), and White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus) - with a few prime Puffballs (Lycoperdon) hidden at the bottom. We had the puffballs - after slicing them of course, to make sure they were fresh and not Amanita "eggs" in disguise - with scrambled eggs the next morning, really delicious.
Locality: Lycoperdon) hidden at the bottom. We had the puffballs - after slicing them of course
CommonNames: deer; Hedgehog Mushrooms; Chanterelles; White Chanterelles; Puffballs
ScientificName: Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantherellus subalbidus; Lycoperdon
associatedTaxa: Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantherellus subalbidus; Lycoperdon

File.Name: dc09d_db28-034.jpg
Notes:
11298791315 Here is a pan of fresh-picked Thanksgiving Day Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms sizzling on the stove. These remarkable mushrooms deepen their color as they cook. We added some of them to the Turkey gravy and it was absolutely delicious. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2013)

These were easily our favorite mushrooms at our Thanksgiving feast, followed by the Hedgehog Mushrooms (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum). See my [Previous] photo for the cooking details.
CommonNames: Black Chanterelles; Horn of Plenty; Hedgehog Mushrooms
ScientificName: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum
associatedTaxa: Craterellus cornucopiodes; Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum

File.Name: dc09e_db28-026.jpg
Notes:
11298466446 Here is a pan of fresh-picked Thanksgiving Day mushrooms sizzling on the stove. It's a mixed pan of Hedgehog Mushrooms (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum), Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius), and White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus). We let them "stew in their own juices". It's really remarkable how much juice these mushrooms make in a hot pan! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2013)

We "dry sautÈ" them. Clean your mushrooms by trimming them and avoid water if possible - they are like sponges. Then slice them into a pan on high heat with no oil, and they will make their own sauce as the water cooks out of them. Let the juice cook down until it's almost gone, and add a bit of butter or olive oil, salt, and garlic and let it simmer... delicious!
ScientificName: Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantherellus subalbidus
associatedTaxa: Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum; Cantharellus cibarius; Cantherellus subalbidus

File.Name: dc09f_db28-012.jpg
Notes: 11298248806 Here are Grandma Julie and our twin grandkids hiking through the mushroom woods in Oregon on Thanksgiving Day morning. That's Otto in the middle and Kelvin on the right. The kids are good mushroom hunters and already know a few, thanks to good instruction from their dad. They are closer to the ground! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 28 November 2013)

File.Name: dc10a_dc10-014.jpg
Notes:
11317080054 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly looking like a target on a sandstone rock outcrop in the canyon today. I did see the butterfly on the ground, but it flew away to this rock that's out of my reach. This is a 800x800 pixel crop from my 4800x3200 original. It was another frosty morning, but it did warm up a bit today - in the 40s rather than the 30s. These almost-winter butterflies are interesting. See my photo of a Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly from yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2013)

The rest of my photos today are many different kinds of Chanterelle mushrooms from Oregon.

File.Name: dc10b_db27-005.jpg
Notes: 11316868566 This is a mysterious cluster of Black Chanterelle aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms in the Oregon woods. They are hard to notice, looking more like dead leaves or a hole in the ground. These mushrooms are growing in the same place we've found them for the last few years. It helps to know where they are! They are absolutely delicious - we rated them first at our Thanksgiving feast, served straight up and in our turkey gravy. See this photo of the same mushrooms sizzling "in the pan". (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

File.Name: dc10c_dc03-021.jpg
Notes:
11316648006 These are tasty White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus) growing in the Oregon woods. They look like ordinary Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius), but are white instead of orange. Most of these mushrooms that we found were too far gone for our Thanksgiving table, but these look pretty good. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

We rated them third at our Thanksgiving feast - after the Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty and Hedgehog mushrooms, but ahead of the ordinary Chanterelles. In my "mushroom basket" photo, it's the second from the left on top.

File.Name: dc10d_db27-012.jpg
Notes:
11316310836 These are typical Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods. We collected them the next day for our Thanksgiving dinner. Compare the mushrooms in my [Previous] photo. The "gills" are thick folds of tissue rather than the thin gills of other mushrooms. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

These Oregon Chanterelles are smaller and thinner than our mushrooms at home, and maybe they don't taste quite as good. The rain may have something to do with it. We rated them fourth at our Thanksgiving feast - after the Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty, Hedgehog mushrooms, and White Chanterelles.

File.Name: dc10e_dc01-016.jpg
Notes: 11316262453 I guess these are ordinary Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods - but they sure look different. The cap margins are smooth, and the stems are whitish and a bit scaly. The "gills" definitely have a chanterelle look, with thick folds of tissue rather than the thin gills of other mushrooms. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc10f_dc03-011.jpg
Notes: 11315943216 I believe these are the Funnel Chanterelle aka Winter Chanterelle (Cantharellus infundibuiformis) mushrooms growing on well-rotted wood in the Oregon woods. They are different from the usual chanterelles because of where they grow and the fibrillose-scaly caps. They are said to be edible, but they are rather small and scarce. I haven't tried them on the table. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc11a_dc11-019.jpg
Notes:
11332444695 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

I went back to the same place in the canyon where I was yesterday, and got this closer photo of a Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly. I reckon it's a different individual than I showed yesterday. It's a bit more tattered, and has a "bird-slice" missing from its left hindwing. They look more brown up close, and you can also see some blue on its forewings. (San Marcos Pass, 11 December 2013)

It was warmer today - in the 50s rather than the 40s like yesterday, or the 30s on the day before. It brings out some insects, like the Syrphid flies I've been seeing. But mostly it feels dry. We've had 1.81 inches of rain this year, just 29% of normal. I know that early rain does not predict total rainfall - see this chart. A third drought year will have real consequences, in the woods and the city.

File.Name: dc11b_db29-008.jpg
Notes:
11332405733 Here is a group of Hedgehog Mushrooms (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum) in the Oregon woods. Instead of gills or pores, they have spines or "teeth" to distribute their spores. They are delicious when cooled in a dry sautÈ. We rated them second at our Thanksgiving feast - right after the Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

That mushroom at the top-right had a Maple leaf covering it, which I removed for the photo. It looks a bit moldy. I wouldn't collect it for the table.

File.Name: dc11c_dc01-019.jpg
Notes:
11332099335 Here is a group of old Puffball (Lycoperdon) mushrooms in the Oregon woods. They have an interesting "skin" of geometrical shapes which are 5-, 6-, or 7-sided. I don't know what species they are, and I don't know if they're the same kind we added to our scrambled eggs on the morning after Thanksgiving. They were really tasty! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

As far as I know, all the Lycoperdons are edible or at least non-poisonous. It's important to get young caps, that are solid and white when you slice them. These old caps won't do. This also guards against Amanita "eggs" which might deceive you and spoil your meal!

File.Name: dc11d_dc01-022.jpg
Notes: 11332059784 This is a lobster-red "Lobster Mushroom" (Hypomyces lacifluorum) in the Oregon woods. The "lobster" is a parasitic crust over another host mushroom, typically a Lactarius or Russula, but you really can't tell because the lobster takes over. It's interesting that they come in both red and orange forms. See my [Previous] photo for more info.
Locality: Hypomyces lacifluorum) in the Oregon woods. The "lobster" is a parasitic crust over another host mushroom

File.Name: dc11e_db27-027.jpg
Notes:
11331583605 This is a fresh "Lobster Mushroom" (Hypomyces lacifluorum) in the Oregon woods. Actually the "lobster" is a parasitic crust over another host mushroom, typically a Lactarius or Russula, but you really can't tell because the lobster takes over. Woodland critters seem to love these mushrooms. We found many of them broken up with pieces scattered. This is one mushroom my grandkids can recognize right off! Usually they are orange, but sometimes red. See my [Next] photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

I've been leery about eating them, because you never know what mushroom it's growing on. Surprisingly, they are really delicious - at least fresh off the grill, when the fungus is fresh. True to their name, they have a slightly fishy taste, like crab cakes. We didn't collect them for the table this year, but we have in the past. There's a market for them in Portland.

File.Name: dc11f_dc03-005.jpg
Notes: 11331429076 Of course we humans aren't the only critters that enjoy eating wild mushrooms! Here are tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects enjoying what I guess is an Ivory Waxy Cap aka "Cowboy's Handkerchief" (Hygrophorus eburneus) mushroom in the Oregon woods. The caps are very slimy when wet, hard to even pick up. That may be a bit of the evanescent slime veil close to the stem. These little critters spoil mushrooms for the table, but they are having their own feast! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)
CommonNames: Springtail; Ivory Waxy Cap; Cowboy's Handkerchief

File.Name: dc12a_dc10-002.jpg
Notes:
11344783655 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

This is a handsome Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. It was still quite cold on this morning, but warm enough to bring out these pretty flies to perch in the sun. There seem to be many different kinds - bigger and smaller, with yellow and white stripes on their abdomen. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2013)

I'm posting early today. It was a busy day, and it will be a busy night with a good friend holding a ready and book signing for her new book. Hopefully I'll get new photos tomorrow, and of course more Oregon mushroom photos.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: dc12b_dc03-033.jpg
Notes: 11344699044 I believe this beauty is the Shaggy-Stalked Parasol (Lepiota clypeolaria or kin) mushroom in the Oregon woods. It's said to be poisonous, but it sure is striking. I only found a few of them this year, and never in the sunlight, whereas they were quite common last year. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc12c_db30-010.jpg
Notes: 11344597626 I believe this is the "Questionable Stropharia" (Stropharia ambigua) mushroom with purplish-black spores, though the gills look quite white in this photo. They have distinctive cottony white veil fragments on the margin of the cap. They are said to taste like "old leaves". David Arora (author of "Mushrooms Demystified") says it is "one of the most exquisitely beautiful of all mushrooms" - and I think I agree. I've never seen them at home, and I only found this one specimen in Oregon. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2013)

File.Name: dc12d_db29-032.jpg
Notes: 11344544133 This is an odd-looking "Delicious Lactarius" (Lactarius deliciosus) mushroom in the Oregon woods. It looks anything but delicious with those green colors on the cap! It is said to be "edible, but not necessarily delicious". This one is growing under Douglas Fir - like most of my Oregon mushrooms. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc12e_dc02-008.jpg
Notes:
11344249804 I'm not sure, but these sure look like the Ringless Honey Mushroom (Armillariella tabescens). They have white spores, the caps are dry, and there is no sign of a ring or veil. They appear to be growing from the ground, but there could well be buried wood down there. It seems odd, because that species is mostly eastern and southern - but here they are. They are said to be edible and delicious - IF that is what they are. Please let me know if you've found this mushroom in Oregon. (Road to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 December 2013)

We went up the river on a rainy Oregon day last week, and I got this photo handheld, in the rain, with my "normal" lens. Not bad, considering.

File.Name: dc12f_db29-006.jpg
Notes: 11344214273 Here is another remarkable "Lobster Mushroom" (Hypomyces lacifluorum) in the Oregon woods. Actually the "lobster" is a parasitic crust over another host mushroom, typically a Lactarius or Russula, but you really can't tell because the lobster takes over. They are either remarkably pretty or remarkably ugly depending on your tastes! See my photo yesterday for more info. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc13a_dc13-014.jpg
Notes:
11360755155 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

Here are the first flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family in the woods today. They are only the second new flowers of the rain year, after the Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) which started flowering way back on the autumnal equinox day. (See this photo.) Wild bees and hummingbirds depend on these early manzanita flowers, though I didn't see any today. You can see some old, dark galls of the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the old order Homoptera on the right. (San Marcos Pass, 13 December 2013)

It was another relatively cold day with a frosty morning and highs in the low 50s. I'll keep posting more Oregon mushroom photos from our recent trip until I run out or we get some mushrooms at home!
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Chaparral Currant; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: dc13b_db29-034.jpg
Notes: 11360647254 This is a beautiful and strange Coral Mushroom in the genus Ramaria, like something under the ocean. I find these corals very hard to key out. This one seems to have pink or salmon tips on the younger branches on top, so maybe it's the Pinkish Coral Mushroom (Ramaria formosa) - but I wouldn't bet the farm on it! Compare my [Previous] photo - are they the same or different? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc13c_dc03-026.jpg
Notes: 11360449004 This is a fresh Coral Mushroom in the genus Ramaria. Maybe it's the Yellow Coral Mushroom (Ramaria rasilispora), but I wouldn't bet the farm on it! The branch tips are definitely yellow colored, but I find these corals very hard to key out. This is a young one that is just pushing up. They are beautiful and strange, like something under the ocean. Compare my [Next] photo - are they the same or different? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc13d_dc03-016.jpg
Notes: 11360397943 These are strange Truncate Club Coral (Clavariadelphus truncatus) mushrooms growing with maple leaves under conifers in the Oregon woods. They are said to be delicious and sweet, but I haven't tried them. They look and feel like a Chanterelle, but they are related to the Coral mushrooms. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc13e_dc01-015.jpg
Notes: 11360150586 When I found this fungus, I thought it was a different Coral Mushroom, but I believe it is a Fetid False Coral (Thelephora palmata). I'm sorry I didn't smell it. It's said to have a garlic-like odor that becomes fetid (unpleasant) in age. The branches look like little moose antlers. This mushroom looked more violet when I saw it, but this is what my camera captured. It's a tough one to notice, blending into the background. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc13f_db29-017.jpg
Notes:
11359807496 At first I took this for another Polypore (Polyporaceae) growing on the ground from buried wood in the Oregon woods. When I got back to our cabin, I noticed a photo in David Arora's "Mushrooms Demystified" book that looked right.

I guess this is Hydnellum zonatum (or Hydnellum scrobiculatum or Hydnellum concrescens) in the Hydnaceae mushroom family. It's actually related to the Hedgehog Mushroom (Dentium repandum aka Hydnum repandum), with teeth instead of gills. I think I can see a few teeth on the left. I went back to confirm my guess the next day, and... it was gone. Some mushroom-hunting critter must have got it first. It's a lovely fungus. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)
ScientificName: Polyporaceae; Hydnellum zonatum; Hydnellum scrobiculatum; Hydnellum concrescens; Hydnaceae; Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum
associatedTaxa: Polyporaceae; Hydnellum zonatum; Hydnellum scrobiculatum; Hydnellum concrescens; Hydnaceae; Dentium repandum; Hydnum repandum

File.Name: dc14a_dc14-027.jpg
Notes:
11376405603 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a new "oak apple" gall of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's two galls growing side by side that have grown together? It looks like a fungus, but that's all oak tissue, with gall wasp larvae inside. This one has unusually rich colors for this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 14 December 2013)

Today was a wonderfully calm day. It warmed up to the 60s, but the ground is still frozen all day in places that never get the sun this close to the winter solstice. We don't get Santa Ana Winds in the Santa Barbara mountains, though I guess they are blowing down south. The air feels really dry - down to 9% RH down by the river at Paradise. I guess that's why we don't have any mushrooms yet, as they need some moisture in the air to develop spores.
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: dc14b_db27-024.jpg
Notes:
11375868276 These are tiny still-streaming plasmodia - forming sporangia - of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) growing on dead wood in the Oregon woods. They look like little mushrooms, but they have a different life cycle. Those black spots on the wood are no doubt tiny fungi. Fortunately I don't have to know slime molds in Oregon. I have enough trouble with our local slime molds back home in California! At least I can recognize what they are. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

Slime molds are NOT mushrooms or fungi, though they look fungus-like. Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals" - but they are really animals, not fungi. Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in decaying wood and humus. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures like these that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one decaying log to another.

File.Name: dc14c_db29-028.jpg
Notes: 11375909603 This is a closer view of the sporangia of a Leathery Grape Fern (Botrychium multifidum) in the Opioglossaceae plant family, growing in the Oregon woods. It's an odd native fern with basal compound leaves and a separate flowering scape with spore-bearing sporangia. See my [Previous] photo for a shot of the whole plant. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc14d_db29-027.jpg
Notes: 11375709414 This is an unusual fern that I've only noticed in this one place in the Oregon woods, and I find it every visit. I guess that it's a Leathery Grape Fern (Botrychium multifidum) in the Opioglossaceae plant family. It has a basal compound leaves and a flowering scape with spore-bearing sporangia rising separately from the ground. See my [Next] photo for a closer photo of the sporangia. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc14e_dc03-023.jpg
Notes: 11375355826 These are cute little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariaceae family - with tiny "eggs"! The eggs are really spore sacs called peridioles. The cap on top-right seems to have a bit of the original "lid" or "epiphragm" that initially covers the top of the nest. We had recent rain, so the cups are filled with water. Maybe they are genus Nidula with those shaggy hairs and habitat on wood? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc14f_dc01-010.jpg
Notes:
11375155715 These are strange antler-like fruiting bodies of the Candlesnuff Fungus (Xylaria hypoxylon or kin) - or do they look more like the grasping hands of the dead, especially that one on the left? I'm sure they are growing from buried wood under the moss. There are many other species that you need a microscope to distinguish, so this ID is really just a placeholder. The white dusting on the black stems is from asexual spores (conidia). Later on they form sexual spores from "perithecia" at the top of the fruiting body. Maybe that's what that all-black stalk is doing? (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

We also have these fungi at home, see this photo.

File.Name: dc15a_dc15-014.jpg
Notes:
11394708614 This is another brilliant Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly perched on the same sandstone rock outcrop in the canyon. This one looks almost perfect. That black crusty stuff in the upper-left is a tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plant that shrivels up when it's dry and comes back when it rains - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2013)

Julie asked the right question when she saw this photo, "What's it doing here now?" - so close to the winter solstice? I don't know the answer. My book says their flight time is all year, and food plant is nettles, which don't grow in the canyon. I went through my records, and I've found them before in September, October, November, December, January, April, and June. Today I saw a pair of them fluttering up in a spiral flight - maybe a mating tryst?

"Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!" I had enough interesting photos for today, I'll get back to Oregon mushrooms tomorrow. It was warm today - in the 60s at home, and probably warmer in the sun. It was also increasingly cloudy, a tropical disturbance from the south. There is a chance of showers on Thursday, but it doesn't sound like much.

File.Name: dc15b_dc15-003.jpg
Notes: 11394334704 This isn't the best photo because of the angle of the butterfly, but it's a record photo so I don't really care! This is a Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly with its proboscis out looking for moisture on fallen leaves of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this rain year. This one seems early. Last year, I didn't see them until January. This is a very plain butterfly on the underside, but quite brilliant on top. See this photo. You can see a bit of blue color on its wing fringes. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2013)

File.Name: dc15c_dc15-009.jpg
Notes: 11394088466 This isn't the best photo because of the angle of focus, but it's a handsome little Wasp (Hymenoptera) perched for a moment on dead-looking leaves of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. I guess it's an Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae) because of that long ovipositor, but I really don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2013)
CommonNames: Wasp; Bush Monkey Flower; Ichneumon Wasp
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Ichneumonidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae; Ichneumonidae

File.Name: dc15d_dc15-042.jpg
Notes:
11393551116 I happened to notice this tiny insect husk today on the underside of a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess it's a Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the old insect order Homoptera. It seems to have its proboscis stuck in the leaf, maybe as an anchor as it molts to become an adult? (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2013)

The top of its thorax is split like it's molting, but it also has a chunk missing from the side of its abdomen like a parasite emerged. It seems to have wings which are an adult feature, but I guess grasshoppers also get "training wings" as nymphs. I guess this one was molting.

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, and the husk is 1224 pixels long in the original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the hopper is 23.4x1224/4800 = 6 mm, less than 1/4 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

File.Name: dc15e_dc15-017.jpg
Notes:
11393344826 We had lizard weather today, in the 60s at least. This well-camouflaged little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) is resting on a sandstone bank by the creek in our canyon. That black stuff below it is a tiny Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plant that shrivels up when it's dry and comes back when it rains - see this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2013)

I only find these small lizards on these chilly mornings. It takes longer for the BIG critters to warm up than the small ones. It's simple geometry. A lizard that's twice as large in length has four times the surface area to collect sunlight, but eight times the bulk to warm up. (Think of cubes.) I guess that's why cold-blooded lizards don't get too big.
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Liverwort

File.Name: dc15f_dc15-029.jpg
Notes: 11393068735 This is mostly native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the Poaceae plant family growing in the canyon. But it's more complicated than that! There's a seedling California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) tree in the foreground, and a Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens, Rubiaceae) behind it, and a dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa, Cyperaceae) behind that! The sedge and the grass are similar size, but the grass has jointed stems which are quite shaggy. (San Marcos Pass, 15 December 2013)
CommonNames: Thin Grass; California Bay; Climbing Bedstraw; Roundfruit Sedge
ScientificName: Agrostis pallens; Poaceae; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Galium porrigens; Rubiaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae

File.Name: dc16a_dc16-003.jpg
Notes:
11412356755 These are mature "oak apple" galls of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family. It's a beautiful deciduous oak that grows on flat land in the valley. You can see exit holes on the left-most gall, where some wasps got out. Is this really the same gall as the one I showed on Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) a few days ago? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 15 December 2013)

It was uncommonly balmy today - almost 70 at home, and in the 80s at the river where I was today. There is a slight chance of rain on Thursday, but it doesn't sound like much. I liked what the weather person said in the NWS discussion this morning, in all caps and funky spelling as they do:

"A QUICK LOOK AT THE TEN DAY MDLS SHOW NO RAIN THROUGH THE 26TH BUT NO WISE MAN EVER TRULY BELIEVES THE DAY 8-10 FCST."

(This sincere comment disappeared from later versions of the discussion.)

File.Name: dc16b_db29-002.jpg
Notes: 11412075194 I guess these mushrooms from Oregon are (from left to right) - the Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa), The Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle (Helvella maculata or maybe H. sulcata?), and the Fluted White Elfin Saddle (Helvella crispa). All have carved out stems. Is that one one the left really the same as my [Previous] photo? I personally wouldn't collect any of them for my dinner table. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)
CommonNames: Fluted Black Elfin Saddle; Fluted Brown Elfin Saddle; Fluted White Elfin Saddle
