File.Name: dc16c_dc02-007.jpg
Notes: 11412012443 I believe these are large Fluted Black Elfin Saddle (Helvella lacunosa) mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods - as they should be. They are said to be edible when cooked, but I've never felt inclined to try it. Compare the left mushroom in my [Next] photo. I got this photo handheld, in the rain, with my "normal" lens. Not bad, considering. (Road to Twin Lakes, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 2 December 2013)

File.Name: dc16d_dc05-004.jpg
Notes: 11411617633 I believe this is the "Hooded False Morel" (Gyromitra infula or kin) growing from dead wood in the Oregon woods. It's quite large, almost fist-size. I first thought genus Helvella, but I think this is right. It's said to be quite poisonous, with MMH (monomethylhydrazine) - rocket fuel! Admire it, but don't eat it. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 5 December 2013)

File.Name: dc16e_dc01-006.jpg
Notes: 11411232545 Here are more cute little Bird's Nest Fungi in the Nidulariaceae family - some with tiny "eggs". The eggs are really spore sacs called peridioles. Maybe they are genus Nidula with strings attached to the eggs, shaggy hairs, and habitat on wood? These mushrooms are "splash cups" - raindrops knock the eggs out of the nest to scatter the spores. The empty cups persist for some time. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc16f_db29-021.jpg
Notes: 11411195083 These are cute little mushrooms known as Hare's Ear or Donkey Ears (Otidea onotica in the Oregon woods. I would call them Pigs' Ears, but I guess that name is already taken. They look like the ears of all those critters. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)
CommonNames: Hare's Ear; Donkey Ears; Pigs' Ears

File.Name: dc17a_dc17-022.jpg
Notes:
11428266774 Here is a rave of tiny Thrips (order Thysanoptera) insects on a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. I guess they are genus Aeolothrips in the family Aeolothripidae - see Bug Guide. I watched them for a while - they are like little roosters in the chicken yard! They seem to spend most of their time chasing each other away, though you can also see collecting at the throat of the flower. The pair on the lower-right seem to be thinking of mating. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2013)

These thrips are tiny. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, and the thrips are about 260 pixels long in the original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the thrips is 23.4x260/4800 = 1.3 mm, less than 1/16 inch. Like I said, these critters are tiny!

Darn it sure looked like rain today, but it didn't feel like rain. It was another balmy day in the 70s, and very dry despite the clouds. The is a slim chance of showers tomorrow evening. It's starting to feel like a third drought year, which will have some real consequences.

File.Name: dc17b_dc01-023.jpg
Notes: 11428136323 I guess this is the Scarlet Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe punicea) mushroom, but cp. Hygrocybe coccinea. It's one of those. This specimen was rather old and sagging, and it was the only one I found. The cap is viscid, and the stem is red, fading to yellow. It's the size of my fist, and very beautiful. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc17c_db30-011.jpg
Notes:
11427344084 This is a stunning little "waxy-cap" mushroom. It's small and quite brilliant. Maybe it's the Miniature Waxy Cap (Hygrocybe miniataaka Hygrophorus miniata)? It has a non-viscid cap that is convex rather than conical. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2013)

Note to self - don't try to photograph mushrooms with a curious dog! My son's dog "Rufus" rushed into the photo every time I got down for a photo and scattered everything!

File.Name: dc17d_dc03-030.jpg
Notes: 11427239954 I believe this is a strange and beautiful Crested White Coral (Clavulina cristata) mushroom, growing among fallen leaves of Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family in the Oregon woods. It looks like it belongs in an aquarium. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc17e_db29-015.jpg
Notes: 11426927254 My first thought was that this is some kind of Omphalina mushroom with those yellow caps and decurrent gills. But the shaggy caps don't seem right for that genus. They look like the Funnel Chanterelle aka Winter Chanterelle (Cantharellus infundibuiformis), and they are growing in the right place, on decomposing wood. Compare this photo. They are quite small. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc17f_db29-030.jpg
Notes:
11426761984 Either this is an old mushroom or some critter hurled!

Hah, I'm sure it's an old mushroom in the Oregon woods. If you look close on the left, you can make out a hoard of tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects on the last of it. I have no idea what it used to be, but I guess this is why we call them MUSH-rooms. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc18a_dc18-001.jpg
Notes:
11444082543 Trip photos are nice, but today's photos are always the best!

This is a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth. I reckon it's almost a butterfly because it is so pretty! I guess it is Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. You can see its "snout". It has brilliant red "underwings" that makes them easy to spot in the air, but you only get a glimpse on the ground with wings folded. They are quite small, about 1/2 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2013)

I noticed this one flying by as I got out of my truck today, and it landed in the middle of West Camino Cielo road! I unloaded my tripod and camera and got this photo laying in the middle of the road. Fortunately there's not much traffic of this road!

It was gloomy and breezy today as our new storm moves in. I don't expect much rain from this storm, but we had some wonderful clouds today, including unusual "mammary clouds" that only lasted for a few minutes.

File.Name: dc18b_dc01-009.jpg
Notes: 11443793994 I guess this is the Yellow-Staining Milk Cap (Lactarius vinaceorufescens) mushroom growing in the Oregon woods. We have the very similar Lactarius chrysorrheus in the woods at home, but that one grows under oak instead of conifers like this one. If you slice the gills, it makes white milk that very quickly turns yellow. It's said to be poisonous. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)11443602884 I guess this is the Western Painted Suillus (Suillus lakei) or kin mushroom in the Oregon woods. It's a "Bolete" mushroom with pores instead of gills, said to be associated with Douglas-Fir trees, as this one was. It's also said to edible, but not choice. I haven't tried it. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)
CommonNames: Yellow-Staining Milk Cap; Western Painted Suillus
ScientificName: Lactarius vinaceorufescens; Lactarius chrysorrheus; Suillus lakei
associatedTaxa: Lactarius vinaceorufescens; Lactarius chrysorrheus; Suillus lakei

File.Name: dc18c_db29-022.jpg
Notes: 11443602884 I guess this is the Western Painted Suillus (Suillus lakei) or kin mushroom in the Oregon woods. It's a "Bolete" mushroom with pores instead of gills, said to be associated with Douglas-Fir trees, as this one was. It's also said to edible, but not choice. I haven't tried it. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc18d_dc01-028.jpg
Notes:
11443506086 I'm pretty sure this is an Amanita mushroom in the Oregon woods - the only one I found on this visit. I meant to get back to it, but the weather turned funky and I never did. It's pushing up a "mushrump" in the mosses. I won't try to guess what species it is, but it sure is handsome. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

Update Dec. 20: That's what I THOUGHT it was, but Salabat has me convinced that it's really a White Matsutake (Armillaria ponderosa). (See his photo below.) I was thinking of Amanita calyptrata with that stand-up ring and I never got around to digging it up to check for a volva. °QuÈ l·stima!

File.Name: dc18e_db30-007.jpg
Notes: 11443240326 I guess these are Cortinarius mushrooms in the Oregon woods. You can see some rusty-brown spores at the base of the stalk on the left. The caps were dry, but when I went to remove the maple leaf from the cap on the right, the mushroom fell apart! I reckon that shows that the caps are slimy-viscid when wet, which puts it into subspecies Phlegmacium with cap viscid and stem without a rimmed bulb. I won't try to guess which species it is. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2013)

File.Name: dc18f_db29-029.jpg
Notes:
11443213363 So that's why we call them MUSH-rooms! This is an old mushroom in the Oregon woods. I have no idea what it used to be. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

As many mushrooms as I found in the Oregon woods during our Thanksgiving visit, we missed the peak of the season. I had to hunt for the mushrooms I found, and I saw many fallen soldiers like this one. My impression is that it's been dry in southern Oregon too.

File.Name: dc19a_dc17-007.jpg
Notes:
11458358226 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

This is a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae curled up like a kitty cat on dead leaves of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family. It has sad-looking eyes on its elongated head. This little spider isn't much more than 1/4 inch long. I actually saw several of them nearby once I noticed this one. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2013)

Many people are spooked by spiders. I'm more spooked that they have been so scarce in this drought autumn, like the silent spring that Rachel Carson wrote about many years ago.

We did get a splash of rain last night, but just 1/2 of 1/10 of an inch. I'll still be out looking for mushrooms tomorrow. Some mushroom has got to be first!

File.Name: dc19b_db29-031.jpg
Notes: 11458217844 This is some kind of Conk fungus - genus Ganoderma or kin - engulfing a pair of twigs on a Douglas-Fir tree in the Oregon woods. There are many Oregon conks, and I won't try to guess which kind this is. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc19c_db29-005.jpg
Notes: 11457906075 This is some kind of Conk fungus - genus Ganoderma or kin. It's not really a mushroom as it's not at all fleshy, but it works just like a mushroom. This one is growing on a dead Douglas-Fir tree next to an old excavation, no doubt by a Pileated Woodpecker. I hear these large woodpeckers all the time in Oregon, but I rarely see them. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 29 November 2013)

File.Name: dc19d_dc01-003.jpg
Notes: 11457727226 I don't know what this large fungus is. I reckon it's some kind of Polypore in the Polyporaceae, but it grows on the ground. I figure those Douglas-Fir needles are about an inch long, so you can appreciate how large it is - dinner-plate size or larger! The shelves of this fungus seems to grow around in a counter-clockwise spiral. If you know what this is, please let me know. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc19e_dc03-008.jpg
Notes: 11457667396 These are the tiny fruiting bodies of Yellow Fairy Cups or Lemon Discos (Bisporella citrina or kin) in the Helotiaceae family of fungi. They are only a couple of mm across, but there are lots of them. They are rather pretty if you get close enough to notice them. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc19f_db30-004.jpg
Notes: 11457502426 This is Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica), a familiar Jelly Fungus in the Tremelliales. It's common from Oregon to Southern California (at least!), but it's nice to see after months of drought back home. They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. These odd "mushrooms" appear after rain and then dry out into a hard yellow crust as it looses moisture. "Live long and prosper." (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 30 November 2013)

File.Name: dc20a_dc19-011.jpg
Notes:
11472359904 Trip photos are nice, but recent photos from home are always the best!

These are brilliant California Fuschia (Epilobium canum aka Zauschneria californica) in the Onagraceae plant family. Most of the flowers in the mountains are done for the year, but this one plant along Highway 154 is in full flower. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2013)

I went into the woods to look for mushrooms today, but there are still no mushrooms to find. We're heading off to solstice party at Painted Cave tomorrow, so I'm not sure if I'll be able to post. The moment of the Winter Solstice happens tomorrow morning at 9:11 AM PST. This is the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. Happy Solstice everyone!

File.Name: dc20b_dc01-008.jpg
Notes: 11472207245 I guess this is some kind of Laccaria mushroom in the Oregon woods, but I won't try to guess which kind it is. It's quite pretty with its gills up in the air like this. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc20c_dc01-027.jpg
Notes: 11472218053 I guess these are Cortinarius mushrooms in the Oregon woods, but I'm not sure. They appear to have rusty-orange spores, and don't seem at all viscid or sticky. What a rich shade of brown they are! (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc20d_dc03-012.jpg
Notes: 11472110853 These are cute little Mycena mushrooms growing in the moss in the Oregon woods. You can see a few tiny orange Springtail (order Collembola) insects on the caps on the left. I was surprised to find so few of them In Oregon, but it's been (relatively) dry there too. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)

File.Name: dc20e_dc01-007.jpg
Notes: 11471806186 This is a neat little Cup Fungus in the genus Peziza or Helvella - I didn't dig it up to see if it had a stem. Mushrooms have enough troubles without me digging them just to see if they have a stem! The fertile spore-bearing surface seems to be on the outside of the cup. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 1 December 2013)

File.Name: dc20f_dc03-006.jpg
Notes: 11471623696 I think these may be the small caps of the Alpine Jelly Cone aka Poor Man's Gumdrops (Guepiniopsis alpinus) mushroom growing on a fallen conifer branch in the Oregon woods. I had to squint to see if the caps are full or empty! (They appear to be mostly empty.) The little caps really do feel like gumdrop candy. Compare this photo from last year when there were more of them. They are larger and have more of a stem than Yellow Fairy Cups aka Lemon Discos (Bisporella citrina or kin) in the Helotiaceae, compare this photo. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 3 December 2013)
CommonNames: Alpine Jelly Cone; Poor Man's Gumdrops; Yellow Fairy Cups; Lemon Discos

File.Name: dc21a_dc21-010.jpg
Notes:
11486685515 This is new growth on dead-looking stems of Galium nuttallii in the Rubiacae plant family. I guess they always look dead at this time of year, but I was really worried they wouldn't come back in the drought year! I figure it's an appropriate image for the Winter Solstice - new life, new hope for the new year. I hope it really rains! (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2013)

I figure that the Winter Solstice is the real start of the new year, despite what the calendar says. The moment of the solstice happened this morning at 9:11 AM PST. This is the "dark night of the year" in the Northern Hemisphere. The days will start getting just a bit longer tomorrow, and the sun will rise just a bit higher in the sky tomorrow. Happy Solstice everyone!

File.Name: dc21b_dc21-005.jpg
Notes: 11486698133 Here are new leaves of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus vestitus) in the Fabaceae plant family entwined in the spiny leaves of Chaparral Yucca (Yucca whipplei) plants in the Liliaceae plant family - now renamed as Hesperoyucca whipplei in the Agavaceae. It's good to see new growth in the very dry autumn of a dry drought year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 December 2013)

File.Name: dc21c_dc16-008.jpg
Notes:
11486481473 Here are new flowers and berries of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. You can clearly see the manzanita-like peeling bark of the plants. They are full of hope for the new year. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2013)

They are the first new flowers of any native plant in the new rain year, coming even before the rains. I found the first flowers in the woods way back on the Fall Equinox on September 22, and now it's the Winter Solstice! See this photo. I guess this is the longest flowering native plant in our woods, from September until March.

File.Name: dc21d_dc19-019.jpg
Notes: 11486268654 Fall color (almost winter) on native Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family in the canyon at home. These are hard colors to capture with a digital camera. It looks more intense than it appears here. (San Marcos Pass, 19 December 2013)

File.Name: dc21e_dc17-027.jpg
Notes:
11485963764 Fall color (almost winter) on native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family. They grow deep in our canyon where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 17 December 2013)

Thimbleberries are common and almost a roadside weed in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are quite uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I guess it's a relict plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change. I've never found the berries here.

File.Name: dc21f_dc18-012.jpg
Notes: 11485794575 These are unusual "Mammary clouds" aka "Mammatus clouds" that appeared in the dark sky at sunset a couple of days ago just before we had a bit of rain. The name means just what you think it means! I was hoping they were a portent, but the storm was disappointing. Wikipedia has more info. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2013)

File.Name: dc22a_dc22-017.jpg
Notes:
11505230566 This is a little Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) working the new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. The flower is anything but a typical hummingbird flower, but I guess it works for the hummer. I know they also love manzanita flowers. Of course it was a lucky shot! I just had one chance for this photo before the hummer took off. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2013)

*Gulp* I'm finished with Oregon mushroom photos, which means I have to get back to our local woods full time. Actually I've been taking photos every day in the last few weeks that I have to work through. That's the trouble with trips - you show trip photos and then have to make up the home photos! I'll get there.

File.Name: dc22b_dc22-030.jpg
Notes: 11505008425 This is a lovely Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly on new flowers of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family in our mountains. If you look close, you can see her proboscis winding with perfect aim into an open flower. It's a beautiful butterfly on beautiful flowers. Cold as it's been, this has been a good winter for butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2013)

File.Name: dc22c_dc22-010.jpg
Notes: 11504833893 This is a brilliant Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly trying to warm up on leaves of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. (Sorry Sandman - it feels cold to us! The temps are in the 40s.) The photo is a bit of a crop. This is the first one of these butterflies I've found outside of our canyon, on the trail that starts with the Knapp's Castle trail and goes off to the side to the ridge above Paradise Canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2013)

File.Name: dc22d_db27-002.jpg
Notes:
11504606004 This is a last photo of the tasty Black Chanterelle aka Horn of Plenty (Craterellus cornucopiodes) mushrooms in the Oregon woods. They are hard to notice, looking more like dead leaves or a hole in the ground. These mushrooms are growing in the same place we've found them for the last few years. It helps to know where they are! They are absolutely delicious - we rated them first at our Thanksgiving feast, served straight up and in our turkey gravy. See this photo of the same mushrooms sizzling "in the pan". (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

*Sigh* Looking at these photos makes me hungry! These are the last of my mushroom photos from Oregon this year. I have a few more photos, but I've been stretching them out as it is. It's time to move on - to butterflies, if not yet our own local mushrooms. We need real rain to bring them up!

File.Name: dc22e_db27-009.jpg
Notes:
11504456665 This is a last photo of typical Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms growing in the Oregon woods. We collected them the next day for our Thanksgiving dinner. The "gills" are thick folds of tissue rather than the thin gills of other mushrooms. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

These Oregon Chanterelles are smaller and thinner than our mushrooms at home, and they don't quite have the texture of ours. The rain or the cold may have something to do with it. We rated them fourth at our Thanksgiving feast - after the Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty, Hedgehog mushrooms, and White Chanterelles.

File.Name: dc22f_db27-018.jpg
Notes:
11504357694 This is a last photo of the tasty White Chanterelles (Cantherellus subalbidus) growing in the Oregon woods. They look like ordinary Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius), but are white instead of orange. We collected these for our Thanksgiving table. (Near Susan Creek, North Umpqua River, Oregon, 27 November 2013)

We rated them third at our Thanksgiving feast - after the Black Chanterelles aka Horn of Plenty and Hedgehog mushrooms, but ahead of the ordinary Chanterelles. In my "mushroom basket" photo, it's the second from the left on top.

File.Name: dc23a_dc23-024.jpg
Notes:
11524872013 I swear I didn't see the naked lady's torso draped in a peeling skirt when I took this photo, but there she is! I just saw a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family with interesting shredding bark - until I got this photo home and saw it on my computer screen. It's a lovely tree and a lovely lady. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

I'm done with Oregon mushroom photos, but there is always something interesting to photograph in our woods! It was warmer today, very pleasant.

File.Name: dc23b_dc23-033.jpg
Notes: 11524663764 These are new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family - with holes! I believe the holes are from wild bees or other insects doing "nectar theft" on the flowers before they open, like those on the right. They remind me of those fancy easter eggs that can you look into. In this case, you can see the stamens inside. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

File.Name: dc23c_dc23-041.jpg
Notes: 11524572615 This is a remarkable new flower of native California Aster (Corethrogyne filaginifolia aka Lessingia filaginifolia) in the (Asteraceae) plant family. Any native plant that can flower at this time of year - in our summer-dry climate, in a drought year - has my respect! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

File.Name: dc23d_dc23-048.jpg
Notes: 11524532654 These are mature nuts of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. I've tasted them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting nuts! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

File.Name: dc23e_dc23-055.jpg
Notes:
11524062643 These sure look like red leaf galls of the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the old order Homoptera. These are growing on Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. But it's curious because I usually find the galls much earlier in the year on Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca). See this photo from April 15 this year and this one from November 15. Is this something different? (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

Maybe it's the Manzanita Leaf Fungus (Exobasidium vacinii)? If so, it's remarkable that the gall and the fungus look so much alike, though they are both building on the same genome.
CommonNames: Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid; Eastwood Manzanita; Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Fungus
ScientificName: Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Exobasidium vacinii
associatedTaxa: Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca; Exobasidium vacinii

File.Name: dc23f_dc23-036.jpg
Notes: 11523588265 This is a native Coulter Pine (Pinus coulteri) in the Pinaceae plant family in the mountains showing signs of extreme drought stress in this drought year, our second in a row. Maybe it's the Western Bark Beetle (Dendroctonus brevicomis that's killing our trees? It starts with a few dead branches like this, and soon kills the whole tree. See this photo. I've never seen this kind of damage before in our mountains. I feel helpless. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

File.Name: dc24a_dc23-028.jpg
Notes:
11540151935 Here are ripe berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. The plants are also called Christmas Berries, which is appropriate for tonight. The ripe berries are edible, but astringent. If you make an infusion in boiling water and add a bit of honey, they make a delicious drink. They are having a surprisingly good year, despite our drought. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

Today's photos are all fruit, flowers, buds, and seedlings - hope for the new year.

I wish you all the best for the holidays,
Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
ScientificName: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
associatedTaxa: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: dc24b_dc23-016.jpg
Notes: 11540214636 These are new flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. I've heard it called the "taco plant" because of the folded leaves, like hard taco shells. The buds wait on the plant for months to flower, until next March or April. The foliage is beautiful too, with thick succulent leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

File.Name: dc24c_dc23-059.jpg
Notes: 11540122986 Here are blushing new flowers of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. Dry as it's been, they are making beautiful flowers. I saw several different insects working the flowers, but I'll have to get back to them when I'm not so busy. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

File.Name: dc24d_dc22-004.jpg
Notes: 11539848604 These are odd flower buds of native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. The leaves are furry-tomentose underneath. They've been hanging on this shrub since early October - see this photo. I think I see signs of swelling between the segmants, and I hope they will be flowering soon. (San Marcos Pass, 22 December 2013)

File.Name: dc24e_dc22-011.jpg
Notes:
11539678365 These are new flower buds of native Bigpod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family in the mountains. In a good year, it's the shrub that covers the south-facing slopes of the mountains in flowers, like snow. At least there are flower in buds this so far dry year! (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

Notice that these shrubs have alternate leaves. Most of these Ceanothus species with thick leaves have opposite leaves, but this one is unusual. It only grows on the south-facing side of our east-west trending mountains. On the north-facing slopes, it is replaced by Ceanothus cuneatus.

File.Name: dc24f_dc24-013.jpg
Notes: 11539596015 Here are new seedlings growing in the canyon. I like to think they are California native plants, but I really don't know at this point when they are just starting to get their first true leaves. Good luck little seedlings, I hope we get some rain soon! (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2013)

File.Name: dc25a_dc25-001.jpg
Notes:
11557371426 Spiders for Christmas Day? Well, why not - it's a nativity scene!

This is the same bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae under the eaves of our house that I've photographed before. See this photo. She's become a mother, with a nest of her own! I took several photos of this critter with and without flash, but I liked this one best, taken without flash with a 4/10 second exposure on our shaky upstairs porch. The white balance isn't quite right, but it's still a nice photo. The white anchor-spots of the webbing are remarkable, like the old MGM musicals where the floor lights up under your feet! (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2013)

I wish you all the best for the holidays,

Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
ScientificName: Araneus; Araneidae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all.
associatedTaxa: Araneus; Araneidae; Peace on earth, goodwill to all.

File.Name: dc25b_dc24-010.jpg
Notes:
11557047306 Here is another brilliant Red Admiral (Vanessa atalanta) butterfly perched on dead oak leaves along the road in the canyon. I've been seeing them every day that I go to this place that gets morning sunlight. They are having a good year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2013)

The warm weather is bringing out other butterflies, most notably (I'm pretty sure) an unusual California Tortoiseshell (Nymphalis californica) butterfly yesterday. (See
CommonNames: Red Admiral; California Tortoiseshell; Spring Azure; Red Admirals; California Dogface
ScientificName: Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis californica; Celastrina ladon; Vanessa atalanta; Colias eurydice
associatedTaxa: Vanessa atalanta; Nymphalis californica; Celastrina ladon; Vanessa atalanta; Colias eurydice

File.Name: dc25c_dc18-003.jpg
Notes:
11556906043 This is a pretty little diurnal (daytime) moth perched on the rear bumper of my car a week ago. I'm pretty sure it's Pyrausta dapalis - a Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. You can see its "snout". It has brilliant red "underwings" that makes them easy to spot in the air, but you only get a glimpse on the ground with wings folded. See this photo. They are quite small, less than 1/2 inch long. (San Marcos Pass, 18 December 2013)

This photo is a stand-in for the moth I saw today but couldn't get a photo of.

File.Name: dc25d_dc24-012.jpg
Notes:
11556272565 This is the tiny gall of a "Pumpkin Gall Wasp" (Dryocosmus minusculus) in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera. It's growing on a leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It does look a bit like a tiny pumpkin or gourd. It might be the smallest "detachable" gall I've found in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 24 December 2013)

This is an 800x800 crop from my original 4800x3200 photo (in DX mode) taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens. The gall is about 400 pixels wide in my original photo, and my camera sensor in DX mode is 23.4 mm wide. So the size of the gall is 23.4x400/4800 = 2.0 mm, less than 1/10 inch. Like I said, this gall is tiny!

I don't know what that sand-like stuff below the gall is. It's actually much too small to be sand. I looked all around the gall for the sign of a new opening, and couldn't find one. It's surely not an accident.
Locality: in DX mode) taken at 1:1 magnification with my macro lens.  The gall is about 400 pixels wide in my original photo
CommonNames: Pumpkin Gall Wasp; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Dryocosmus minusculus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: dc25e_dc25-005.jpg
Notes: 11556194364 Here is new growth on a perennial native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family in a rocky place. I guess you'll have to take my word for the ID, but I'm pretty sure it's right. All new grasses look more or less the same. It's good to see new growth on this dead-looking plant. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2013)

File.Name: dc25f_dc23-022.jpg
Notes: 11556194073 These are ripe berries of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. They go with my [Previous] photo of Toyon berries, though they are more orange. The berries are edible, though a bit mealy. The trees really belong further north, but here the are in our mountains. They're having a tough time in this drought year, and only a few trees have berries. The trees are very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 23 December 2013)

File.Name: dc26a_dc26-005.jpg
Notes:
11576047504 Here is a remarkable furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched oh so delicately on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's cute! You can see the outline of the real fly underneath its vestiture of thick hairs. I saw them for the first time yesterday in the air, and got this photo today on the ground. I found these little flies for the first time last year in mid-January. I think of them as winter flies. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2013)

This winter continues to be very dry. The relative humidity was down to 5% today at Paradise. That's fire weather! These flies must be confused, and so am I.

File.Name: dc26b_dc26-012.jpg
Notes:
11575750333 My, what remarkable eyes you have!

I found this tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) perched on a slender leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family today. I reckon it's a female with that ovipositor showing. What amazing iridescent eyes! I'm not sure what it's doing out in the woods so early in the rain year, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2013)
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Mimulus aurantiacus; pubescens; Scrophulariaceae; Phrymaceae

File.Name: dc26c_dc26-008.jpg
Notes:
11575077935 This little Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) is in lizard heaven! It's perched with eyes closed on a sunny dead leaf of native Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's been catching flies that rest on the leaves. What could be better? (San Marcos Pass, 26 December 2013)

I was hoping it would catch a fly for a photo. See this photo and this one at Fotolog, taken way back in 2005. If you enjoy my natural history photos, take a look at my Fotolog page where I have over 9,000 more photos dating back to 2003. I like to think that my photography skills have improved over the years, but not all the photo-ops!
CommonNames: Blue-belly Lizard; Western Fence Lizard; Coast Live Oak

File.Name: dc26d_dc20-001.jpg
Notes: 11575462836 There are bigger flies and smaller ones. This fly (order Diptera) is tiny, 1/8 inch or so. I have no idea what kind of fly it is, but it has an unusually large head. It's perched on a leaf of California Coffeeberry (Rhamnus tomentosa aka Frangula californica ssp. tomentella) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (We have shrubs with both glabrous and furry leaves in the canyon.) (San Marcos Pass, 20 December 2013)
ScientificName: Diptera; Rhamnus tomentosa; Frangula californica; tomentella; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Rhamnus tomentosa; Frangula californica; tomentella; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: dc26e_dc10-008.jpg
Notes:
11574717343 Just a fly... but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds! Sometimes it seems like every fly I see in the woods is a different species. This one looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera, but I don't really know or care much. (San Marcos Pass, 10 December 2013)

If you notice dates, you'll see that this photo dates back a few weeks to our return from Thanksgiving in Oregon. That's the trouble with trips - you show trip photos and then have to make up the home photos!

File.Name: dc26f_dc25-020.jpg
Notes:
11574512234 Well, sharpness isn't everything to a photo!

This is a small Orb-Weaver Spider (Araneus) spider in the family Araneidae subduing a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera). I've shown the spider before. Spider and fly were rapidly spinning around and around, but I took the photo anyway. I like this photo even though it's not sharp because it shows the spider's face - with palps and chelicerae open, showing its red fangs. I have another sharper photo that is boring by comparison. You might want to stand back from your screen a few feet. (San Marcos Pass, 25 December 2013)

File.Name: dc27a_dc27-012.jpg
Notes:
11596132076 These are mature "oak apple" galls of the California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera growing on a branch of Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) in the Fagaceae plant family. This one tree is particularly attractive to the gall wasps, maybe because it's on the edge of a field where the weak-flying wasps tend to collect? There are hundreds of galls on this one tree and more on the ground below. Forgive me - I sliced one open to find the critter inside. See my [Previous] photo! (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 December 2013)

Today's photos are just two trees - native Valley Oak and Blue Oak. It was another very dry day with relative humidity down to just 3% at the river, where I was. I didn't know RH went that low!
CommonNames: California Gall Wasp; Valley Oak; Valley Oak; Blue Oak
ScientificName: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Fagaceae

File.Name: dc27b_dc27-010.jpg
Notes:
11595708366 I sliced open a mature "oak apple" gall that I found on the ground under a Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family, see my [Next] photo. It's the gall of a California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. I found this little grub inside, close to its original capsule - that hole in the upper-left. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 December 2013)

I can't swear that it's the larva of the gall wasp, since these galls also host inquilines (which just hang out) and parasites (which attack the wasp larvae). These galls are really apartment buildings for wasps with many other inhabitants! It's interesting that when I sliced open the gall, this displaced critter immediately started digging in to the spongy gall material.

I don't like opening these galls and (probably) killing the inhabitants, but I figured I would do it once for an educational purpose. The gall inside feels like fine-grained foam. But it looks more like wood fiber in this photo, and it also has a "radial structure" like a tree branch. Remember that these galls are actually made by the tree, of tree tissue.
ScientificName: Quercus lobata; Fagaceae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus lobata; Fagaceae; Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: dc27c_dc27-002.jpg
Notes: 11594746093 These are two photos of the same Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) tree in the Fagaceae plant family taken from roughly the same spot 11 days apart. This is a deciduous oak that drops its leaves for the winter. See this photo to see the lobed leaves that distinguish it from the Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in my [Previous] photos. The fall colors aren't much. It's interesting that the scene looks greener in the first photo. It's been very dry. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 16 December 2013 and 27 December 2013)

File.Name: dc27d_dc27-032.jpg
Notes: 11594611303 This is a closer photo of foliage of a native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's a deciduous tree, and these leaves are loosing their green before they drop. The "fall color" isn't much. Note that the leaves are entire or shallow-lobed, unlike the Valley Oaks (Quercus lobata). (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 December 2013)

File.Name: dc27e_dc27-043.jpg
Notes: 11594496204 Native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) trees in the Fagaceae plant family typically form these lovely "potreros" - grassy areas with well-spaced trees that look like an orchard. I think of as a dry land tree, but there are a few groves on the hills along the Santa Ynez River. These trees are deciduous, and the fresh leaves have a distinctly blue-green color which lives up to their common name. Here they are showing some fall (now winter) colors. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 December 2013)

File.Name: dc27f_dc27-045.jpg
Notes: 11594848116 This is a lovely native Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It's not a common tree in our area, but there are a few groves on the hills along the Santa Ynez River. These trees are deciduous, and the fresh leaves have a distinctly blue-green color which lives up to their common name. They sort of look like a deciduous Coast Live Oak. See my [Next] two photos. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 27 December 2013)

File.Name: dc28a_dc28-016.jpg
Notes:
11615288623 This is a little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) caught in the air moving towards a cluster of new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing in our mountains. See my [Previous] photo. You can see a bit of pollen stuck to the hummingbird's bill. Thus pollination happens. I got lucky with focus for this photo. It's close to 1:1 crop, but not quite. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 December 2013)

I can always tell when the manzanita starts to bloom because our resident hummers at home suddenly disappear for a few weeks.

Today was another beautiful day in paradise, but a bit cooler and breezier. There is no change in the weather expected through New Years. Autumn is usually the toughest time for new photos, but this year it goes on and on into winter.

File.Name: dc28b_dc28-018.jpg
Notes:
11614821045 This is a little female Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) with its bill in a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing in our mountains. The flower is anything but a typical hummingbird flower, but I guess it works for the hummer. They also like the short-tubed Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum, Grossulariaceae) flowers - see this photo. There is a bit of manzanita pollen stuck to the hummingbird's bill. Thus pollination happens. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 December 2013)

Of course this was a lucky shot! This is a 1:1 crop from my original photo. There were half a dozen hummers in the shrub, but they didn't like to pose and avoided me. This photo is not quite sharp, but who cares?
CommonNames: Anna's Hummingbird; Bigberry Manzanita; Chaparral Currant
ScientificName: Calypte anna; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Calypte anna; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Ribes malvaceum; Grossulariaceae

File.Name: dc28c_dc28-013.jpg
Notes:
11614925414 This is a common Honey Bee (Apis mellifera, Hymenoptera) digging in to a new flower of Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Bee heaven! (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 December 2013)

I also saw a few wild bees and flies, but I couldn't get photos. I'll try again.
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Hymenoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: dc28d_dc28-002.jpg
Notes:
11614254995 These are the light-trails of the tiny flies (order Diptera) that dance in the sunbeams. I got the photo near the creek, with a California Bay (Umbellularia californica, Lauraceae) tree behind. I'm sure the "double-tracks" is the sun reflecting from each of the flies' wings. These little flies are quite invisible unless you stand facing the sun just so. I assume this is a mating thing. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2013)

In some years, we have massive mating flights of these flies raising like clouds above the oak trees. See this photo from last year, and this old photo (and the two previous photos) on Fotolog from Christmas Day, 2009. I don't think this will happen in this very dry year, so I figured I'd better post while I can!
Locality: and the two previous photos) on Fotolog from Christmas Day

Notes:
11614284093 Just another fly... but I love photographing flies. They have so much structural detail and there are so many different kinds! This one looks bristly enough to be a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera, but I don't really know. It's perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 28 December 2013)

I used to key out insects - especially flies - several decades ago, but it's been too many years. I'm sure someone else can figure out the wing venation from this photo, and I'll be grateful for a comment!

File.Name: dc29a_dc29-024.jpg
Notes:
11637342703 Here are beautiful yellow flowers of native Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. I sometimes half-joke that there are always flowers on Bush Poppy *somewhere* in the mountains, and in this drought year I guess it's true! There's a new seed pod forming in the very top-right. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2013)

These beautiful days are getting boring. We need real rain to break this third consecutive drought year. There have been reports that this is the driest calendar year in recorded history in California, at least in LA and SF. The woods are showing it, but not these flowers!

File.Name: dc29b_dc29-013.jpg
Notes: 11637194934 The new flowers of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family are quite variable in our mountains. Sometimes they are blushing pink like those on the left, other times they are most white. Usually it's the whole shrubs that show the different colors, so I guess it's genetic. Which would you pick for your garden? (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 December 2013 and San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2013)

File.Name: dc29c_dc29-010.jpg
Notes:
11636641564 This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera), showing the interesting "rising sun" pattern on it's wings. This is the same kind of fly with wonderful eyes that I showed in this photo a couple of days ago. It's perched on a slender leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus aka Mimulus aurantiacus var. pubescens) in the Scrophulariaceae or Phrymaceae plant family today. I reckon it's a female with that ovipositor showing. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2013)

Maybe it's genus Trupanea? Compare these photos at BugGuide.

File.Name: dc29d_dc29-004.jpg
Notes:
11636036635 There are signs of drought stress everywhere in the woods as we move into our third consecutive drought year. This is native Southern California Dudleya aka Lanceleaf Liveforever (Dudleya lanceolata) in the Crassulaceae plant family, growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon at home. It's barely hanging on. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2013)

I will keep trying to document the drought in my photos. I guess it's an opportunity for a naturalist photographer, but I'd much rather find plants that are doing well!

File.Name: dc29e_dc29-020.jpg
Notes:
11635694805 Here are lots of ripe nuts on a shrub of winter-bare native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. The nuts are about 1/2 inch across. For some reason, these plants along West Camino Cielo road are having a good year. I haven't see a single nut on the plants in our canyon. See this photo for a closer shot. (San Marcos Pass, 29 December 2013)

The nuts split open to eat, like pistachios. I've eaten them, and they seem edible and good. What a remarkable shrub - it grows in woodland and chaparral, it has wonderful white flowers, the deciduous leaves turn golden in the fall, and it has these interesting nuts!

File.Name: dc29f_dc28-023.jpg
Notes: 11635727333 You have to look close in the center of this photo to see anything. This is a little male Anna's Hummingbird (Calypte anna) with its bill in a new flower of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family growing in our mountains. You can see a bit of it's brilliant red iridescent florets on its head, and a bit of manzanita pollen stuck to the hummer's bill. See my [Previous] two photos for the female. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 28 December 2013)

File.Name: dc30a_dc30-016.jpg
Notes:
11658560953 Here are many Monarch Butterflies (Danaus plexippus, order Lepidoptera) hanging from the non-native Eucalyptus trees at the Ellwood Main Monarch Grove today, part of the Coronado Butterfly Preserve. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2013)

The monarch butterflies from all the western states on our side of the Rocky Mountains congregate on the West Coast every year in the same trees. (East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.) Some of them resting here for the winter, though there are many other groves that are less well known. This is one of the world's great migrations.

I had to go to town today, so I took a trip to the butterfly preserve to see how it's doing. I talked with a docent at the preserve who said this was the fewest butterflies he has seen in many years. There are thousands of butterflies rather than tens of thousands. He mentioned the drought, and also that many butterflies are flying around town since it's been so warm. I really wanted to get close photos of single butterflies, but I couldn't find them. I guess it's so warm they are feeling adventurous. It's still worth the trip!
Locality: East Coast Monarchs head to Mexico.)  Some of them resting here for the winter

File.Name: dc30f_dc30-021.jpg
Notes:
11657932126 I believe this counts as a gall, though it's caused by a fungus rather than an insect. This photo shows bright orange spore masses on a slightly swollen stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. I believe it's the work of the Baccharis Rust Gall Fungus (Puccinia evadens) in the order Pucciniales aka Uredinales. Sometimes the swollen stems look more gall-like. (Ellwood Mesa, 30 December 2013)

I was really looking for butterflies on the mesa today - see my [Next] photos.

File.Name: dc31a_dc31-012.jpg
Notes:
11678189043 This is the first shiny red flower of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family that I've noticed in the mountains this year. The flowers typically hang from the stems, but it's too early for that. This is a bit of a crop because it's growing on a steep hillside that is downright slippery when it's this dry, and I didn't want to push my luck. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2013)

This is the 3rd flowering shrub I've found this year - after Chaparral Currant and Bigberry Manzanita. It's blooming as though there's no drought at all! It's a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. There's been some discussion on Facebook about whether Anna's Hummingbirds are native, and I submit the timing of our hummingbird flowers as evidence that they are. Along with California Fuschia, these shrubs pretty much wrap around the new year to keep the hummers going.

A new flower makes an appropriate image for the new year. I believe the real new year started a week ago on the solstice, and I've always been curious why the new year begins on January 1 in our calendar. Happy New Year!
CommonNames: Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry; Chaparral Currant; Bigberry Manzanita; California Fuschia

You can see that they are growing on the underside of an oak leaf. The mass is made up of individual capsules which each house a single wasp larva. (San Marcos Pass, 31 December 2013)

File.Name: dc31f_dc31-026.jpg
Notes: 11676966195 These are old dried-up galls of the Clasping Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis prehensa) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. The hole is an "exit hole" made by the wasp which matured inside. When they are fresh, they produce real "oak honey" on their surface, which attracts ants and many other insects. See this photo. File.Name: c101a_c101-058.jpg
Notes:
68400206 New Year's Day should be a day to look ahead, but I looked back instead. I hiked out to our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch, across the highway from home. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2012)

Julie and I used to rent this cabin when it was seeing better days about 35 years ago. Now, it's just another tumble-down cabin in the woods - but for me this will always be "The Cabin."

Julie and I lived here for 10 years with no electricity or phone back in the 70s and into the 80s. We had artesian spring water, propane refrigerator and hot water, Aladdin kerosene lamps, and a dangerous - but wonderful - wood-fire sauna. We had a large garden, along with goats, pigs, rabbits, ducks, chickens, and geese. I started learning local natural history here, we played music into the night, I built my first computer using wire-wrap and a propane torch to solder, and I started teaching middle school. Our two kids started life here.

So many memories in this tumble-down cabin that make it a glow for me now... Maybe every tumble-down cabin in the woods has this glow in memory for someone.

We moved a few miles away to our new house in a different canyon in 1983. The cabin has been abandoned for many years except for the deer mice and wood rats. Now it's a "Hanta Hotel" - with a threat of Hanta Virus from rodent droppings just breathing the air inside. Life goes on.

File.Name: c101b_c101-014.jpg
Notes:
68400189 This is looking under the surprising cap of a "Man on Horseback" (Tricholoma flavovirens) mushroom! It's surprising because it has such brilliant yellow gills hidden under those dull brown caps. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2012)

It's said to be edible and delicious, but I haven't tried it for the table. It has a "farinaceous" smell like linseed oil which I don't like, and it's quite uncommon in our mountains.

This one is growing under non-native Knobcone Pine (Pinus attenuata). I've only found it at this one place near our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch. I found it growing in the same place 35 years ago!

The real question is the name - why "Man on Horseback"? Mushroom author David Arora has a sense of humor, and calls another mushroom "Woman on a Motorcycle"!

One explanation comes from Yahoo! groups:
The most interesting part was not about bovinus at all, but about the derivation of the name "Man on Horseback"...apparently, back in medieval times, the peasants were "allowed" to gather Suillus bovinus as an edible mushroom; edible, but certainly not incredible. Tricholoma equestre though, a fantastically flavored and textured fungus, was reserved for medieval knights at table... the original "Men on Horseback!"

(I would take this - like the mushroom - with a grain of salt!)
Locality:
Pinus attenuata). I've only found it at this one place near our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch. I found it growing in the same place 35 years ago!

These pretty 'shrooms are growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods near an ancient Chumash rock-art site today. They are a little the worse for the dry weather we've been having, but still recognizable.

Notes:
68400063 This is a huge Manzanita Bolete (Leccinum manzanitae) mushroom that I found growing under a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree today. My knife is about 3 1/2 inches long, so this mushroom is dinner plate-size or larger!

You can see the distinctive dark scales ("scabers") on the stem of this mushroom. Like all Boletes, it has pores instead of gills under the cap. It's said to be edible, but not the best. I haven't tried it. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2012)

I posed this mushroom on the eroded sand nearby to get some sun, maybe a mistake - it looks too much like the beach.

I'm glad there are still some mysteries in my well-walked woods! (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2012)

It's the first day of the new year, so I decided to look back in time. I hiked out to Kinevan Ranch on the Pass, where we used to live 30 years ago. Despite the dry weather, I found mushrooms!

I recommend this is as something to do when it's too dry for mushrooms and too early for butterflies - just find a leafy plant and wait, and nature will arrive! I also got photos of a wasp or two, and more spiders.

File.Name: c103a_c103-004.jpg
Notes:
68407774 I had errands to do today, including having coffee with a good old friend I haven't seen for almost 10 years!

Coming home, I found this dead Broad-footed Mole (Scapanus latimanus) on the road by our mailbox. I don't know what killed it and left it here. I moved it off the road to a nearby gopher mound (seemed appropriate) for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2012)

(Gophers are herbivores which eat plants, and moles eat earthworms and other small invertebrates - but both can devastate gardens from below!)

Here you can see the mole's face and a digging foot. They are quite blind, but I think I can make out a circular patch where its eye should be. You can see its "vibrissae" or whiskers, which are specialized hairs to feel rather than see underground. They have wonderful fur like "moleskin" - that stuff you use to prevent blisters - with a "velvet-like texture of the pelage permitting broad-footed moles to move forward or backward in their tunnels with little friction". They are remarkable critters.

As a gardener, I have no regrets - they can take out a whole row of plants with one burrow! But as a naturalist, I find them fascinating native animals which are perfectly adapted to finding worms and other critters in the ground.

Doing some research, I found this great short video on the web of a naturalist actually catching a mole!
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/hottamale956/3536167975/
Locality: Gophers are herbivores which eat plants

In the summer, these spiders have bright flower colors for camouflage to capture pollinators. I'm not sure what these winter spiders are after, but there are tiny flies and gnats in the woods that have to perch somewhere.

I'm proud of this photo in natural light with perfect focus. Of course I took 20+ photos to get this one keeper! That tan color in the background is a sandstone outcrop.
