Notes:
68407655 Do you ever get the feeling you are being watched in the woods? It might be true!

I spotted this tiny Jumping Spider (Salticidae) looking right at me with its amazing eyes. I couldn't get closer without disturbing it, so this photo is a tight crop. Its looking out from a native shrub of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2012)

File.Name: c103e_c103-006.jpg
Notes:
68407585 This large bottle cap-sized Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae has made an egg case under the rain gutter on our roof. Here she is tending her eggs. Note the beautiful white "stars" she makes as anchors for the webbing. It might be a Cat-Faced Spider (Araneus gemmoides or kin). (San Marcos Pass, 3 January 2012)

The sun never shines under the rain gutters, and the sun rarely shines anywhere near our house this close to the solstice! I held up a white card to reflect some light to the spider. It worked, but only for a 15 minute window when the sun actually shines on our porch. I'm glad the days are now getting longer and the sun is getting higher!

File.Name: c103f_c102-014.jpg
Notes: 68407518 I found this handsome small Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a small Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree yesterday morning. I think I can see a "collar" in another photo, so it might be family Sphecidae, though I'm not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 2 January 2012)

File.Name: c104a_c104-003.jpg
Notes:
68411955 This is a remarkable tiny Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. I found it on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family above the retaining wall on our driveway this morning. If you look close, you can see its sucking proboscis folded under its head. It has a beautiful checkered pattern. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2012)

Like my [Previous] photo, I got this at 1:1 magnification in the shade, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The bug is about 633 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the bug is 23.6x633/4288 = 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. This bug is really tiny!

I expect these insects in summer. I had no idea they are active in winter.
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c104b_c104-025.jpg
Notes:
68411911 I noticed this tiny Mite (Acari) this morning on a blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on our driveway. Unlike spiders, they have only a single body part. This one is rather pretty! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2012)

I got this photo in deep shade - that's all there is at our house this close to the winter solstice.

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. It's a tight 1:1 crop. The mite is about 145 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the fly is 23.6x145/4288 = 0.8 mm. This mite is mighty tiny!

But look at that yellowish bump on the mite's back - is that an even tinier mite on the big one? I see a reddish area below it, like a tick bite. I tried to bring out the details, but I couldn't. I need forensic software!

Reminds me of the poem, originally by Jonathan Swift ("On Poetry: A Rhapsody"):
   "So, naturalists observe, a flea
   Has smaller fleas that on him prey;
   And these have smaller still to bite 'em,
   And so proceed ad infinitum.
ScientificName:
Acari; Bromus carinatus; So, naturalists observe, a flea
   Has smaller fleas that on him prey;
   And these have smaller still to bite 'em,
   And so proceed ad infinitum.
associatedTaxa:
Acari; Bromus carinatus; So, naturalists observe, a flea
   Has smaller fleas that on him prey;
   And these have smaller still to bite 'em,
   And so proceed ad infinitum.

File.Name: c104c_bc31-009.jpg
Notes: 68411735 I found this beautiful little diurnal (daytime) moth resting in the woods this morning. It was quiet and let me get close. I'm pretty sure it is Pyrausta dapalis in the Crambid Snout Moth family (Crambidae) of the order Lepidoptera. You can see its "snout". It has brilliant red "underwings" - but you only get a glimpse in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2012)

File.Name: c104d_c104-017.jpg
Notes:
68411645 I wouldn't want to meet this fly in a dark alley!

I found this dark fly (order Diptera) in the woods this morning at my usual place near the creek. It's a tough-looking critter, but it has amazing structural detail. I have to admit, I love photographing flies! (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2012) And look close - the fly is dusted with pollen from some flowers!

File.Name: c104e_c101-008.jpg
Notes:
68411562 This is another blue-gilled Cortinarius mushroom in the woods by our old cabin on Kinevan Ranch, taken on New Year's Day. I don't know what species this mushroom is, but Blue Cortinarius seems a fine name. I didn't break the stem. I found the mushroom like this, kicked over by a person or critter. I'm sure it's in the sub-genus Bulbopodium with a viscid cap and abrupt swollen base. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2012)

You can't see the signature "cortina" of cobwebby hairs in this photo, but there are signs of its presence. Look at those rusty-brown hairs on the stem and the edge of the cap. With practice, you get good at reading these subtle mushroom signs!

The problem is that all cortinarius mushrooms turn dingy brown with age and lose the brilliant colors. There are lots of species, which you can't tell apart. It's probably not edible.

File.Name: c104f_c101-010.jpg
Notes: 68411471 I guess these are beautiful Ivory Waxy Cap (Hygrophorus eburneus) mushrooms in the woods. Neither caps nor stems are slimy-viscid, though both have debris stuck to them which indicates that maybe they once were viscid. This dry weather we've been having makes mushroom identification difficult! I found this mushroom on my New Year's Day hike to Kinevan Ranch on the Pass, near our old cabin. (San Marcos Pass, 1 January 2012)

File.Name: c105a_c105-051.jpg
Notes:
68415992 I guess this is a large Western Leaf-footed Bug (Leptoglossus clypealis) in the family Coreidae of the order Hemiptera. You can see the leaf-like enlargements on its hind legs. I found it on an enormous flower stalk of (I believe) Agave attenuata at the beach today. (Goleta Beach, 5 January 2012)

Julie has been in Portland with the grandkids this past week. I had some time to kill before she arrived home at the airport today, so I went to Goleta Beach and found this interesting insect. I mostly keep my photos to local insects (etc.) on San Marcos Pass, but I couldn't resist!
ScientificName: Leptoglossus clypealis; Coreidae; Hemiptera; Agave attenuata
associatedTaxa: Leptoglossus clypealis; Coreidae; Hemiptera; Agave attenuata

File.Name: c105b_c105-040.jpg
Notes:
68415945 Here is a small Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) burrowing into a new flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I waited five minutes for it to come out, but it didn't! (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2012)

It already feels like spring with temperatures near 80. Beautiful, but it's not right for January. I want more rain to bring up the mushrooms again!

File.Name: c105c_c105-031.jpg
Notes:
68415911 These are gorgeous new flowers of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti var. xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family, with some backlight. I've seen the flowers before in the woods, but these were the first I've found standing up for a photo. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2012)

These flowers are "shakers" - the pollen-bearing stamens clasp the female style, and pollen falls out as wind and critters jiggle the flowers.

File.Name: c105d_c105-022.jpg
Notes: 68415886 These are the female pistillate flowers of a native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. I showed the male flowers in my [Previous] photos, which release pollen. These female flowers will catch the pollen on their little styles to make fruit. They are more discrete than the male flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2012)

File.Name: c105e_c105-037.jpg
Notes:
68415839 This is looking closer at the "string of pearls" flower catkins of a native male (staminate) Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. The individual "flowers" appear to come from "decussately connate bracts" - I think that means they come from little cups. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2012)

These are striking flowers in our mountains. They would make good garden plants.

File.Name: c105f_c105-025.jpg
Notes:
68415770 These are the "string of pearls" flower catkins of a native male (staminate) Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. The strange flowers hang like pearls on a thread. (San Marcos Pass, 5 January 2012)

This native shrub is "dioecious" with separate male and female plants. The flowers of this particular shrub will only produce pollen. They appear to be wind-pollinated, at least they release pollen when I shake the plant. They are striking, and would make good garden plants.

File.Name: c106a_c106-004.jpg
Notes:
68419559 This is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's cute! You can see the real fly underneath its thick hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2012)

They're like roosters in the hen yard - ready to jump on anything that comes along! They are hard to approach with camera and tripod. I've been trying all week to get a photo.

File.Name: c106b_c106-013.jpg
Notes: 68419512 I found another cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family today. This is the one with a golden scalp. It's remarkable to make eye contact with anything so small! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2012)

File.Name: c106c_c106-016.jpg
Notes:
68419486 This must be a different little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family today. Look at those almost-black "chelicerae" - the tooth-like jaws of the spider! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2012)

I found this little spider on the same plant as the spider in my [Next] photo.

File.Name: c106d_c106-007.jpg
Notes:
68419376 This is a handsome little spider in the woods. I'm pretty sure it's a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae). It has an abdomen shaped like a bottle gourd! (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2012)

Many Orb-Weaver Spiders make a stabilimentum - those bands of heavy silk through the center of the web. These little spiders add the bodies of their prey.
CommonNames: Garbage Line Spider; Orb-Weaver Spiders; Orb-Weaver Spiders

File.Name: c106e_c106-032.jpg
Notes:
68419187 There are a few new mushrooms in the woods, but not many because of the dry weather we've been having. I think I recognize this 'shroom as a Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus). They have purple colors and a "slime veil" that leaves bands of slime behind on the stem - only visible here as a faint banding in the sand grains adhering to the stem. (San Marcos Pass, 6 January 2012)

See my photo of the same mushroom from two years ago:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/58456334

File.Name: c106f_c105-056.jpg
Notes:
68419057 There are the enormous flower stalks of (I believe) Agave attenuata at the beach yesterday. I found the Western Leaf-footed Bug in my [Previous] photo on the unopened flower buds of one of these stalks. I can walk under the stalks, though I have to hunch down a bit. (Goleta Beach, 5 January 2012)

Our dear old friend David used to call them "Flacid Boojums" and the name is stuck in memory!

File.Name: c107a_c107-009.jpg
Notes:
68423248 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the woods today. This early-flowering perennial native has red and green leaves. The red colors are mostly leafy bracts, though if you look close you can see a few real flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2011)

This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. It comes up mushroom-like, ready to flower.

The flower heads of these Indian Warrior plants are sold on the Internet "legal high" market. The effects are described on one web site as "tranquilizers, muscle relaxants, powerful aphrodesiacs, and sedatives", and they sell for quite a bit.

I'd rather enjoy the flowers. This "wildcrafting" of native plants - especially these interesting root-parasites that can't be cultivated - bothers me!
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. It comes up mushroom-like

File.Name: c107b_c107-042.jpg
Notes:
68423229 There's a lot happening in this photo!

Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a new flower of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. There's also a tiny Thrip (order Thysanoptera) on the lowest flower. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

More interesting is that some little critter has been boring holes through the sides of these flowers to get to the nectar inside before they open. They make nice windows to look inside the flowers - like those candy "Easter eggs" with a scene hidden inside!

This is the other manzanita in our mountains. The Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) usually flowers first. They have similar urn-like flowers. Eastwood Manzanita grows from a burl, but not Big-berry Manzanita.
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) usually flowers first. They have similar urn-like flowers. Eastwood Manzanita grows from a burl
CommonNames: Honey Bee; Eastwood Manzanita; Thrip; Big-berry Manzanita
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Thysanoptera; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: c107c_c107-034.jpg
Notes:
68423194 This little black fly (order Diptera) is working new flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family. I have no idea what kind of fly it is. It looks pretty ordinary, but this is an interesting place to find a fly, well off the beaten path. The fly has its proboscis fully engaged in the flower. I suspect it's an interesting fly. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

This is the other manzanita in our mountains. The Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) usually flowers first. They have similar urn-like flowers. Eastwood Manzanita grows from a burl, but not Big-berry Manzanita.
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) usually flowers first. They have similar urn-like flowers. Eastwood Manzanita grows from a burl
ScientificName: Diptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae; Arctostaphylos glauca

File.Name: c107d_c107-020.jpg
Notes:
68423144 These are beautiful new Russula emetica (or kin) mushrooms in the woods growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. The deep red caps are unmistakable. I went to pick the smaller mushroom, and the stem snapped like chalk - the classic Russala test. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener", and it checks out on Wikipedia - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our more beautiful creatures!

File.Name: c107e_c107-019.jpg
Notes: 68423092 It's been dry enough to dry enough to split open the caps of these Russula mushrooms in the woods - but there are still new mushrooms to be found in spite of the drought. I snapped a stem of one of these mushrooms - the Russala test - and they break like chalk. See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

File.Name: c107f_c107-051.jpg
Notes:
68423019 These are LBMs - "little brown mushrooms" - growing from moss on the ground today. I have no idea what kind they are, but moss banks are a specialized environment, and I suspect these are specialized mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

Note the tiny water drops on the moss. They can't be raindrops as we've had no rain and little dew in a month. Mosses are bryophytes - non-vascular plants - so I don't think the water drops can be from guttation. I don't understand.

File.Name: c108a_c107-027.jpg
Notes:
68426593 This is a beautiful fresh-looking Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom in the woods today. The deep red caps are unmistakable. That red berry of Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family under the cap - like a little apple - is a perfect accent. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2012)

For more info about the mushroom, see my photo yesterday at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68423144

Our dry La NiÒa weather continues. Last year we had 24.5 inches of rain by now; this year we've had just 5.7 inches. We're running at about 50% normal. It's bad for mushrooms and plants, but maybe a dry year after a wet year will be good for the butterflies? Whatever happens, this will be an interesting year!
ScientificName: Russula emetica; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; La NiÒa
associatedTaxa: Russula emetica; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; La NiÒa

File.Name: c108b_c108-007.jpg
Notes:
68426533 There are still Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms in the woods, though these are rock-hard from the dry weather we've been having for the last month. They are edible and delicious when fresh, but I don't know about these old specimens. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2012)

Usually we cook the water out of chanterelles so they can "stew in their own juices". Would it be enough to re-hydrate these dry ones with a soak??

File.Name: c108c_c107-022.jpg
Notes: 68426454 I found this surprisingly solid and juicy Milky Cap (Lactarius) mushroom in the dry woods yesterday. I slit the gills with my knife, and you can see the white latex it contains. The cap is tan colored and feels moist. I don't know what kind of Lactarius it is. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

File.Name: c108d_c107-005.jpg
Notes:
68426269 Maybe this is a Panther Amanita (Amanita pantherina) coming up in the woods? It's been so dry for the last month that it's hard to tell. You can see the white spore deposit under the cap. This anamita has a fungus infection that obscures the base, so I couldn't see much of the volva. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

This mushroom is quite poisonous. It contains the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. It should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.

File.Name: c108e_c107-053.jpg
Notes:
68426196 I thought these "Satanic Bolete" () mushrooms were all done for the year, but here is another fresh one. I found this growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees in the woods yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)

There were many of these mushrooms in November. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68205712

These mushrooms are quite poisonous to you and me, though I often find them nibbled by critters. The symptoms include vomiting, diarrhea, and severe cramps. Don't eat red-spored Boletes!

File.Name: c108f_c106-001.jpg
Notes:
68426129 This is a sad-looking "cluster" of new Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods. It's not much of a cluster. As our woods continue to dry out, the mushrooms are having a hard time - but they are still trying! (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2012)

Dry weather continues. Last year we had 24.5 inches of rain by now; this year we've had just 5.7 inches. We're running at about 50% normal. It's bad for mushrooms and plants, but maybe a dry year after a wet year will be good for the butterflies? Whatever happens, this will be an interesting year!

File.Name: c109a_c109-028.jpg
Notes:
68430672 This is a pretty little Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly in the winter woods today. It really is blue on its upper wings! I captured this one on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia). It's our earliest butterfly and it's already missing a chunk of its wing from a bird attack. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2012)

I've been chasing this butterfly all year - at least since my December 31 photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68397062

Our dry weather continues - not good for mushrooms and plants, but not bad for insects. I'm starting a "mushroom hunting" elective for Dunn Middle School on Wednesday. It will be pretty slim pickings, but we'll find a few.

File.Name: c109b_c109-030.jpg
Notes:
68430598 Here is our first wildflower of the year! This is native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a perennial mustard growing in a place where the winter sun never shines. I had to fiddle with the white balance, and it's still not quite what I saw. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2012)

It has an edible root (the "tooth"), but I really don't care to dig it up. It tastes like a radish, not worth the pretty flowers.

File.Name: c109c_c109-021.jpg
Notes:
68430500 I found another furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's cute, like a little flying mouse. It has a remarkable stiff proboscis. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2012)

They're like roosters in the hen yard - ready to jump on anything that comes along! They are hard to approach with camera and tripod. I sure they are really looking to "hook up" with another bee fly.

File.Name: c109d_c109-022.jpg
Notes:
68430431 I found this different Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera on a stem of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the woods today. It's twice the size of the leafhopper in my [Previous] photo, but it's still pretty small. It looks so much like a little Cicada - in the same insect order, but much larger. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2012)

Browsing at BugGuide, I think this might be genus Gyponana or Rugosana. See this photo:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/398626/bgimage
ScientificName: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Gyponana; Rugosana
associatedTaxa: Cicadellidae; Homoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Gyponana; Rugosana

File.Name: c109e_c109-016.jpg
Notes:
68430313 This is a tiny Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera. I believe it is the "Blue-green Sharpshooter" (Graphocephala atropunctata) insect. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2012)

It's grooming its wings. Is that why it has such spiny hind legs?? The real stumper is how do they bend their legs like that! (As Bart Simpson would say, "I could do that but I don't wanna!")

File.Name: c109f_c109-005.jpg
Notes:
68430218 I'm pretty sure this is a small Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera). They have speckled wings and amazing iridescent eyes. I got this photo in contrasty light this morning, and only had this one chance before it flew away. It's resting on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) shrub in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2012)

These little flies may gall the flowers of this plant. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/60719971

File.Name: c110a_c107-047.jpg
Notes:
68434765 This must be Honey Bee heaven!

Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working a new flower of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa ssp. mollis) in the Ericaceae plant family a few days ago. The bees love these flowers, and seem to get a bit tipsy from the nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 7 January 2012)
ScientificName: Apis mellifera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Apis mellifera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; mollis; Ericaceae

File.Name: c110b_c110-007.jpg
Notes:
68434727 These are new flowers of Sticky Snapdragon (Antirrhinum multiflorum) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family - in January! (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2012)

It's another fire-follower plant in our mountains, though this one is growing in a place that was "masticated" - mechanically cleared for a fire break - a few years ago. For some of these fire-followers, it's enough to clear the ground.

But what's it doing with flowers NOW in January? I've found these flowers before in March, but most of my photos are from June and July. Our unseasonably warm and dry weather lately is screwing up the native plants. I hope the woods aren't broken.

File.Name: c110c_c109-004.jpg
Notes:
68434641 Here are the remarkably intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and strange, related to sassafras and avocado. (San Marcos Pass, 9 January 2012)

I think I can make out a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with six orange glands at the base, finally surrounded by a circle of six stamens. Each of the fat stamens seems to have two or four anthers. Like I said, they are remarkably intricate.

File.Name: c110d_c108-021.jpg
Notes:
68434483 Here is a brilliant white flower cluster of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2012)

The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! The delicate purple 3-parted styles are beautiful. They have a shiny "floral disk" at the base of each flower that serves as a nectar gland. In these flowers the disk is rich blue, but in other plants of the same species they are yellow.

File.Name: c110e_c108-012.jpg
Notes: 68434404 These are new flowers of native Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Fabaceae) plant family. They are pretty fire follower plants in a place that burned a few years ago in the Gap Fire. The plant grows large, several feet tall. With age, the flowers look quite red. (San Marcos Pass, 8 January 2012)

File.Name: c110f_c105-014.jpg
Notes:
68434308 These are ripe berries of native Toyon aka California Holly (as in Hollywood), Heteromeles arbutifolia in the Rosaceae plant family. They are having a good year - which is also good for the birds that depend on them. (San Marcos Pass, 10 January 2012)

If you look closer than I did when I took this photo, you might notice the green "stink bug" in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera near the center-top. I'm sorry I didn't see it until later.

The raw berries are astringent - they dry your mouth out - but they are tasty when cooked and were a native American Chumash staple. I've made a sweet Toyon cider by pouring hot water over the berries and letting it sit, like tea.
ScientificName: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Chumash
associatedTaxa: Heteromeles arbutifolia; Rosaceae; Pentatomidae; Hemiptera; Chumash

File.Name: c111a_c111-001.jpg
Notes:
68438921 I was scouting for my first "Introduction to Mushroom Hunting" class for Dunn Middle School kids this morning, and I found this handsome cluster of Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) growing from the stump of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the woods. The mushrooms are cracked and scarred by the dry weather we've been having. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2012)

It's interesting - as the woods gets drier, these Honey Mushrooms still appear on schedule, but the clusters get smaller.

I did my class today, followed by a (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2012)board meeting. I'm tired. Three photos will do fine today.

File.Name: c111b_c111-009.jpg
Notes:
68438887 I did my first "Introduction to Mushroom Hunting" class for a fun group of Dunn Middle School students this afternoon. We're at a disadvantage because there are few mushrooms in the woods right now - it's been so dry with no rain for a month. I brought the kids to our house in the mountains where I know there still are some mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 11 January 2012)

More or less from left to right, we found: brown-spored Inocybes, Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea), lots of dry Chanterelles (Cantharellus cibarius), a Cortinarius, a bunch of pink-spored Entoloma mushrooms - and a few oddballs, including a Bolete infested with Hypomyces chrysospermum and an Earthstar.

Not a bad foray for dry weather with no mushrooms!

(I'm not sure if the Chanterelles are still edible. Usually we have to cook the water out of the mushrooms, but this time we'll have to add water! I'm not sure I want to try them.)
Locality: I'm not sure if the Chanterelles are still edible.  Usually we have to cook the water out of the mushrooms

These drosophilid flies are having a tough year. There are still Honey Mushrooms in the woods, but they are coming up so dry from our lack of rain that the maggots can't develop. Is this how a crash in an insect population happens? What will happen next year?

File.Name: c113e_c112-057.jpg
Notes:
68446084 We did a little experiment last night for dinner, and discovered that dried-out Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms taste just fine! That's good to know, and I guess it's not surprising as the chanterelles in local markets look about the same. We added the really dry ones to the compost, and cooked up the rest. Delicious! (San Marcos Pass, 12 January 2012)

Chanterelle mushrooms are so tasty to us, but it's interesting how nothing else in the woods eats them - including critters like mice and woodrats, flies, and other parasitic fungi. Do they know something we should know?

These are the mushrooms we found on my first "Introduction to Mushroom Hunting" class with a fun group of Dunn Middle School students. Sorry kids, I didn't know how to share them. See my photo at:

File.Name: c115c_c113-035.jpg
Notes:
68453054 This photo shows the whole plant of a remarkable native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the woods today. This early-flowering perennial native has leafy red bracts, though if you look close you can see a few real flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2011)

This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. It comes up mushroom-like, ready to flower.

The flower heads of these Indian Warrior plants are sold on the Internet "legal high" market. The effects are described on one web site as "tranquilizers, muscle relaxants, powerful aphrodesiacs, and sedatives". They sell for quite a bit.

I'd rather enjoy the flowers. This "wildcrafting" of native plants - especially these interesting root-parasites that can't be cultivated - bothers me!

File.Name: c118a_c107-002.jpg
Notes:
68464909 When there's nothing else to photograph in the woods, there are always galls!

This is another strange insect gall growing on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family. It might be the gall of the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera, but I'm not sure. (San Marcos Pass, 13 January 2012)

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.

It's been a tough time for getting new photos every day, but I've been trying. It's too dry for mushrooms and flowers, and too early for most insects. But there is a good chance of real rain on Friday or Saturday, which will return everything to normal. Bring it on!

68472960 This is a tiny fly (order Diptera) perched on a grass blade above the retaining wall along our driveway, where the sun never shines at this time of year. It's only about 1/4 inch (6 mm) long. There seems to be a problem with it's footing! It almost looks like a tiny Crane Fly in the family Tipulidae. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

Fotolog went down last night, so I'm adding a couple of photos on Saturday morning. We did get some much-needed rain last night! We received about 1.5 inches, better than I expected, but we're still only 46% of normal.

I hope the rain brings back the mushrooms! On the other hand, it's been a fun challenge trying to capture these tiny insects in the woods that I probably would never have noticed if there were mushrooms and new wildflowers out.

File.Name: c120b_c120-031.jpg
Notes: 68472893 Here is a gnarly-looking dark fly (order Diptera) peacefully working new flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It seems to be gathering nectar from the deep blue "nectar disks" in the base of the flowers. You never know where these flies have been! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

File.Name: c120c_c120-023.jpg
Notes:
68471585 This is a tiny Beetle (order Coleoptera) perched on a leaf of my favorite Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek this morning. I'm not sure what kind of beetle it is - maybe a Weevil (Curculionidae) without a snout? (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

I think I've found this beetle before. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68346715

File.Name: c121a_c121-017.jpg
Notes:
68474675 Banana Slug weather again!
Slugs have teeth?

We had 1-1/2 inches of rain last night, and the large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slugs (Ariolimax columbianus) are already out and about. This one was crawling along when it ran into a grass plant - and developed "teeth" to munch on it! They must hide in the ground during the dry spells, though I've never uncovered one. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2012)

There's nothing like a good rain in a drought year to instantly bring life back to the woods. And the forecast is for more rain tomorrow night!

My photos today divide between wet (the first three) and dry (the last three) - today and yesterday. Don't miss my photo of the remarkable tiny jumping spider at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68474444

File.Name: c121b_c121-007.jpg
Notes: 68474591 This is a tiny caterpillar (Lepidoptera) trying to look like a Moss (Bryophyta) stem in the woods today. What an instant difference a bit of rain makes after six dry weeks without! (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2012)

File.Name: c121c_c121-005.jpg
Notes:
68474538 These are interesting small Liverworts (Marchantiophyta) showing their umbrella-like sporophytes for sexual reproduction. They are growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. It's remarkable that these liverworts looked shrunken and black yesterday. They revive so quickly after a rain! They are real "resurrection plants". (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2012)

They might be the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know.

File.Name: c121d_c120-020.jpg
Notes:
68474444 Here is the tiniest Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that I've ever managed to photograph. It's hugging the spine of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf. These are my usual measuring stick for small critters in the woods. I guess the spine is about 1 mm long, and the spider looks about that wide. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

Let's see if I'm right. I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The spider's body is about 180 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. (It's a very tight crop.) So the size of the spider is 23.6x180/4288 = 0.99 mm, a bit over 1/32 inch. Like I said, this spider is tiny!

I took about 60 photos of this tiny spider to get this one keeper!
Locality: It's a very tight crop.)  So the size of the spider is 23.6x180/4288 = 0.99 mm

File.Name: c121e_c120-012.jpg
Notes:
68474346 This is another remarkable fly (order Diptera) perched on the leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) yesterday morning. It's quite small, though structurally perfect. The gray colors and red eyes are attractive. I won't try to guess what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

This fly is really small. I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The fly's body is about 800 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x800/4288 = 4.4 mm, a bit under 3/16 inch. Like I said, this fly is small!

File.Name: c121f_c120-009.jpg
Notes:
68474206 This is a handsome fly (order Diptera) perched on the leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree yesterday morning. I guess it is the same kind of dark-winged Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae that usually flock to the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods. It has an impressive raised scutellum. It looks like a public statue! (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

The last three of today's photos should have gone up yesterday, but Fotolog was down last night. Just as well - it's been very windy today, hard to get photos.
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Drosophilid Fly; Honey Mushrooms
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Drosophilidae; Armillariella mellea
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Drosophilidae; Armillariella mellea

File.Name: c122a_c122-004.jpg
Notes:
68478025 I wouldn't expect new mushrooms a day after the first new rain in weeks, but here they are! This is a Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom growing under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains. There are also Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2012)

Despite the Italian name, the Coccora is only known from the west coast of the US. It has a thick "volva" - the cup at the base of the stem - and an equally thick volval patch on the cap. This mushroom is new, but it must have come from an already-formed "amanita egg" in the ground. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66476123

This mushroom is said to be edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is a distinctive mushroom that I should try. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't encouraging.

It's starting to drizzle as our next storm moves in tonight. The National Weather Service predicts another 1-2 inches of rain, just what we need to keep these mushrooms going!
CommonNames: Coccora; Fir; Coast Live Oak; amanita egg
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita

File.Name: c122b_c122-002.jpg
Notes:
68477975 I first thought this was the beautiful but deadly Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom, but looking closer at the cap on my computer screen, I realize it's an edible Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom. Not surprising - it was growing a foot away from another Amanita calyptrata in my [Next] photo. It's OK if I confuse a poisonous mushroom with an edible one! (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2012)

These Cocorra mushrooms have a thick white volval patch on the cap - which is barely visible here. The Italian name means cocoon, but you can't see any resemblance here. See my [Next] photo.

File.Name: c122c_c122-009.jpg
Notes:
68477934 These are pretty new flowers of the strange native Dodder plant - probably Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae family. It's an orange twining parasitic plant that grows on other plants in the woods. This one is growing on native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2012)

Is it odd that it's flowering now? I think of these plants flowering in mid-summer, but I really don't pay them much attention.

See my old photo from 25 July 2008:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/38384686
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae
Families: Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae

File.Name: c122d_c121-010.jpg
Notes:
68477886 I found this a "new" Cortinarius mushroom in the woods yesterday - on the morning after our rain last night in a place I'd checked a few days before. Mushrooms are fast, but not that fast! It must have been in the ground already, and the rain washed away the leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2012)

It has rusty-brown gills, remnants of "cortina" hairs on the stem, and a bulbous base. I'm sure it's in the sub-genus Bulbopodium, but I'm not sure which species.

File.Name: c122e_c121-011.jpg
Notes:
68477855 These are strange Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica) mushrooms growing on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) on the ground. They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. They have a remarkable ability to revive after a rain. It rained overnight, and here they are in the morning. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2012)

The other fungus in the photo is a resupinate Crust Fungus - some kind of the genus Stereum. My book says that "Most Tremellas are frequently found with Stereum, suggesting some kind of relationship between the two.
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak; Crust Fungus

File.Name: c122f_c121-001.jpg
Notes:
68477819 Here is another hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) prowling through the woods yesterday. They're 6-8 inches long! This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains, and we only see them after rains. These slugs have two pairs of 'feelers' or tentacles on their head. The upper pair have eye spots, while the lower pair provides a tactile sense and smell. (San Marcos Pass, 21 January 2012)

Did you ever kiss a banana slug? Don't - they are coated in slime like rubber cement!

File.Name: c123a_c123-001.jpg
Notes:
68481315 This is a beautiful new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the canyon, with raindrops. It's almost the first flower I've seen this year. The flowers remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2012)

Today was a rain day, and my photos are all rain photos - mostly taken in the rain. We got just over 0.8 inches - a bit less than forecast, but still appreciated. It was a good soaker, just what the woods need.

File.Name: c123b_c123-013.jpg
Notes: 68481247 These are new flower buds of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family with raindrops from our rain today. You can see the woods reflected in the raindrops! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2012)

File.Name: c123c_c123-009.jpg
Notes: 68481221 These are new flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family with raindrops from our rain today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2012)

File.Name: c123d_c123-005.jpg
Notes: 68481148 This is a strange Jelly Fungus (class Heterobasidiomycetes) growing on a pencil-sized broken twig in the woods with mosses and lichens. These are all small creatures that come to life during a rain, but disappear soon afterwards. I love exploring this micro-world with my camera. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2012)

File.Name: c123e_c123-006.jpg
Notes: 68481043 This is an odd white fungus erupting from a pencil-sized twig of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia), with other mosses and lichens. (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2012)

File.Name: c123f_c123-015.jpg
Notes: 68481003 Here is a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family with raindrops from our rain today. The drops are reflecting the woods, and maybe our house. These stems hold the raindrops like nobody's business! (San Marcos Pass, 23 January 2012)

File.Name: c124a_c124-036.jpg
Notes:
68485137 What a difference a rain makes! This a large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) crawling over a yummy new Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushroom in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2012)

I admit this photo is a setup. I moved the slug about two feet to the mushroom for a photo. I think banana slugs actually prefer green plants, see my photos:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68474675
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/57814328

But also see:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/66064199

Today was clear and beautiful, and the winds never come up. I was hoping for more mushrooms, but they are starting.

File.Name: c124b_c124-019.jpg
Notes:
68485050 These are perfect Panther Amanitas (Amanita pantherina) coming up in the woods under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) today. Note the brown caps with white "stars" that are the remnants of the universal veil that also leaves a cup or volva at the base of the stem. You can also see the ring or partial veil on the stems. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2012)

Many of the Amanita pantherina mushrooms in our woods show signs of crossing with A. gemmata, but these look like the real thing.

These mushrooms are quite poisonous. They contain the same interesting toxins as A. muscaria, but in higher concentrations. It should not be used for recreational purposes. My book says it's one of the most common causes of mushroom poisoning in the Pacific Northwest.
ScientificName: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; A. muscaria
associatedTaxa: Amanita pantherina; Quercus agrifolia; Amanita pantherina; A. gemmata; A. muscaria

File.Name: c124c_c124-007.jpg
Notes: 68484939 Here is a handsome Crab Spider (Thomisidae) perched beneath a flower cluster on a leaf of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. I like its tiny claws! This is the only spider or insect I saw today after our rain yesterday. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2012)

File.Name: c124d_c124-021.jpg
Notes:
68484834 No plant in our woods holds raindrops like the native perennial Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - though the Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the same family comes close. There's a large drop in the center of the palmate leaf acting like a magnifying glass. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2012)

I wonder why some leaves hold rain drops so well, and others shed them so fast? It's a stumper!

File.Name: c124e_c124-012.jpg
Notes: 68484740 These are leaflets of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family still holding raindrops from the rain yesterday. I wonder why some leaves hold rain drops so well, and others shed them so fast? See my [Next] photo for another native plant that holds onto raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2012)

File.Name: c124f_c124-004.jpg
Notes:
68484681 This is another beautiful new flower of Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the canyon. The glandular flowers remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 24 January 2012)

I got this photo in a beam of sunlight in the otherwise dark woods this morning. t's important not to over-expose these flowers. This photo with backlighting is two stops less than my camera thought was right.

File.Name: c125a_c125-005.jpg
Notes:
68490264 This may be the tiniest critter I've ever tried to photograph. It's a tiny Mite (Acari) on a blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on our driveway. Unlike spiders, they have only a single body part. This one is rather pretty. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2012)

If you look close, it has a pair of even tinier yellow mites on its back. Reminds me of the poem, originally by Jonathan Swift ("On Poetry: A Rhapsody", 1733):
   "So, naturalists observe, a flea
   Has smaller fleas that on him prey;
   And these have smaller still to bite 'em,
   And so proceed ad infinitum."

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. This mite's body is about 160 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So its size is 23.6x160/4288 = 0.9 mm, a bit more than 1/32 inch. The mites on it's back are only 22 pixels across = 0.12 mm across!

It's unnaturally warm tonight, in the 60s. Just what we don't need - warm winds to reduce humidity and dry out the woods after 2+ inches of rain last weekend!

File.Name: c125b_c125-008.jpg
Notes:
68489052 Here is a tiny Planthopper insect in the order Homoptera. I guess it's an Issid Planthopper - family Issidae of the superfamily Fulgoroidea. It's perched for the moment on a leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus), but it soon hopped away from my too-close camera lens. Like all my photos today, I got this shot above the retaining wall along our driveway, where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2012)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. This planthopper's body is about 370 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So its size is 23.6x370/4288 = 2 mm, less than 3/32 inch. Like I said, it's tiny!

File.Name: c125c_c125-014.jpg
Notes: 68489025 This is a small (not tiny) Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) resting on a grass stem this morning. Like all my photos today, I got this shot above the retaining wall along our driveway, where the sun never shines at this time of year. I didn't notice the magnification, so I can't calculate the ant's size. (San Marcos Pass, 25 January 2012)

File.Name: c125d_c122-001.jpg
Notes:
68488979 This is another tiny aphid (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) perched on a dead grass stem above the retaining wall along our driveway, where the sun never shines at this time of year. It has beautiful blue eyesIt has beautiful blue eyes with a "pupil spot" which means the eyes are well-developed - but why would an aphid have such good eyes? (San Marcos Pass, 22 January 2012)

It has the same "swept-back wings" as another of my "tiny aphid" photos, but this one looks quite different:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68449780

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. This aphid's body is about 250 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the aphid is 23.6x250/4288 = 1.4 mm, less than 1/16 inch. Like I said, it's tiny!

File.Name: c125e_c120-005.jpg
Notes:
68488934 I guess this is a tiny aphid (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) - or maybe a tiny Hemiptera nymph? - walking across a grass blade. The grass is probably native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) in this place. I got this photo above the retaining wall along our driveway, where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. This critter's body is about 235 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x235/4288 = 1.3 mm, less than 1/16 inch. Like I said, it's tiny!

File.Name: c125f_c120-003.jpg
Notes:
68488891 I found this tiny Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a leaf above the retaining wall along our driveway, where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

I got all of today's photos along this wall. It's a convenient height to look for and photograph tiny insects, my window into the woods. I often start my photo day here.

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The wasp's body is about 500 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the wasp is 23.6x500/4288 = 2.8 mm, less than 1/8 inch. Like I said, this wasp is tiny!

File.Name: c126a_c126-004.jpg
Notes:
68492516 This a gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera. It looks like it's guarding the flowers of this native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree, but I'm sure it's waiting for prey. (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2012)

Maybe this is the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema)? See the photos at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/7900/bgimage

We're having another unnaturally warm night, in the 60s, after a near 80 high today. Just what we don't need - warm winds to reduce humidity and dry out the woods. It's a tough time for the mushrooms.

File.Name: c126b_c126-015.jpg
Notes: 68492314 This is another pretty little Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly taking moisture from a still-wet sandstone rock outcrop in the canyon. It's our plainest "Blue" butterfly below, but it is deep blue above. Of course it usually folds its wings up when it perches - just to frustrate us photographers? (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2012)

File.Name: c126c_c126-010.jpg
Notes: 68492242 I found this strange spider stretched out on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) today. My, what long legs it has! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2012)

File.Name: c126d_c126-011.jpg
Notes: 68492191 This is another attractive Crab Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree today. These spiders are cute and adorable. You can't sneak up on them. Even when they aren't looking right at you, they are looking right at you! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2012)

File.Name: c126e_c120-018.jpg
Notes:
68492119 This is another photo of the smallest Crab Spider (Salticidae) I've ever noticed, from a few days ago. I have another photo of the same spider at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68474444

In that photo I figured the size of this spider as 0.99 mm. Here it is hiding under the cupped leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 20 January 2012)

File.Name: c126f_c126-013.jpg
Notes: 68491976 I found this handsome little Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree today. It's tiny. It seems to have dark eye spots front and back! (San Marcos Pass, 26 January 2012)

File.Name: c127a_c127-008.jpg
Notes: 68496081 This is one of our tiniest wildflowers - native Common Peppergrass (Lepidium nitidum) in the Brassicaceae plant family. I put my pen in the photo for scale, with a 0.7 mm ball. The flower petals are less than 2 mm long. The notched seed pods are much larger than the flowers. True to it's name, the seeds have a pleasant spicy taste. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2012) *Sigh* It's been breezy today, with more drying wind.

File.Name: c127b_c127-015.jpg
Notes:
68495993 These are sweet-smelling white flowers of native Buck Brush (Ceanothus cuneatus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family just starting to open. The individual flowers are tiny, but elegant - and there are zillions of them! The delicate purple 3-parted styles are beautiful. They have opposite leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2012)

The individual flowers are remarkable up close. They have a shiny "floral disk" at the base of each flower that serves as a nectar gland. In these flowers the disk is yellow, but in other plants of the same species it is blue. The flower buds are remarkable too.

It's interesting that these are a dominate shrub on the north-facing slopes of the mountains, but they are replaced by Ceanothus megacarpus on the south-facing slopes with alternate leaves. They look almost identical.

You can make soap from the flowers - a handful of flowers will make suds when you rub them in water!

File.Name: c127c_c127-004.jpg
Notes: 68495928 This is a fully-open flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family - as open as they ever get, like one of those candy "Easter eggs" that you look into. The flowers are the size of an avocado seed. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The foliage is as handsome as the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 27 January 2012)

File.Name: c127d_c127-022.jpg
Notes: 68495858 I found this beautiful red Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom in the woods today growing under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful creatures! (San Marcos Pass, 27 January 2012)

File.Name: c127e_c125-027.jpg
Notes: 68495816 This is a Banded Cortinarius (Cortinarius collinitus) mushroom growing in a mixed forest of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees. It has the remains of a universal slime veil on the cap and stem, and a slime layer covering the partial veil under the gills - which is just tearing free in this specimen. It's not something you would want to eat! (Nojoqui Falls County Park, 25 January 2012)
CommonNames: Banded Cortinarius; Coast Live Oak; California Bay
ScientificName: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica
associatedTaxa: Cortinarius collinitus; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica

File.Name: c127f_c125-033.jpg
Notes:
68495705 I guess this is what mushroom-author David Arora (Mushrooms Demystified) calls a BUM - a "Boring Ubiquitous Mushroom". I ran it through his long key to the genus Cortinarius and came up with the Brown Cortinariius (Cortinarius laniger) - but I don't believe it, and he has a host of stand-ins for this species.

It's a good sized mushroom that was growing in a cluster under mixed woods of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and California Bay (Umbellularia californica) trees. It has silky white remnants of an universal veil on the cap, and rusty-brown hairs as remnants of the partial veil on the stem. Edibility is unknown - do not experiment! (Nojoqui Falls County Park, 25 January 2012)

The problem with Cortinarius mushrooms is that there are so many different kinds, and they all look the same as they get older. Leave them be.

I took my "Introduction to Mushroom Hunting" class to Nojoqui Falls on Wednesday, and we did find lots of mushrooms, including a basketful of yummy chantrelles!
CommonNames: Brown Cortinariius; Coast Live Oak; California Bay
ScientificName: Mushrooms Demystified; Cortinarius; Cortinarius laniger; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica; Cortinarius
associatedTaxa: Mushrooms Demystified; Cortinarius; Cortinarius laniger; Quercus agrifolia; Umbellularia californica; Cortinarius

File.Name: c128a_c128-008.jpg
Notes:
68499308 Here is a tiny mosquito-like fly (order Diptera) on a new leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. Look - she's ovipositing (laying her eggs) on the plant stem! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2012)

I got this photo (and the previous three) above the retaining wall on our driveway where the sun never shines at this time of year. It's a convenient height to look for and photograph tiny insects, my window into the woods - though it's really terrible light for macro photography. I got this photo at 1/20 second@f/8@ISO 400. I often start my photo day here, and sometimes never leave.
Locality: and the previous three) above the retaining wall on our driveway where the sun never shines at this time of year.  It's a convenient height to look for and photograph tiny insects

File.Name: c128b_c128-003.jpg
Notes: 68499243 Look close! This is a remarkably long-legged spider perched on a dead grass stem below new tendrils of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. File.Name: c128d_c128-007.jpg
Notes:
68499178 This is a beautiful dark-winged Drosophilid Fly in the family Drosophilidae of the order Diptera resting on a leaf of Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. These are the flies that flock to the Honey Mushrooms (Armillariella mellea) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2012)

I got this photo (and the next three) above the retaining wall on our driveway where the sun never shines at this time of year. It's a convenient height to look for and photograph tiny insects, my window into the woods - though it's really terrible light for macro photography. I often start my photo day here, and sometimes never leave.

Notes: 68499058 This is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's taking water from the rock - a sandstone outcrop in this case. It has impossibly skinny legs! (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2012)

File.Name: c128f_c128-017.jpg
Notes: 68499007 This is a beautiful new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the canyon, with the sun behind. The glandular flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 28 January 2012)

File.Name: c129a_c129-030.jpg
Notes:
68502433 I found this gorgeous little golden spider in the woods today on a leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Fagaceae chryolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. It has the devil's horns on it's abdomen. I don't think I've ever seen this spider before. I have a sharper photo, but I like the sun-flecked background of this photo, like water. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2012)

It's a challenge to get new photos every day in this drought year. We're below 50% normal rainfall during what should be our rainy season. I'm taking it one day at a time like they say in AA, and so far I'm doing fine!
CommonNames: spider; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak

File.Name: c129b_c129-019.jpg
Notes:
68502359 Here is a tiny Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens, cp. P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae on a leaf of a flowering California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. It's bigger than a spiderling, but not by much. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2012)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. Its a tight crop. The spider is about 830 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the spider is 23.6x830/4288 = 4.6 mm, a bit less than 3/16 inch. Sounds right.

I got this photo above the retaining wall on our driveway this morning where the sun never shines at this time of year. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2012)

68502140 This new flower of native Hillside Gooseberry (Ribes californicum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family looks like an alien landing craft! It's not glandular like the more-common Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum). (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2012)

I'll post another photo that doesn't look so alien tomorrow.

Notes:
68502099 These are new shiny red flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The shiny leaves are pretty too. They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. (San Marcos Pass, 29 January 2012)

I was perched on a steep hillside to get this photo and could quite line up my focus with the flowers. Good enough!

In my original photo at least, I can make out the ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up its compound eyes. I found it this morning on my favorite Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2012)

There's a Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family nearby. I think these flies are partial to that plant.

It was cloudy today, but the forecast is dry as far as it goes. *Sigh*

I've photographed this spider (or it's twin) before. This is the whitish spider with almost-black "chelicerae" - the tooth-like jaws of the spider.

Notes:
68509955 This little "true bug" in the order Hemiptera has amazing colors! It also has 5 antennae segments and and large scutellum which places it in superfamily Pentatomoidea of that order. It's perched on a leaf of Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden this morning.

Browsing at BugGuide, I believe it might be the Red-Cross Shield Bug (Elasmostethus cruciatus), though this one is more colorful than other photos I've seen on the web. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2012)

File.Name: c131b_c131-012.jpg

File.Name: c131c_c130-039.jpg
Notes: 68509828 This is a small but beautiful iridescent-blue Beetle (order Coleoptera) on a stem of (I think) native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. The beetle is "so blue that it looks black." (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2012)

File.Name: c131d_c130-056.jpg
Notes:
68509787 I wish this photo was sharper, but the important part is clear enough. This is a small fly (order Diptera) working new flowers of native BigPod Ceanothus (Ceanothus megacarpus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. If you look close at the flower on the left, you can see the fly's long proboscis touching the "nectar disk" at the base of the flower to get sweet nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2012)

I've found several different kinds of flies on these flowers. I don't know if they are "ordinary flies" or flies that are specialized for the flowers. The fly in my photo does seem to have a very long proboscis.

File.Name: c131e_c131-020.jpg
Notes: 68509731 There are still new mushrooms in woods despite the dry weather. This is a new Cortinarius mushroom in the sub-genus Bulbopodium with an abrupt basal bulb, but I'm not sure which kind. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2012)

File.Name: c131f_c131-022.jpg
Notes: 68509687 I didn't expect to find any slugs today - it's been sunny and dry since last week - but here is a large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) making its way over rough ground. I like the way its good eye is in the sunlight, but it also seems to have an injured eye that is closed up. They only have the breathing hole - called a pneumostome - on their right side. (San Marcos Pass, 31 January 2012)

The hardest part of photographing these tiny insects is noticing them! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2012)

Maybe I shouldn't be surprised - see my photo from January 4:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68411955

File.Name: c201b_c201-011.jpg
Notes:
68513628 These are the sad prizes of my mushroom hunting class with Dunn Middle School kids on Figueroa Mountain today. We only found these two mushrooms, though they are both in perfect shape. I reckon there were more hidden under the leaves - they just didn't show, not even as "mushrumps". Well, you don't come back with game after every hunting trip, and we did have fun! (San Marcos Pass, 1 February 2012)

That's a robust red Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom on the left, and a Cortinarius mushroom in the sub-genus Bulbopodium with an abrupt basal bulb on the right.

We've had just 8.13 inches of rain this season, about 45% normal. We're on track to match or beat the driest year on record - 10.90 inches in 2006-7. For comparison, our wettest year was 87.95 inches in 1982-3. (Records on San Marcos Pass only go back to 1965.) It's been a tough year for mushrooms, but whatever happens there will be photos to take!
Locality: Records on San Marcos Pass only go back to 1965.)  It's been a tough year for mushrooms

File.Name: c201c_c130-047.jpg
Notes:
68513538 This photo shows the strange orange twining parasitic stems of a native Dodder plant - probably the California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae family. It's putting a strangle hold on a stem of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family, which happens to be flowering. (San Marcos Pass, 30 January 2012)

If you look close, you can see the orange stem of the parasite developing bumpy "haustoria" - the structures that will penetrate the host's tissue. Look close on the main stem behind the purple flower at the top-center. Yeah, it's kind of scary.
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae; haustoria

I got this photo in a place that burned a few years ago during the "Gap Fire". Life goes on!

File.Name: c202a_c202-008.jpg
Notes:
68517613 These spiders are in love!

That's the male of the left with the smaller abdomen and the female on the right. Look close! The male spider is holding a tiny drop of sperm in his leg-like pedipalp and preparing to insert it in the epigynum of his lady to make babies. *Sigh* (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2012)

I believe from the web that these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae). They are having their nuptials under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It was in the shade, so I opened my lens 2-1/3 stops to capture the photo.

I had Hike Club today, followed by a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society lecture by Walt Koenig on Acorn Woodpeckers. It was a great talk, and I got home late - but I still wanted to post this photo!
Locality: Quercus agrifolia) tree. It was in the shade

File.Name: c202b_c202-018.jpg
Notes: 68517599 I was lucky to notice this little husk of a spider on the leaf of a Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) today. The only green spider I know is the Green Lynx Spider (Peucetia viridens, cp. P. longipalpis) in the family Oxyopidae. That disconnected piece in the back sure looks like a head with eyes. Maybe it's the shed skin after a molt, or a spent male which has already done his bit for the species? (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2012)
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Peucetia viridens; P. longipalpis; Oxyopidae

File.Name: c202c_c202-002.jpg
Notes:
68517596 Here is another photo of the intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and strange, related to sassafras and avocado. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2012)

I think I can make out a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with six orange glands at the base, finally surrounded by a circle of six fat stamens with four anthers each. They are remarkably intricate for such a common tree.

File.Name: c203a_c202-024.jpg
Notes:
68521173 Here is a small ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on a new flower bud of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2012)

The ants have been carefully placing the aphids on all the new flower buds of this shrub, and they tend them. It's a kind of farming.

File.Name: c203b_c203-001.jpg
Notes: 68521134 This is a small Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) digging into a flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They are hard to photograph because they move so fast. Look how perfectly the bee's abdomen fits the pistil of the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2012)

File.Name: c203c_c202-047.jpg
Notes:
68521096 The Mirror of Galadriel?

I took my after-school Hike Club kids to our old cabin at Kinevan Ranch yesterday. Here they are looking into the frog pond. The pond is reflecting the oaks beyond, like a magical mirror. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2012)

The pond is low this year, but I was surprised by how much water there is. There are thousands of frog eggs. I hope we get rain soon so the tadpoles can prosper. I used to bring our two and three year old kids here to listen to the deafening chorus of Pacific Tree Frogs (Pseudacris regilla). *Shush* and listen!

File.Name: c203d_c202-036.jpg
Notes:
68521010 I took my after-school Hike Club kids to our old cabin at Kinevan Ranch yesterday. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68400206

These bedrock mortar holes are in the old orchard just beyond the cabin. They were made by the Chumash people in the old days. They are remarkable - partly because adjoining holes have grown together through repeated use over many years. They are still filled with rainwater, reflecting the sky. (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2012)

Notes:
68524758 This is a bee-size bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae in the order Diptera. Those red bumps on the leaves are "Erineum Galls", maybe caused by an barely visible Eriophyid mite in the genus Eriophyes?

Our unseasonal beautiful dry weather continues, but there is a chance of rain next Tuesday!

File.Name: c204c_c204-018.jpg
Notes:
68524567 Here is a huge container ship in the Santa Barbara channel, with Santa Cruz Island on the left and Santa Rosa Island on the right. The pines are mostly Coulter Pines (Pinus coulteri), though I can make out a couple of Gray Pines (Pinus sabiniana) in the mix, and there might be introduced Knobcone Pines (Pinus attenuata) as well. I got this photo today from the ridge above the headwaters of Paradise Creek. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2012)

It was a beautiful day today - we've had nothing but beautiful days lately. But we have a chance of rain on Tuesday. Bring it on, we need it!

Notes: 68524502 The native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plants in the Fabaceae plant family are just starting to flower. They are a butterfly plant, and I did see two (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012) today. I couldn't get the photo. Maybe tomorrow. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2012)

File.Name: c204e_c130-050.jpg
Notes:
68524456 Here are new fruit of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The berries have pin-head glands like the rest of the plant. They are edible when ripe, but they don't taste great. (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2012)

Our shrub at home is still flowering - as it has been since early October! See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/68057056

File.Name: c204f_c203-021.jpg
Notes:
68524383 Here are more small ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on a new flower bud of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2012)

To answer Gary's question, the ants "milk" honeydew from the aphids by stroking them. In exchange, the aphids get protection from the ants. Sounds like a fair exchange where both parties benefit.

There's more info here:
   http://insects.about.com/od/coolandunusualinsects/f/antsandaphids.htm
