File.Name: c205a_c205-027.jpg
Notes:
68528267 I found this native Dusky-Footed Wood Rat (Neotoma fuscipes) along the side of East Camino Cielo road today. The unfortunate rat was still frisky, but it was dragging its hind legs like its back was broken, no doubt by a passing car. Usually these critters are nocturnal. I wonder what it was doing out in the daytime? (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2012)

I wondered whether to take it home, but I had no box, and I figured it will soon become a welcome meal for some hungry fox or hawk or owl. I let it be.

You can see its white feet and long "vibrissae" or whiskers, which are specialized hairs to feel rather than see. These rats build large nests from piles of sticks. I'm shocked that I can't find a photo of a nest, but I'll fix that tomorrow. The rats can move into houses too - we've had some trouble with that at home!

File.Name: c205b_c205-018.jpg
Notes:
68528175 This is a gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera ... feeding from a flower??

The flower is a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. I believe the insect is the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema)? See the photos at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/7900/bgimage

So what is this predator insect doing feeding on flowers? I saw three on them on the flowers today! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2012)

I've always assumed these Assassin Bugs in the Reduviidae are all predators, but now I'm confused. I've been living in woods for 40+ years and I still get confused. Wahoo!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; California Bay; Spiny Assassin Bug; Assassin Bugs
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Umbellularia californica; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Reduviidae
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Umbellularia californica; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Reduviidae

File.Name: c205c_c205-017.jpg
Notes: 68528094 This is the first new catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek, with backlight. It's odd that they are female pistillate catkins - usually the male staminate catkins of these "dioecious" (with separate male and female) trees appear first. They look wind-pollinated, but they produce nectar which attracts many insects, though apparently not yet. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2012)

File.Name: c205d_c205-005.jpg
Notes:
68528001 I noticed this tiny Leafhopper insect in the family Cicadellidae of the order Homoptera on a leaf of native Wood Mint (Stachys bullata) in the Labiatae plant family. I got this at my usual morning place above the retaining wall on our driveway in deep shade. (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2012)

It's interesting how digital gets grainy (like Tri-X film!) in low light, even though it's the same ISO 400 speed I use for most on my insect photos, which don't look grainy when taken in sunlight.

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The leafhopper is about 500 pixels long on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x500/4288 = 2.8 mm, about 0.11 inch. This leafhopper is tiny!
Locality: like Tri-X film!) in low light

File.Name: c205e_c203-018.jpg
Notes:
68527835 This is a member of the "Microlepidoptera" (micromoths, order Lepidoptera) resting on a blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on our driveway. We're getting moments of real sunshine through the trees as the sun gets ever higher after the solstice! (San Marcos Pass, 3 February 2012)

This is another photo I got near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The moth is about 960 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x960/4288 = 5.3 mm, a bit less than 1/4 inch. Like I said, it's a micro-lepidoptera!

File.Name: c205f_c204-012.jpg
Notes:
68527731 I can't resist posting another photo of a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae). This one is perched on the leaf of a flowering California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. It's putting on a display for me! (San Marcos Pass, 4 February 2012)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The spider is about 680 pixels long on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x680/4288 = 3.7 mm, a bit more than 1/8 inch. These jumping spiders are tiny!

File.Name: c213a_3605-004.jpg
Notes:
6872979699 Soap bubble on grass with reflections, the world turned inside out. (Santa Ynez Valley, 6 June 2003)

This was the first photo I posted on my Fotolog site many years ago on 21 June 2003 - see www.fotolog.net/treebeard/251716/. It's still one of my favorites, shot with a humble Olympus C-2040Z camera. It seems appropriate to start using my Flickr account with this photo.

I've stuck with Fotolog for 9 1/2 years and 9,250+ photos, through times of bad service and spam - but mostly they've been good times. I still like their (former) elegant screen layout, and "daily photos blog" format. They recently changed everything - and now it's time to move on to Flickr. Zen, I should have listened to your advise!

I was smart to get my name "Treebeard" on Flickr way back in October 2004 when it was still fresh. Fotolog recently became more Flicker-like - without the benefits of larger photos and sets and collections. I guess they wanted to be more friendly for mobile apps, though I don't have a cell phone either and don't want one. I still intend to make these posts my daily "photo blog" so expect some chat here.

File.Name: c214a_c213-009.jpg
Notes: 6878513781 Here is a beautiful golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree on the ground. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2012)

File.Name: c214b_c214-004.jpg
Notes: 6878464129 I believe this is an early Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly working flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in the Ericaceae plant family. It's a duskywing without a white fringe on it's hindwings. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2012)

File.Name: c214c_c213-015.jpg
Notes:
6878391705 I believe this is the Jonquil Amanita (Amanita gemmata) - yellow cap with white "warts" which are the remains of the universal veil, skirt-like ring on the stem, and a free-rimmed volva at the base. It looks a bit like a yellow Amanita muscaria, but the volva is different. It's an attractive mushroom, but it's not edible because it intergrades with the poisonous Amanita pantherina. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2012)

I found this mushroom yesterday growing under Big-berry Manzanita Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. I've also found it under Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees in the same plant family.

Did you notice that little ant on the stem?

Notes: 6878331247 This is a beautiful tiny fly (order Diptera) perched on a tiny flower of non-native (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family. It looks a bit like a Dance Fly in the family Empididae, but it might also be an atypical Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae. I don't know. (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2012)

File.Name: c214e_c205-024.jpg
Notes:
6878294541 I found this native Dusky-Footed Wood Rat (Neotoma fuscipes) along the side of East Camino Cielo road last week. The unfortunate rat was still frisky, but it was dragging its hind legs like its back was broken, no doubt by a passing car. Usually these critters are nocturnal. I wonder what it was doing out in the daytime? (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2012)

I wondered whether to take it home, but I had no box, and I figured it will soon become a welcome meal for some hungry fox or hawk or owl. I let it be.

These rats build large nests from piles of sticks, like beaver dams in the woods. The rats can move into houses too - we've had some trouble with that at home.

File.Name: c214f_c202-008.jpg
Notes:
6878240479 Spiders in love!

That's the male of the left with the smaller abdomen and the female on the right. Look close! The male spider is holding a tiny drop of sperm in his leg-like pedipalp and preparing to insert it in the epigynum of his lady to make babies. *Sigh* (San Marcos Pass, 2 February 2012)

I believe from the web that these are Sheetweb Spiders (Linyphiidae). They are having their nuptials under a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It was in the shade, so I opened my lens 2-1/3 stops to capture the photo. I like the "high-key" effect.
Locality: Quercus agrifolia) tree. It was in the shade

File.Name: c214g_c205-018.jpg
Notes:
6878217439 This is a gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera ... feeding from a flower??

The flower is a California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree. I believe the insect is the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema)? See the photos at BugGuide:
bugguide.net/node/view/7900/bgimage

So what is this predator insect doing feeding on flowers? I saw three on them on the flowers! (San Marcos Pass, 5 February 2012)

I've always assumed these Assassin Bugs in the Reduviidae are all predators, but now I'm confused. I've been living in woods for 40+ years and I still get confused. Wahoo!
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; California Bay; Spiny Assassin Bug; Assassin Bugs

File.Name: c215b_c214-021.jpg
Notes: 6884387961 This is a well-named native Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflower in the Primulaceae plant family growing in the woods yesterday - almost the first I've seen this year. They are really beautiful, especially that crinkly tissue at the base of the anthers that look like little bird beaks. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2012)

File.Name: c215c_c214-011.jpg
Notes:
6884345173 I found this remarkable native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plant in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the woods yesterday. This early-flowering perennial has leafy red bracts with a few real flowers in between. (San Marcos Pass, 14 February 2012)

This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. It comes up mushroom-like, ready to flower.

The flower heads of these Indian Warrior plants are sold on the Internet "legal high" market. The effects are described on one web site as "tranquilizers, muscle relaxants, powerful aphrodesiacs, and sedatives". They sell for quite a bit.

I'd rather enjoy the flowers. This "wildcrafting" of native plants - especially these interesting root-parasites that can't be cultivated - bothers me!
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. It comes up mushroom-like

File.Name: c215d_c213-024.jpg
Notes:
6884278125 There are still yummy Chanterelle (Cantharellus cibarius) mushrooms in the woods, even though it's been very dry this winter. They are actually one of our most reliable mushrooms. (San Marcos Pass, 13 February 2012)

We've had just 8.35 inches of rain this season, about 37% normal. We're on track to match or beat the driest year on record - 10.90 inches in 2006-7. For comparison, our wettest year was 87.95 inches in 1982-3. (Records on the pass only go back to 1965.) It's been a tough year for mushrooms, but whatever happens there will be photos to get!
Locality: Records on the pass only go back to 1965.)  It's been a tough year for mushrooms

File.Name: c215e_c211-054.jpg
Notes: 6884242177 I guess this is an unattached young male Northern Elephant Seal (Mirounga angustirostis, family Phocidae, sub-order Pinnipedia) on the beach at the Piedras Blancas rookery near San Simeon on the way to Big Sur. It has large puppy dog eyes, and it's alone, without a harem of females like the big boys have. But he's been trying - look close to see bloody tears on his snout. (Piedras Blancas near San Simeon, 11 February 2012)

File.Name: c215f_c211-043.jpg
Notes:
6884194051 These Northern Elephant Seals (Mirounga angustirostis, family Phocidae, sub-order Pinnipedia) are snuggling after "doing it" on the beach at the Piedras Blancas rookery near San Simeon on the way to Big Sur. They look happy. The seals are mating and birthing right now, and it's quite a show! (Piedras Blancas near San Simeon, 11 February 2012)

You can see the huge scared male (3,000 to 5,000 pounds) with his fin around his smaller lady (900 to 1,800 pounds). There's a dark-skinned pup behind him.

6884107363 Humans (Homo sapiens) watching Northern Elephant Seals (Mirounga angustirostis, family Phocidae, sub-order Pinnipedia) at the Piedras Blancas rookery near San Simeon on the way to Big Sur. The seals are mating and birthing right now. It's quite a show! (Piedras Blancas near San Simeon, 11 February 2012)

You can see a huge male sitting up at the bottom of my photo. The males weigh between 3,000 to 5,000 pounds!

File.Name: c216d_c208-005.jpg
Notes:
6889676297 This is a perfect Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom that I found in the woods a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2012)

These deadly mushrooms are easy to recognize. Pure white cap - like a styrofoam cup in the woods; ring or "skirt" on the stem; and the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem. They are beautiful, but deadly - don't eat them!

File.Name: c216e_c208-008.jpg
Notes:
6889674297 Beautiful, but deadly...

This is a perfect Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata) mushroom that I found in the woods a few days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 8 February 2012)

They have a pure white cap - like a styrofoam cup in the woods. If you look close you can see the ring or "skirt" on the stem. It's more difficult to see the characteristic "death cup" volva at the base of the stem, but you can see a bit of the flaring volva in this photo.

People get into fatal trouble by pulling this mushroom from the ground - and leaving the volva behind - rather than digging it up.

File.Name: c216k_c206-017.jpg
Notes:
6889353005 This is a chubby but tiny Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched for a moment on a leaf of Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Could it possibly be a Gall Wasp in the family Cynipidae? (San Marcos Pass, 6 February 2012)

I got this photo from my usual place above the retaining wall on our driveway where the sun never shines at this time of year. It's a convenient height to look for and photograph tiny insects, my window into the woods - though it's really terrible light for macro photography. I got this photo at 1/40 second@f/11@ISO 400. I often start my photo day here, and sometimes never leave.

This is another photo I got near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The wasp is about 335 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x335/4288 = 1.8 mm or 0.07 inches, a bit more than 1/16 inch. Like I said, this wasp is tiny!

File.Name: c217d_c217-019.jpg
Notes:
6894585337 This gall is interesting because it's growing on Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Fagaceae chryolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family, which doesn't usually have many galls in our mountains. Heh - it looks like a big tit, complete with the nipple! (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2012)

Structurally, this looks a lot like the gall of the Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella in the family Cynipidae, order Hymenoptera). The Canyon Live Oak is considered an "intermediate oak" between white and black oaks, so maybe it is - but it sure looks different.

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Fagaceae chryolepis; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Fagaceae chryolepis; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Cynipidae

File.Name: c217e_c217-031.jpg

File.Name: c217f_c217-041.jpg
Notes:
6894509103 These are new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It's another of the white-flowered Ceanothus shrubs that cover our mountains every spring. The individual flowers are quite intricate. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2012)

This one is distinctive. It has two-toned leaves that are green on top and white underneath. It also has "corky stipules" - those round dark things that look like little brown coconuts at the base of the opposite leaves. It doesn't grow as low as our canyon, but it does grow about a mile up East Camino Cielo road near the Painted Cave.

File.Name: c217g_c217-056.jpg
Notes: 6894412121 This is an odd clump of Helvella mushrooms coming up under a rock in our garden. They do have non-fluted stalks and the undersides of the caps are not obviously hairy. Maybe Helvella compressa or H. elastica? (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2012)

File.Name: c218a_c218-002.jpg
Notes:
6900338187 I found this odd spider in the woods this morning. I suspect it's a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae). It has an odd "bottle gourd shape" to it's abdomen. When disturbed, it swings violently. (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2012)

It was breezy today, which made getting these photos difficult. Clouds moved in this afternoon, but I'm not expecting any rain. *Sigh* We really need a "March Miracle" rain.

File.Name: c218b_c218-020.jpg
Notes:
6900232285 I happened to notice this tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods today perched on an old seed head of grass. It's just waiting (and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me! Ticks can spend weeks or months just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that! (San Marcos Pass, 18 February 2012)

It was a challenge to get this photo in the wind and low light. I bumped up my ISO. I also took 50+ photos to get this one!

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The tick is about 415 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x445/4288 = 2.3 mm or 0.09 inches, about 3/32 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

I'm not up on my "Acarology" - the study of mites and ticks - so I'm not sure what kind of tick this is. Local ticks can carry Lyme Disease, but I'm not worried. If you get ticked, just pull the damn thing out! Use tweezers if you have to. The first sign of a tick bite is a persistent irritation. The bites usually swell up and hurt for a few days after you remove the tick. No big deal.

File.Name: c218c_c218-018.jpg

These were the only mushrooms I found today. This continuing dry weather is getting me down.

"Oak apples" are very different on Scrub Oaks and Coast Live Oaks, caused by different wasps. See my [Previous] photo.

associatedTaxa: Andricus quercuscalifornicus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c218g_c217-063.jpg
Notes:
6899800971 This is a golf ball-size "oak apple gall" growing on a branch of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree. It is the gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2012)

I noticed this gall from our upstairs porch, and got the image with my "big glass" Tamron 200-500 mm lens. These galls have more interesting textures than the "oak apple" galls on white oaks in my [Next] photo, but they are not as common. I can see some exit holes where the wasps got away. (San Marcos Pass, 17 February 2012)

Like many of our oak gall wasps, these wasps have two distinct generations. The spring wasps make different galls. See my photo at: http://www.fotolog.com/treebeard/20350708

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Gall Wasps
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Cynipidae
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Cynipidae

File.Name: c219a_c219-016.jpg
Notes:
6907053097 Here is an open flower of a native Dodder plant - probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae family. It's a parasitic plant without chlorophyll that gets nutrients from another green plant, native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family in this case. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

We had just a trace of rain last night - 0.01 inch or less. It's been that kind of year, very dry when it should be our rainy season. I'm worried about the trees and wildfires come summer.
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti; Solanaceae
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti; Solanaceae

File.Name: c219b_c219-020.jpg
Notes:
6906991587 This photo shows the strange orange twining parasitic stems of a native Dodder plant - probably the California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae family. It's putting a strangle hold on a stem of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family. The flowers are not quite open, but see my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

If you look close, you can see the bumpy "haustoria" - the structures that will penetrate the host's tissue. Yeah, it's kind of scary.
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti; Solanaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Solanum xanti; Solanaceae; haustoria

File.Name: c219c_c219-033.jpg
Notes:
6906880543 Here is a tiny Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera. Those skinny wings can't be useful for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

This critter is perched on a leaf of an unusual native oak in the woods. I believe it's a "Shreve Oak" - which might be Quercus shrevei; or Q. parvula; or Q. parvula var. shrevei; or maybe Q. wislizenii var. frutescens, or even Q. wislizenii! The large buds are distinctive.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Quercus shrevei; Q. parvula; Q. parvula; shrevei; Q. wislizenii; frutescens; Q. wislizenii

File.Name: c219d_c219-022.jpg
Notes: 6906841231 These are pretty blue (oh so blue) flowers of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers are surprisingly intricate, like little origami boxes. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

File.Name: c219e_c219-027.jpg
Notes: 6906760291 This is looking close at a single single compound umbel of the native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family in my [Previous] photo. The individual flowers are beautiful and intricate, though you have to get close to see them. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

File.Name: c219f_c219-026.jpg
Notes: 6906725653 These are new "umbel flowers" of native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family. The plant is sometimes known as the Southern Tauschia or Southern Umbrellawort, but it deserves a better proper name. They have handsome foliage, but it's hard to show flowers and leaves in the same photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

File.Name: c219g_c219-004.jpg
Notes:
6906668013 I believe these are new flowers of native Climbing Bedstraw (Galium porrigens) in the Rubiaceae plant family. Everything comes in fours with these tiny flowers on the whorled plant stems. The new flower buds are interesting. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

This is the first Galium I've found flowering this year. They are a favorite plant group of mine, though they are difficult to key out. We have quite a few species on San Marcos Pass.

File.Name: c219h_c219-007.jpg
Notes: 6906603169 These are new leaves of the uncommon native Chaparral Orchid (Piperia elongata) in the Orchidaceae plant family that I found in the woods today. The broad leaves come first, then whither before they flower in summer. (San Marcos Pass, 19 February 2012)

File.Name: c220a_c220-023.jpg
Notes:
6914112553 This is a beautiful Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a dead leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek this morning. I reckon this is an Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae). Her ovipositor is longer than she is, and the tip is beveled like a hypodermic needle! (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

Julie and I are taking the train to Portland, Oregon tomorrow to visit our kids and grandkids. I'll be back with more photos in a week.
CommonNames: Wasp; Coast Live Oak; Ichneumon Wasp

File.Name: c220b_c220-008.jpg
Notes:
6914049171 It's difficult to photograph these shiny black wasps in contrasty bright sunlight!

This is a tiny Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched for a moment on a stem of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek this morning. It has a large abdomen and so sign of a "wasp-waist". Maybe it's a Sawfly in the family Tenthredinidae of the "Symphyta" wasps? (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

File.Name: c220c_c220-038.jpg
Notes:
6913981935 I found this gorgeous fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on my favorite Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek this morning. I don't know what kind of fly it is, though the red eyes and gray color and speckled wings seem distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

I love photographing flies - they have so much structural detail!

File.Name: c220d_c220-027.jpg
Notes:
6913910743 This is an odd little insect that I found today hanging from the leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The tick is about 430 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x430/4288 = 2.4 mm or 0.09 inches, about 3/32 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

I know my insects pretty well, but I don't know what this is. Help me if you know!

File.Name: c220e_c220-032.jpg
Notes:
6913808095 This is a gorgeous little spider sitting in an "open boat" seed capsule of a Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. I suspect from its web that it's a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

I've seen this spider before. See my photos at: http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/68382550/ and http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/68397030/.

File.Name: c220f_c220-035.jpg
Notes: 6913714897 This is an unknown polished gall on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in our canyon, and there are different galls along the same leaf vein. These are not in my book on "Plant Galls of California and Other Western States". They are about the size of a BB. Nature is full of little surprises. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

File.Name: c220g_c220-004.jpg
Notes:
6913601957 I've noticed a native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family turning black with a fungus, and I discovered why today . There is an infestation of Scale insects on the shrub. They are superfamily Coccoidea and maybe family Coccidae in the order Homoptera. Creepy, and there's not much to do about it. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

I suspect those larger black lumps on the twig are older scale insects.
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Coccoidea; Coccidae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Coccoidea; Coccidae; Homoptera

File.Name: c220h_c220-005.jpg
Notes:
6913562861 These are the remarkably intricate new flowers of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family. The flowers are beautiful and strange, related to sassafras and avocado. (San Marcos Pass, 20 February 2012)

I think I can make out a pistil in the center, surrounded by an inner-circle of three fat stamens with six orange glands at the base, finally surrounded by a circle of six fat stamens with four anthers each. They are strange and wonderful for such a common tree in our woods.

File.Name: c229a_c221-006.jpg
Notes:
6942908811 This is Razor Blades - the first surfing spot on "The Ranch" going north from Santa Barbara. The spectacular rocks are the eroded remnants of almost-vertically-tilted layers of Monterrey Shale rock. It looks like there's been a recent landslide in the lower-right. (Razor Blades, Hollister Ranch, 21 February 2012)

I remember walking here along the railroad tracks carrying my *heavy* surfboard many years ago. We were too tired to hike on to Drakes. I can't remember if it was worth it for the waves, but it sure was for the memories.

This is surfer paradise, but private land. You have to know someone or take a long walk or boat ride to get access. Unfortunately the surf was flat this day.

I got this photo through the window of our moving train as Julie and I went to Portland, Oregon last week - not the best way to get sharp photos. We got home last night, and brought a bit of rain with us!

File.Name: c229b_c221-017.jpg
Notes:
6942868805 There are many legendary surfing points on "The Ranch" - actually two ranches, Hollister Ranch and Bixby Ranch - around the curve of Point Conception. They have names like Drake's, Little Drake's, Utah, Rights and Lefts, Auggies, Lefts and Rights, Cojo Point, Perko's, and Government Point. This is one of those, but I lost count riding through on the train. If you look close, you can see two sailboats washed up on the beach. (Hollister Ranch, 21 February 2012)

It's surfer paradise, but private land. You have to know someone or take a long walk or boat ride to get access. Unfortunately the surf was flat the day we went by.

I got this photo through the window of our moving train as Julie and I went to Portland, Oregon last week - not the best way to get sharp photos!

File.Name: c229c_c221-027.jpg
Notes:
6796736278 I'm pretty sure this rocky headland is Point Arguello on the Vandenberg Air Force Base. Along with Point Conception further south, it pivots the California coast from north-south to east-west to create the Santa Barbara channel. It's an important landmark, but it's easy to miss when you're flying past on the train! (Point Arguello, 21 February 2012)

I got this photo through the window of our moving train as Julie and I went to Portland, Oregon last week - not the best way to get sharp photos!

File.Name: c229d_c221-030.jpg
Notes:
6942828183 I'm sure this little estuary on Vandenberg Air Force Base has a name, but I don't know what it is. It's between Point Arguello and Surf Beach. (Surf Beach, 21 February 2012)

I got this photo through the window of our moving train as Julie and I went to Portland, Oregon last week - not the best way to get sharp photos!

File.Name: c229e_c221-042.jpg
Notes:
6942792065 This photo shows restoration work in progress, and I'm happy to see it. That fence seems to be the boundary. Beyond the fence is all invasive Beach Grass (Ammophila arenaria) and Ice Plant (Carpobrotus edulis), but it's all been killed in front of the fence to allow the Giant Coreopsis (Coreopsis gigantea) in the (Asteraceae) family room to flower. The location is on Vandenberg Air Force Base near Surf Beach at the mouth of the Santa Ynez River. (Surf Beach, 21 February 2012)

Beach Grass and Ice Plant were introduced here deliberately to stabilize the dunes and protect the railway. It's a mistake we are just starting to pay for now.

I got this photo through the window of our moving train as Julie and I went to Portland, Oregon last week - not the best way to get sharp photos!
CommonNames: Beach Grass; Ice Plant; Giant Coreopsis
ScientificName: Ammophila arenaria; Carpobrotus edulis; Coreopsis gigantea; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Ammophila arenaria; Carpobrotus edulis; Coreopsis gigantea; Asteraceae

File.Name: c229f_c221-048.jpg
Notes:
6796648086 This is the view looking into a side part of the estuary at Surf Beach near the mouth of the Santa Ynez River. The dominant plant is invasive Ice Plant aka Hottentot Fig (Carpobrotus edulis) in the Aizoaceae plant family, though you can see a few natives poking through. (Surf Beach, 21 February 2012)

I got this photo through the window of our moving train as Julie and I went to Portland, Oregon last week - not the best way to get sharp photos!

File.Name: c229g_c221-051.jpg
Notes:
6942719419 This is beautiful, but the plants are mostly invasive Ice Plant aka Hottentot Fig (Carpobrotus edulis) in the Aizoaceae plant family. This is part of the estuary at Surf Beach near the mouth of the Santa Ynez River. (Surf Beach, 21 February 2012)

It was introduced here deliberately to stabilize the dunes and protect the railway. It's a mistake we are just starting to pay for now.

I got this photo through the window of our moving train as Julie and I went to Portland, Oregon last week - not the best way to get sharp photos!

File.Name: c229h_c221-069.jpg
Notes: 6942697035 This is the beautiful green ranch land north of San Luis Obispo. I got this photo from the train - in motion - last week as Julie and I went to Portland to visit our kids and grandkids. The highway is below the highest ridge, and the view from a car is not so nice. (San Luis Obispo, 21 February 2012)

File.Name: c301a_c301-025.jpg
Notes:
6945461319 These are new "flowers" of the native dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family growing in the canyon. It's really a remarkable plant - related to plants that grow in water, but here it is flowering in the woods. The male stamens are mostly on top, and the female pistils are mostly below. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

We had just a bit of rain last night - 0.08 inches by my rain gauge. It's been that kind of rain year in California - fractions of an inch instead of inches. We stand at 8.47 inches, just 32% of normal. I'm worried about our long, hot summer...

File.Name: c301b_c301-008.jpg
Notes:
6945419789 These are tiny bisexual winter galls of one of the tiny Cynipidae wasps of the order Hymenoptera that will mate and produce larger summer galls on our Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia shrubs in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

The wasps have a complicated life cycle. These bisexual winter galls have produced male and female wasps which mate and lay eggs that will produce the unisexual summer galls. The summer galls will hatch only females that produce the bisexual galls that produce males and females, and the cycle repeats. Nature is sometimes complicated like that!

File.Name: c301c_c301-017.jpg
Notes: 6945386313 This is the only insect I managed to capture today. (It was cold and windy after we had a bit of rain last night.) It's a small Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a stem of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek. It has a large abdomen and no sign of a "wasp-waist". I guess it's a Sawfly in the family Tenthredinidae of the "Symphyta" wasps. There are willows nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

File.Name: c301d_c301-012.jpg
Notes:
6799251768 I guess this is a kind of gall, though it is not an insect gall. These are deformed leaves of a native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family which has been invaded by a rust fungus (Uredinales, Basidiomycotina). (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

The plants completely change when they are infected - the leaflets are thin and widely spaced, and the stems have no thorns. I find this every year on a few plants in the canyon. It doesn't seem to be spreading.

File.Name: c301e_c301-021.jpg
Notes:
6799221596 This plant looks like a little conifer, but it's really a fern - at least it's fern-like, because it produces spores rather than flowers. This is Bigelow's Spike-Moss (Selaginella bigelovii) growing on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. Dinosaurs stepped on primitive plants similar to this! (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

These plants are tough and they grow in dry exposed places, like lichens. They are collected and sold for model train layouts as scale conifer trees.

File.Name: c301f_c301-030.jpg
Notes:
6945319129 These are new catkins of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek, with backlight. They are female pistillate catkins of this "dioecious" (with separate male and female) tree. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

They look wind-pollinated, but they produce nectar which attracts many insects - though it was too windy for most insects today.

File.Name: c301g_c301-023.jpg
Notes:
6945282421 Despite the dry winter, here are new flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

They seem like perfect butterfly flowers with such a "deep throat" - and they do sometimes visit, but not as often as I would expect.

File.Name: c301h_c301-016.jpg
Notes:
6945260071 "Leaves of three, let it be.
Leaves of four, eat some more!"
- Homer Simpson

These are the first shiny new leaves and flower buds of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 1 March 2012)

The leaves contain the oil urushiol that causes an allergic skin reaction in many people. It's an allergen, not a poison. Enjoy, but don't touch - at least know your limits!

I'm one of the lucky ones who doesn't usually get the rash. It's a good thing - considering how much time I spend in the woods!

File.Name: c302a_c302-028.jpg
Notes:
6947716031 This is a gnarly Assassin Bug in the Reduviidae family of the order Hemiptera waiting for prey on a flower cluster of native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family today. You can see its mighty proboscis folded under its head.

I believe the insect is the Spiny Assassin Bug (Sinea spinipes, but cp. S. diadema)? See the photos at BugGuide:
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/7900/bgimage

These insects are definite predators, but they are not above tasting a flower when there are no insects. See my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6878217439/in/photostream

I got more photos today, but I'm posting a few early because Julie and I have a date tonight to see the show Don't Fence Me In featuring the Quebe Sisters among others! We got comp tickets from our friend on the sound crew.
CommonNames: Assassin Bug; California Bay; Spiny Assassin Bug
ScientificName: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Don't Fence Me In
associatedTaxa: Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Sinea spinipes; S. diadema; Don't Fence Me In

File.Name: c302b_c302-027.jpg
Notes: 6947704621 This is a mighty Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower cluster of a native California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family today. Be careful little fly, those flowers have teeth! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2012)

File.Name: c302c_c302-012.jpg
Notes:
6947652073 This cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) is perched on a flowering Aloe plant in the Aloaceae or Asphodeloideae or Xanthorrhoeaceae plant family, depending on your authority. Sure it's looking at me - these tiny spiders have terrific vision. It's remarkable to make eye contact with anything so small! (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2012)

The aloe is an oddball flowering in our garden. We've had this plant for 20+ years, and this is the first time its ever flowered. (I guess it's too cold in the mountains.) We've had some other flower oddities this winter, including ginger and Mexican Tree Daisy (Montanoa bipinnatifida) in the Asteraceae plant family. Global warming?
Locality: I guess it's too cold in the mountains.)  We've had some other flower oddities this winter
CommonNames: Jumping Spider; Aloe; ginger; Mexican Tree Daisy
ScientificName: Salticidae; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae; Montanoa bipinnatifida; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Salticidae; Aloaceae; Asphodeloideae; Xanthorrhoeaceae; Montanoa bipinnatifida; Asteraceae

File.Name: c303a_c303-032.jpg
Notes:
6804816246 This is one of our most beautiful butterflies - the Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly, perched on a stem by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2012)

It's larval food plant is Dudleya which grows nearby. It looks like just another Spring Azure (Celastrina ladon) butterfly in the air, but it's something else when it lands!

The last time I got a photo of this butterfly was way back on March 12, 2005. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/9644927/

We saw the Quebe Sisters, Wylie Gustafson, and others in the Don't Fence Me In show last night. It was a really great show that felt like a music festival. If you don't know the Quebes yet, look on YouTube:
   http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GcRgdHASYz8
ScientificName: Philotes sonorensis; Dudleya; Celastrina ladon; Don't Fence Me In
associatedTaxa: Philotes sonorensis; Dudleya; Celastrina ladon; Don't Fence Me In

File.Name: c303b_c303-063.jpg
Notes: 6950891111 This is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched on a leaf of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2012)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Phacelia ramosissima; austrolitoralis; Hydrophyllaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Phacelia ramosissima; austrolitoralis; Hydrophyllaceae

File.Name: c303c_c303-026.jpg
Notes:
6804747794 These are tiny Flies (Diptera) working a female pistillate catkin of Arroyo Willow (Salix lasiolepis) in the Salicaceae plant family growing by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2012)

The flies look like Dance Flies in the family Empididae, but they might also be atypical Bee Flies (family Bombyliidae) in the subfamily Toxophorinae. I don't know.

I have a better photo of the fly at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6878331247/in/photostream

File.Name: c303d_c303-036.jpg
Notes:
6950814877 Comparing images at BugGuide, I think JMDN is right - this is a small Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae. The main difference with the Reduviidae is the clubbed antennae, also the front legs look less predator-like. Thank you JMDN! (5 March 2012)

My original comment:
"Here is a small Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family of the order Hemiptera. It's perched on a leaf of Mugwort (Artemisia douglasiana) in the Asteraceae plant family by the creek today. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2012)
CommonNames: Stilt Bug; Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Mugwort
ScientificName: Berytidae; Reduviidae; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Artemisia douglasiana; Asteraceae

File.Name: c303e_c303-006.jpg
Notes:
6950790241 "It's a dirty job, but someone has to do it!"

This is a slender Syrphid Flower Fly (Syrphidae) in the order Diptera fully engaged with a flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It's dusted with pollen. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2012)

File.Name: c303f_c303-048.jpg
Notes:
6804623162 This is a giant hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) prowling through the woods today, despite the mostly dry weather this year. They're 6-8 inches long! This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains, and we only see them after rains - even little rains like the 0.08 inches a couple of days ago. (San Marcos Pass, 3 March 2012)

These slugs have two pairs of 'feelers' or tentacles on their head. The upper pair have eye spots, while the lower pair provides a tactile sense and smell.

Did you ever kiss a banana slug? Don't - they are coated in slime that sticks like rubber cement!

File.Name: c303g_c302-054.jpg
Notes: 6804584850 This is a odd furry gall on a branch of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I don't often find galls on these shrubs, so this one is especially interesting. It's not in my book on California galls. I don't know what kind of gall it is. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2012)

File.Name: c303h_c302-041.jpg
Notes:
6804518216 This is a strange fang-like gall growing on a leaf bud of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. This kind of oak doesn't usually host many galls in our mountains. I'm sure it's an insect gall, probably caused by a Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae) gall, but this gall is not in my books, so I'm not sure what caused it. It's the only one I've seen. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2012)

Galls are abnormal plant growths caused by an invading organism. Many (including maybe this one) are caused by tiny specialized Gall Wasps (Cynipidae) which lay eggs that are nurtured by the abnormal growth they initiate. They are fascinating because they involve genetic interactions between plant and animal.
CommonNames: Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Cynipid Gall Wasp; Gall Wasps

File.Name: c304a_c304-015.jpg
Notes:
6808351540 March Flies in March!

I believe this is the female March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) that goes with the male fly in my [Previous] photo. It's sitting in the surreal landscape of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. I believe this is the genus Bibio because of the sharp spurs on the front tibia - maybe Bibio xanthopus? (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Opuntia; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Opuntia; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus

File.Name: c304b_c304-012.jpg
Notes:
6954416057 March Flies in March!

I believe this is a male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) spread-eagled on a pad of Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. I believe this is the genus Bibio because of the sharp spurs on the front tibia - maybe Bibio xanthopus? I like those large hairy eyes! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Opuntia; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Opuntia; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus

File.Name: c304c_c304-054.jpg
Notes:
6954357497 These are tiny native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) tending their aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera) on terminal stems of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. The ants are farmers! (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)

I'm not sure what kind of ants they are. I call them "piss-ants" because they have a strong smell when they get all over you in a tree. They might be the California Harvester Ant (Pogonomyrmex californicus) or kin.
CommonNames: ants; aphids; Coyote Bush; California Harvester Ant
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Pogonomyrmex californicus
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Aphididae; Homoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Pogonomyrmex californicus

File.Name: c304d_c304-019.jpg
Notes: 6808224008 Here are new flowers and leaves of deciduous native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family growing along San Jose Creek a mile from home. It was windy, but at least I documented the emergence of these interesting flowers. It's our only native maple, though we also have native Box Elder (Acer negundo var. californicum) growing along the river. There's a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the new flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)
CommonNames: Bigleaf Maple; Box Elder; Honey Bee
ScientificName: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Acer negundo; californicum; Apis mellifera
associatedTaxa: Acer macrophyllum; Aceraceae; Acer negundo; californicum; Apis mellifera

File.Name: c304e_c304-028.jpg
Notes:
6954284193 Here is Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in the mountains. The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. In the background is the Santa Barbara Channel, with the city of Goleta at the bottom and Santa Cruz Island on top. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)

The ripe fruit are tasty, though the unripe fruit are actually poisonous. The flesh is very thin. The indigenous Chumash people mostly ate the large nuts after leaching. I've tried them. They are tasty, like almonds!

File.Name: c304f_c304-036.jpg
Notes:
6808136074 This is a perfect new flower of native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflower in the Iridaceae plant family growing in the woods near home. Shouldn't it be named a yellow-eyed grass? This is the only native iris that grows in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)

It's wonderful to find new flowers like this in our drought year. We've only had about 30% of normal rainfall this year, and we're on track for the driest year since records began. There's no "March Miracle" rain in the forecast. It will be an interesting summer!

File.Name: c304g_c304-044.jpg
Notes:
6808076980 I found this tiny Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey today on a purple flower of native Chaparral Nightshade (Solanum xanti) in the Solanaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The spider is about 450 pixels long on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x450/4288 = 2.5 mm or 1/10 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

File.Name: c304h_c304-004.jpg
Notes: 6808025192 I believe this is a new furry gall of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but cp. Discholcapsis washingtonensis. It is growing on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. As they get older, the galls lose the white hairs and red colors, but these young galls are very beautiful. (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)
ScientificName: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Discholcapsis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Discholcapsis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c305a_c305-022.jpg
Notes:
6811808802 This is an early female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers of Woolly Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja foliolosa) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

I usually expect these butterflies in summer, but maybe these early ones lay the eggs that will make those summer butterflies. See my photo from exactly one year ago on 5 March 2011 at:
   http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/66834106/

File.Name: c305b_c305-011.jpg
Notes:
6811774678 I believe this impressive critter is a bristly Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae in the order Diptera. It's "puddling" on damp soil by a hose in our garden today. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

Adult Tachinid Flies visit flowers, but their larvae are parasitic on other insects in the woods and help control them. They are beneficial - except for the insects they parasitize!

File.Name: c305c_c305-020.jpg
Notes: 6811732544 This little spider has captured a March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera). It's resting on the pad of a Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden. I believe from its nearby web and eyes that the spider is a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae). (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)
CommonNames: spider; March Fly; Prickly Pear; Sheetweb Spider

File.Name: c305d_c305-001.jpg
Notes:
6811706706 This is another cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family on the dry edge of our garden this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

It's tiny - this is an 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo, taken at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. I think I need extension tubes!

File.Name: c305e_c304-011.jpg
Notes:
6811637536 This is a tiny Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. Maybe it's a Mining Bee in the family Andrenidae? (San Marcos Pass, 4 March 2012)

If you look, you can see the hairs that make up the "Pollen basket" on its hind leg - though I think this one was after nectar. It spent most of it's time at these base of the stamens.
CommonNames: Wild Bee; Wild Blackberry; Mining Bee
ScientificName: Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Andrenidae
associatedTaxa: Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Andrenidae

File.Name: c305f_c305-003.jpg
Notes: 6811616524 Here is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) soaking up some sun rays this morning. It's showing some of its wonderful mating colors. Spring is here! (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

File.Name: c305g_c305-038.jpg
Notes: 6957708269 This is a beautiful new flower of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family growing in the canyon, with the sun behind. The glandular flowers always remind me of ballet dancers "en pointe" with tight ballet shoes and petticoats in the air. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

File.Name: c305h_c305-027.jpg
Notes:
6811555448 This is a mature gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's growing on native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in the woods. I don't see and exit hole, so I reckon the wasp larvae is still in there. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

These galls are prettier when they're fresh. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/68222159/
CommonNames: Beaked Twig Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak

File.Name: c306a_c306-002.jpg
Notes:
6814515388 Here is a beautiful golden Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can clearly see its "spurious vein" if you know what to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 6 March 2012)

This looks a lot like another fly I posted a photo of at http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6878513781. But look closer. This fly seems a bit broader and heavier, and the fringe of hairs around its neck is gold not white.

If was cold and very windy today - too windy for photos, but I got lucky with this one. Then we had a bit of a plumbing emergency this afternoon. One photo will do just fine today.

File.Name: c307a_c307-008.jpg
Notes:
6963490727 I literally snatched this tiny "fly" from the air this morning, and dumped it on a leaf. In a few minutes, it recovered and walked away. It's not a "true fly" because it has four wings, so what is it?

I spent some time with my books and BugGuide, and I'm pretty sure this tiny critter is a Barklouse in the family Psocidae of the order Psocoptera. It has long, thin antennae and roof-like wings like a caddisfly. I suspect they are rarely photographed and little known. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2012)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The critter's body is about 428 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the insect is 23.6x428/4288 = 2.4 mm or 0.09 inches. This critter is really tiny!

This photo maybe also answers a "mystery photo" of the mine. The odd insect in http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6913910743 may well be an immature one of these.

File.Name: c307b_c307-011.jpg
Notes:
6963468859 This is some kind of Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidea, order Hymenoptera) perched for just a moment on a leaf of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2012)

I don't know anything else about it. Getting these photos in the woods every day is like casting a net into the ocean and bringing in a fish. I know nothing about the creature except that it was here, today. The real challenge is to understand their lives.

File.Name: c307c_c305-016.jpg
Notes: 6963440921 The odd "bottle gourd shape" of its abdomen suggests that this tiny spider is a "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae). I found it on a succulent plant in our garden, without any visible web. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

File.Name: c307d_c302-035.jpg
Notes: 6963422429 This is a gorgeous little peach-colored spider perched on a new flower bud of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Judging from its web and eyes, I guess it is a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. Its abdomen looks like a porcelain egg. (San Marcos Pass, 2 March 2012)

File.Name: c307e_7901-034.jpg
Notes:
6817246582 I was going through the archives looking for real Thread-legged Bugs (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Hemiptera today (see my [Previous] photo), and I did a double-take on this old photo.

This is NOT a Thread-legged Bug, but it sure looks similar. This is a toothpick-size and shape Walking Stick insect in the order Phasmatodea. It might be the Western Short-horned Walking Stick (Parabacillus hesperus). Look close and you can see the short antennae and long front legs. I found this critter on stems of flowering native California Aster (Lessingia filaginifolia aka Corethrogyne filaginifolia) flower in the (Asteraceae) plant family growing on a dry flat along the river. (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 1 September 2007)

I dig that cubist background!
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bugs; Assassin Bug; Thread-legged Bug; Walking Stick; Western Short-horned Walking Stick; California Aster
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Phasmatodea; Parabacillus hesperus; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Phasmatodea; Parabacillus hesperus; Lessingia filaginifolia; Corethrogyne filaginifolia; Asteraceae

File.Name: c307f_9921-089.jpg
Notes:
6817213014 I was looking through the archives for real Thread-legged Bugs (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order HemipteraHemiptera (see my [Previous] photo), and I found a few!

This insect is possibly genus Emesaya. Its body is about 1.5 inches long (4 cm). It's barely as large around as a broom straw. (San Marcos Pass, 21 September 2009)

I found this critter on the upstairs porch window screen when I got home from school. I carried him (or her) downstairs and got the photo on a sage plant. I wonder what habitat it's adapted for? (I guess grass - crane flies are similar.)

Look where the slender antennae meet the body at the bottom of the photo. That's the head with eyes. The front legs extend beyond. They have tiny spines for catching prey. Like all Reduviidae, these critters are predators, though I wonder what they catch?
Locality:
I guess grass - crane flies are similar.)

Look where the slender antennae meet the body at the bottom of the photo. That's the head with eyes. The front legs extend beyond. They have tiny spines for catching prey. Like all Reduviidae

File.Name: c307g_9827-053.jpg
Notes:
6963288657 I was looking through the archives for photos of real Thread-legged Bugs (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family in the order Hemiptera (see my [Previous] photo), and I found a few.

I got this photo on the dry flats along the Santa Ynez River a few years ago. The insect is prowling along a branch of composite Scale Broom (Lepidospartum squamatum). Maybe it is genus Emesaya? (Paradise, Santa Ynez River, 26 August 2009)

If you look close, you can see that it has no "clubs" on its antennae and it holds its front legs like the predator it is.
CommonNames: Thread-legged Bugs; Assassin Bug; Scale Broom
ScientificName: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Emesaya
associatedTaxa: Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Lepidospartum squamatum; Emesaya

File.Name: c307h_c104-003.jpg
Notes:
6817123800 A few days ago, JMDN corrected my mistaken ID of a Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae for a Thread-legged Bug (subfamily Emesinae) of the Assassin Bug (Reduviidae) family - both in the order Hemiptera. See my photo at www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6950814877.

I decided to go through my collection of Fotolog photos to look for real Thread-legged Bugs. I found a few!

Looking at BugGuide, I think this insect might be genus Empicoris. I found it I found it on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family above the retaining wall on our driveway where the sun never shines in winter. If you look close, you can see its sucking proboscis folded under its head. It has a beautiful checkered pattern. (San Marcos Pass, 4 January 2012)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification in the shade without flash, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The bug is about 633 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the insect is 23.6x633/4288 = 3.5 mm, about 1/8 inch. This critter is really tiny!
CommonNames: Stilt Bug; Thread-legged Bug; Assassin Bug; Fotolog; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Berytidae; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Empicoris; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Berytidae; Emesinae; Reduviidae; Hemiptera; Empicoris; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c308a_c308-002.jpg
Notes: 6819793536 This is a fantastic Sonoran Blue (Philotes sonorensis) butterfly perched on its larval food plant, the Southern California Dudleya (Dudleya lanceolata) in the succulent Crassulaceae plant family. She's not ovipositing, but I'll bet she's thinking about it - if that darn photographer would just leave! (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2012)

File.Name: c308b_c308-010.jpg
Notes: 6819751148 I found this beautiful large Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a new flower of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family by the creek today. Usually I don't try to ID skippers (I just "skip her"), but this one might well be an early Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2012)
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: c308c_c308-026.jpg
Notes:
6965809247 I happened to notice another tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods today perched on an old grass seed head. I was getting a few photos, when suddenly it extended its front legs. Maybe it sensed that I was close? (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2012)

This tick might well be a Western black-legged tick (Ixodes pacificus), which is the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast.

The tick was in deep shade so I used my on-camera flash for this photo. It's just waiting (and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me! Ticks can spend weeks or months just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that!

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The tick is about 550 pixels across on my 4288 pixel original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x550/4288 = 3 mm, less than 1/8 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!
Locality:
and waiting...) for a suitable host to come along - like you or me! Ticks can spend weeks or months just waiting... like an extended moment that passes just like that!

I got this photo near 1:1 magnification

File.Name: c308d_c308-014.jpg
Notes:
6819640008 Here is a pretty Popcorn Flower (genus (Cryptantha or Plagiobothrys) in the Boraginaceae plant family. You need a microscope to tell these flowers apart, and I really don't care. There's a tiny ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) crawling above the flower. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2012)

This plant is so small! *Sigh* I'm sure it's a sign of our very dry "rainy season" this year. Around the plant are the really tiny reddish succulent plants of Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. I'll save those for another photo.
ScientificName: Cryptantha; Plagiobothrys; Boraginaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Crassula connata; Crassulaceae
associatedTaxa: Cryptantha; Plagiobothrys; Boraginaceae; Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Crassula connata; Crassulaceae

File.Name: c308e_c308-011.jpg
Notes: 6819627612 These are the first new flowers of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon. Like all lupines, they have hand-shaped "palmate" leaves - like holding your hand open with fingers extended. We have many different species. This one is perennial, with semi-whorled flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2012)

File.Name: c308f_c308-018.jpg
Notes:
6819575910 These are the small umbrella-like sporophytes of native Liverworts (Marchantiophyta) on a sandstone outcrop in the canyon. They might be the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know. (San Marcos Pass, 8 March 2012)

The plants are starting to lose their color, and their leafy thallus (thalli?) is dried up into a black rind at the base. Our dry weather this year is taking a toll in the woods.
ScientificName: sporophytes; Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; thallus; thalli
associatedTaxa: sporophytes; Marchantiophyta; Asterella californica; thallus; thalli

File.Name: c308g_c305-037.jpg
Notes:
6819532622 This is a succulent new gall forming on a twig of native Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in the woods. I'm not sure what kind of gall it is, but I suspect it's a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera because of the off-kilter shape and the incipient yellow spots. (San Marcos Pass, 5 March 2012)

See my photo at: http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/68222159/
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c308h_c307-003.jpg
Notes:
6819513372 This is another photo of the tiny "fly" I snatched from the air yesterday. See my [Previous] photo. Here it is laying stunned or wary on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family.

I believe it is a Barklouse in the family Psocidae of the order Psocoptera. It has long, thin antennae and roof-like wings like a caddisfly. You can make out the ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up its compound eyes in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 7 March 2012)
ScientificName: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Psocidae; Psocoptera; ommatidia
associatedTaxa: Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Psocidae; Psocoptera; ommatidia

File.Name: c309a_c309-008.jpg
Notes:
6822347770 This is a shockingly green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly perched for the moment on a flower head of non-native English Plantain (Plantago lanceolata) in the Plantaginaceae plant family. It's host plant is native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plants in the Fabaceae plant family, growing nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

I can't think of any other GREEN local butterfly. This beauty is unique.
ScientificName: Callophrys dumetorum; Plantago lanceolata; Plantaginaceae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Callophrys dumetorum; Plantago lanceolata; Plantaginaceae; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c309b_c309-030.jpg
Notes: 6822325628 I guess this is a new Mournful Duskywing (Erynnis tristis) butterfly because of the brown colors, but cp the Funereal Duskywing (E. funeralis). Both skippers are dark with a white fringe on their hindwings. This one is perched on a flower head of Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)
CommonNames: Mournful Duskywing; Funereal Duskywing; Hoary-leaved Ceanothus
ScientificName: Erynnis tristis; E. funeralis; Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae
associatedTaxa: Erynnis tristis; E. funeralis; Ceanothus crassifolius; Rhamnaceae

File.Name: c309c_c309-032.jpg
Notes:
6822239024 This is an interesting spider on a native plant of Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plants in the Fabaceae plant family. I believe it is an unusual (though locally common) Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. It has a "thimble-like" web and only 6 eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

I was just about to get a photo of the interesting web, when a big sloppy "lab" dog stuck its nose in the photo - wiping out everyting! Another hiker came into my spot with his dogs. I'll try again another day.

File.Name: c309d_c309-046.jpg
Notes:
6822221204 This is a little Click Beetle (family Elateridae in the order Coleoptera) on a blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on our driveway. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

This was my last photo today as the sun was fading. I actually took 30+ photos, with and without flash, to get this one keeper - without flash.

File.Name: c309e_c309-029.jpg
Notes:
6822175026 There are few plants more beautiful than the native thistles! And few plants uglier than the non-native weedy thistles.

I believe (in this place) that this native thistle is the California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a variety of "Cobweb Thistle" with white flowers. Time will tell. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

File.Name: c309f_c309-013.jpg
Notes:
6822159444 These are new flowers of native Fuschia-flowered Gooseberry (Ribes speciosum) shrub in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The shiny leaves are pretty too. They are a classic hummingbird flower, out of reach for most insects. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

Getting this photo was pretty precarious - I was perched on a steep hillside, trying not to slip down!

File.Name: c309g_c309-006.jpg
Notes: 6822127398 These are new flowers of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plants in the Fabaceae plant family. They're a butterfly plant - mostly the the brilliant green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterflies. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012) They are flowering now, but I don't think they'll last long in this drought year.

File.Name: c309h_c309-022.jpg
Notes:
6822110780 Here's another photo of new flowers of Snowball aka Hoary-leaved Ceanothus (Ceanothus crassifolius) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Here you can see the "corky stipules" - those round dark things that look like little brown coconuts at the base of the opposite leaves. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

This is another of the white-flowered Ceanothus shrubs that cover our mountains every spring. The individual flowers are quite intricate. This one is rather uncommon in the mountains - but it is common when you find it!

File.Name: c310a_c310-019.jpg
Notes:
6825392546 This must be the smallest wildflower in our woods - Sand Pygmyweed (Crassula connata) in the Crassulaceae plant family. It's a rarely-noticed tiny succulent that grows on bare ground in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2012)

I stuck my pen in the photo at the lower-right. My pen has a 0.7 mm ball, which is pretty close to the size of these flowers.

File.Name: c310b_c310-018.jpg
Notes:
6971489247 This is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a new flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2012)

Pretty as they are, these native flowers don't seem to attract many insects. I guess it will be a long wait.

File.Name: c310c_c310-032.jpg
Notes:
6971422293 These are interesting native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) doing *something* on a bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2012)

These ants might be genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. They have two spines on their thorax and an interesting attachment to their abdomen.

"Live Oak Jam" sound like a music tape, but oak trees can make real honey. See my photo at:
   http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/67943278/
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: c310d_c310-038.jpg
Notes:
6971378771 Looking at BugGuide, I'm sure Terraincognita96 is right - this is not a wild bee, but a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably in the genus Abia. One indication is that it has no hairs on it's legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that. Lonicera may well be its host plant.

Original comment: "This is an interesting Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) perched on a leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2012)"

I won't try to guess what kind of bee it is, but if you know I would appreciate some help!
CommonNames: Cimbicid Sawfly; Wild Bee; California Honeysuckle
ScientificName: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Lonicera; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Lonicera; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Lonicera hispidula; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: c310e_c310-036.jpg
Notes:
6971353291 Here is a tiny caterpillar (Lepidoptera) on a leaf of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. The sun was "going down" - at least going down behind the trees - when I got this photo this afternoon. I like the light. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2012)

It's interesting how digital photos get "grainy" in low light, like shooting with Tri-x. I like it!

File.Name: c310f_c310-008.jpg
Notes: 6825207026 New flowers of native Milk Maids aka Toothwort (Cardamine californica) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a perennial mustard. It has an edible root (the "tooth"), but I really don't care to dig it up. It tastes like a radish, not worth the pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 10 March 2012)

File.Name: c310g_c309-035.jpg
Notes:
6825127250 These flower buds of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family look fully-formed , but they still haven't opened for business. The buds have been on the plant for months. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

It's an interesting strategy to form buds early. Several native plants do it, but most don't. I wonder if there's an advantage, or is this just how it's always been done?

File.Name: c310h_c309-043.jpg
Notes:
6971185557 These are the female pistillate "flowers" of a native Veatch Silk-Tassel (Garrya veatchii) shrub in the Garryaceae plant family. You can see the pistils on the developing fruit. (San Marcos Pass, 9 March 2012)

The male flowers of these dioecious native shrub are more photogenic. Like many native plants, the males mature before the females. This is known as protandry - male function comes before female function. See my photo from January 5 at:
   http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/68415770/

File.Name: c311a_c311-035.jpg
Notes:
6828789736 An old mop? Dreadlock warrior?

This is an odd Mealybug Destroyer (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) insect. I'm pretty sure it's the larva of a Dusky Lady Beetle in the tribe Scymnini of the Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae) family of the Coleoptera. It's been munching aphids on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)

It was cloudy today, but not too breezy. I'll take clouds over wind any day!
CommonNames: Mealybug Destroyer; Dusky Lady Beetle; Lady Beetle; Coyote Bush
ScientificName: Cryptolaemus montrouzieri; Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Cryptolaemus montrouzieri; Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c311b_c311-040.jpg
Notes:
6828720508 Ants, apids ... and a little white Mealybug Destroyer (Cryptolaemus montrouzieri) insect! I found this scene on native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)

The Mealybug Destroyer is the larva of a Dusky Lady Beetle in the tribe Scymnini of the Lady Beetle (Coccinellidae) family of the Coleoptera. See my [Next] photo.

It's so interesting that the ant attending the aphid seems to be snuggling the destroyer. Maybe it tastes like an aphid?

This is definitely my favorite photo today!
CommonNames: Mealybug Destroyer; Coyote Bush; Mealybug Destroyer; Dusky Lady Beetle; Lady Beetle
ScientificName: Cryptolaemus montrouzieri; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera
associatedTaxa: Cryptolaemus montrouzieri; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Scymnini; Coccinellidae; Coleoptera

File.Name: c311c_c311-002.jpg
Notes:
6974774101 This is a tiny Lace Bug in the family Tingidae of the order Hemiptera on a leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)

They have a remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor, but underneath all that armor is a skinny little bug. *Sigh* I'll bet we all feel like that some days!

File.Name: c311d_c311-013.jpg
Notes:
6974747563 Be careful little wing, these flowers have teeth!

Here is a Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a fading flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)

It must be tough for these spiders that perch on flowers that only stays open for a day or two!

File.Name: c311e_c311-029.jpg
Notes: 6974718607 This is a brilliant red flower of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing on the hillside across from our mailbox. The red "petals" are mostly leafy bracts, and the real flowers are the slender reddish tubes with protruding pistils. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)

File.Name: c311f_c311-021.jpg
Notes:
6974678739 The flowers look like they belong under water in a tropical coral reef somewhere!

These are new flowers of native Holly-leaved Cherry (Prunus ilicifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in the mountains. The old spanish name for this plant is Islay. If you crush a leaf between your fingers, you can smell the wild cherry. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012) Do you notice something missing? This flower cluster lacks female pistils. I don't know why.

File.Name: c311g_c311-043.jpg
Notes:
6974606919 These are tiny buds and flowers of Galium nuttallii in the Rubiacae plant family. Everything comes in fours with these tiny flowers on the whorled plant stems. (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)
