Galium plants are kind of "collector's plants" in our mountains. We have many different species with tiny flowers that all look the same, and they are difficult to key out. I'll try to make a collection this spring.

File.Name: c311h_c311-011.jpg
Notes:
6974537057 I actually found a mushroom today in the drought-dry woods!

I guess this is the Woodland Agaricus (Agaricus silicola) mushroom with patches on its ring. It doesn't have a thick felty ring (like A. hondensis) or a yellow-staining base (like A. xanthodermus). It is weakly yellow-staining. It's said to be ebible, but I'm wary of all these woodland Agaricus mushrooms after a bad time several years ago. Caveat emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)
ScientificName: Agaricus silicola; A. hondensis; A. xanthodermus; Caveat emptor
associatedTaxa: Agaricus silicola; A. hondensis; A. xanthodermus; Caveat emptor

File.Name: c312a_c312-019.jpg
Notes:
6978261089 Here is a little "true bug" in the order Hemiptera on new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. Maybe the little bug is a Plant Bug in the family Miridae? (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2012)

This is the most beautiful Ceanothus in our mountains, though it doesn't cover the hills like some of other Ceanothus species. The leaves are three-veined beneath, and rimmed with tiny glandular drops. The flowers have a juicy green glandular "biscuit" at the base of the flower to attract pollinators.

It was cloudy AND windy today. Not the best day for photos, but I did alright.

File.Name: c312b_c312-029.jpg
Notes:
6978227999 This is native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family starting to flower along San Jose Creek a mile from home. New flowers and leaves erupt from the large buds. They are not common. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2012)

The flowers are monoecious with separate male and female flowers on the same raceme. Look close left of center. The female (or bi-) flowers have pretty red structures that will become the characteristic wings of the samara seeds that flutter down like little helicopters when you shake them from the branches in autumn.

File.Name: c312c_c312-021.jpg
Notes: 6978195225 Here are new flowers of native Chaparral Lotus (Lotus grandiflorus) in the Fabaceae) plant family. They are a pretty fire follower plant growing in a place that burned a few years ago in the Gap Fire. The plants grows large, several feet tall. With age, the yellow flowers turn red. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2012)

File.Name: c312d_c312-026.jpg
Notes: 6832051306 These are new flowers of Wild Cucumber aka Chilicothe or California Manroot (Marah macrocarpus in the Cucurbitaceae family) growing in the mountains. At least I believe it is M. macrocarpus and not M. fabaceus - the species are similar, and confusing. The flowers look like they've been dipped in sugar! (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2012)
ScientificName: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus
associatedTaxa: Marah macrocarpus; Cucurbitaceae; M. macrocarpus; M. fabaceus

File.Name: c312e_c312-022.jpg
Notes: 6978158735 These are surprising native flowers of native Common Rush-Rose (Helianthemum scoparium) in the Cistaceae) plant family. It's surprising because we also have several non-native species of this family in the mountains. It's a bushy perennial that grows in dry places, often on burns. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2012)

File.Name: c312f_c311-010.jpg
Notes: 6831996494 These are almost-ripe berries of native Chaparral Currant (Ribes malvaceum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. They turn purple when they are ripe, but the birds usually get them first. The berries have pin-head glands like the rest of the plant. They are edible when ripe, but they don't taste great. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2012)

File.Name: c312g_c311-004.jpg
Notes:
6978006147 This is an odd little critter - maybe a tiny Moth Fly in the subfamily Psychodinae of the family Psychodidae of the Lepidoptera? They are also known as "Drain Flies" or "Sewage Flies". Maybe it has something to do with the fact that we had our septic tank pumped last week! (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)

This is an 800x600 crop from my original 4288x2848 pixel original photo. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The moth is about 320 pixels across on my original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x320/4288 = 1.8 mm, a bit more than 1/16 of an inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

(Note to self - get extension tubes!)

File.Name: c312h_c311-018.jpg
Notes:
6831827548 This is a chubby little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a stem of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family yesterday. Could it be a Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 11 March 2012)

This is an 800x600 crop from my original 4288x2848 pixel original photo. I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, so I can use my digital camera as an expensive micrometer. The wasp is about 530 pixels across on my original photo, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x530/4288 = 2.9 mm, less than 1/8 inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!
CommonNames: Wasp; Wild Sweetpea; Cynipid Gall Wasp

File.Name: c313a_c313-016.jpg
Notes:
6980998905 This is a tiny Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - probably a Katydid (family Tettigoniidae of the order Orthoptera). It's resting on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. Good luck little critter, I wish you well! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2012)

The good news today is that we night get some real rain this weekend! We need it.
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Katydid; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Orthoptera; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: c313b_c313-028.jpg
Notes: 6980974921 This is a beautiful male March Fly (family Bibionidae, order Diptera) resting for a moment on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek this morning. I guess this is a male because of those huge eyes, and genus Bibio because of the tibial spurs. It might well be Bibio xanthopus. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2012)
ScientificName: Bibionidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus
associatedTaxa: Bibionidae; Diptera; Quercus agrifolia; Bibio; Bibio xanthopus

File.Name: c313c_c313-029.jpg
Notes:
6980920593 I found this odd tiny fly (order Diptera) perched for a moment on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree by the creek this morning. Look at those (relatively) huge paddle-like antennae! I only got one chance for a photo before it took off. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2012)

I guess it's a Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae because it looks quite bristly. It really is tiny - this is an 800x600 crop from my original 4288x2848 pixel original photo taken near 1:1 magnification.
CommonNames: fly; Coast Live Oak; Tachinid Fly

File.Name: c313d_c313-002.jpg
Notes: 6980899751 I believe this is a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera. It's perched on an old post in our garden trying to warm up on a cold morning. They nest under our eaves at home, and they never bother us. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2012)

File.Name: c313e_c313-007.jpg
Notes:
6980810135 I've been looking for these caterpillars of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in our woods. They favor "Scrophs" - plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family, like this Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2012)

These caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing in the photo.. Sometimes they shroud the penstemon leaves like "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind.

The little caterpillars spin their webs on the host plants in summer. Then they form tough skins and drop to the forest floor to over-winter. They come back about now in the Spring to feed and pupate into adults. It's a strange cycle, but it works for them.

File.Name: c313f_c313-013.jpg
Notes:
6980783839 I found this cute little spider perched with legs-over-head on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. I'm pretty sure from the web and eyes that it's a tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae. (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2012)

If you don't know it, take a look at the useful BugGuide page on "Spider Eye Arrangements":
   http://bugguide.net/node/view/84423
CommonNames: spider; Bush Monkey Flower; Orb-Weaver Spider

File.Name: c313g_c313-037.jpg
Notes:
6834629114 This is a beautiful new "fiddlehead" of Western Bracken Fern (Pteridium aquilinum var. pubescens) in the woods. It will become a new fern frond. They are edible - or are they? There are reports of cattle and horse poisoning. Caveat Emptor! (San Marcos Pass, 13 March 2012)

It's easy to over-expose these ferns, like spider webs. This photo is under-exposed by 1-2/3 stops from what my camera thought was right.

File.Name: c313h_c312-017.jpg
Notes: 6980737035 Here is another photo of the beautiful new flowers of native Hairy Ceanothus (Ceanothus oliganthus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The flowers come in compact clusters rather than the open clusters of Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) and the leaves are three-veined underneath. They are the deepest blue of our native Ceanothus species in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 12 March 2012)
ScientificName: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus oliganthus; Rhamnaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Ceanothus

File.Name: c314a_c314-006.jpg
Notes:
6837587868 These are brilliant blue native flowers of Larkspur in the genus Delphinium of the Ranunculaceae plant family. But are they D. patens or D. parryi? I found them in the woods near Lake Cachuma today growing on a shaded shale slope. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2012)

I got more wildflower photos today, but they'll wait until tomorrow. Wednesday is my busy day, with my class at Dunn Middle School followed this week by a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting. I'm tired. Two photos will do just fine today.
CommonNames: Larkspur; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society
ScientificName: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; D. parryi
associatedTaxa: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; D. parryi

File.Name: c314b_c314-014.jpg
Notes: 6983696121 Here is a cluster of well-named native Shooting Star (Dodecatheon clevelandii) wildflowers in the Primulaceae plant family growing in the woods by Lake Cachuma today. They are really beautiful. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2012)

File.Name: c315a_c315-002.jpg
Notes:
6986357357 I believe these are new furry galls of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but cp. Discholcapsis washingtonensis. They are growing on twigs of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family.

As they get older, the galls lose their white hairs and red colors (just like me), but these young galls are strange and beautiful.
ScientificName: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Discholcapsis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Discholcapsis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c315b_c315-019.jpg
Notes:
6986342779 This is furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's perched on a dead leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2012)

This is a tight crop. I tried to get closer, but it flew away.

File.Name: c315c_c315-021.jpg
Notes:
6986288047 This is not a wild bee, though it looks vaguely bee-like. It's a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably in the genus Abia. One indication is that it has no hairs on it's legs. It also has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that.

See my photo and the comment by Terraincognita96 at:
   www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6971378771/

Original comment:
"This is an interesting Wild Bee (superfamily Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) perched on a new leaf of Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. I'm not sure what kind of bee it is. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2012)
ScientificName: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: c315d_c315-012.jpg
Notes:
6840134566 I'm not one to post photos of my lunch everyday, but today's lunch was remarkable.

From the top around to the right, we had tossed salad, beet salad - and native Abalone (Haliotis) steaks, served with Rangpur limes that look like oranges. This is not our usual lunch. Thanks, Matt. (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2012)

File.Name: c315e_c314-027.jpg
Notes:
6986216365 I found this this harmless and beneficial Gopher Snake (Pituophis catenifer) catching some rays in a sunny spot on the chaparral. Note the charcoal on the ground. This place burned 10+ years ago. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2012)

The cheeks of rattlesnakes are much more abruptly rounded, and the pattern of scales is different. There's no reason to confuse them.

File.Name: c315f_c314-023.jpg
Notes:
6986199073 These are beautiful and mysterious new seeds of native Hairy Lacepod (Thysanocarpus curvipes) in the Brassicaceae plant family, with the sun behind. With this backlight, the seeds look like fractal designs. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2012)

It's another mustard with tiny white flowers and large seeds. You can strip the seeds off with your fingers and eat them as a snack or a salad garnish.

File.Name: c315g_c314-010.jpg
Notes:
6840059824 There is no blue quite like the blue of these Baby Blue-Eyes (Nemophila menziesii) flowering in the woods near Lake Cachuma. This native flower is part of the difficult Hydrophyllaceae plant family. Nothing else has a color like this. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2012)

These particular flowers are probably the variety integrifolia with smaller flowers and stamen filaments that are longer than the corolla tube.

File.Name: c315h_c314-008.jpg
Notes:
6986123973 This is looking close at a beautiful blue native flower of the Larkspur in the genus Delphinium of the Ranunculaceae plant family in my [Previous] photo. I'm still not sure if they are D. patens or D. parryi, but it hardly matters. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 14 March 2012)

The flowers are remarkable. You mostly notice the 5 petal-like sepals on the outside of the flower. There are also 4 real petals surrounding the central column of stamens and pistils. Beautiful!
ScientificName: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; D. parryi
associatedTaxa: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; D. parryi

File.Name: c316a_c316-015.jpg
Notes:
6988753427 This photo should look scary!

It's a another tiny Tick (superfamily Ixodoidea of the Arachnida) in the woods today, perched on an old grass seed head. I had to use my on-camera flash to freeze the moment. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2012)

This tick might well be a Western black-legged tick (Ixodes pacificus), which is the principal vector of Lyme Disease on the West Coast. It looks like it's reaching out for me!

No rain yet, but we have gathering clouds and wind. I did manage to find a few moment of sun and calm for my photos today. Rain and maybe snow is expected tonight and tomorrow. Bring it on!

File.Name: c316b_c316-019.jpg
Notes:
6988723607 Rain is the usual trigger for these tiny Termites (order Isoptera) to form winged "alates" and fly off into the unknown - but this one is a day early. Rain in expected tonight, but here it is today! It's perched on a serpentine rock on our porch. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2012)

It's a sign of how much we need this promised rain. We stand at 8.52 inches, just 29% of normal. It's our driest year ever.

File.Name: c316c_c316-001.jpg
Notes:
6988652409 Well, you can't fault me for trying to get this photo!

This is a tiny Whitefly - family Aleyrodidae in the order Homoptera. It's perched on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods this morning. I had to reduce my exposure several stops to get this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2012)

These critters are really tiny. This is this is an 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo, taken at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. The whitefly is about 275 pixels high from head to wingtip, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x320/4288 = 1.5 mm, a bit less than 1/16 of an inch. Like I said, this critter is tiny!

I think I need extension tubes...

File.Name: c316d_c316-008.jpg
Notes:
6988635301 This is a fierce-looking Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey on a spent flower native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods this morning. I don't know what prey it expects to find here. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2012)

This might well be the same spider I've photographed before:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6974747563

File.Name: c316e_c316-009.jpg
Notes:
6988604307 Here is another tiny Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae, perched on a native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2012)

I wonder what are those two reddish bumps on the photo-down side of it's abdomen where its epigynum should be?

File.Name: c316f_c316-004.jpg
Notes: 6842469800 This is a different tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae). It didn't have any web to speak of, but I recognize that odd bottle-like hump on its back. It's perched on a dead flower head of of Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2012)
CommonNames: Garbage Line Spider; Orb-Weaver Spiders; Canyon Sunflower

File.Name: c316g_c316-011.jpg
Notes:
6988550955 This is a tiny "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae) perched on it's inner web - with dew drops. (San Marcos Pass, 16 March 2012)

The spider is perched on its "stabilimentum" - that conspicuous zig-zag structure in its web. There's some controversy about its function:
   http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Web_decorations

File.Name: c316h_c315-015.jpg
Notes: 6988537433 I found this little spider yesterday on an old leaf of Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods. There was no sign of a web, so I suspect it is a Wolf Spider (Lycosidae). (San Marcos Pass, 15 March 2012)

File.Name: c317a_c317-023.jpg
Notes:
6991676069 This is a fantastic little Crane Fly in the Tipulidae family of the order Diptera resting on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the woods this morning after our good rain last night. I happened to see it land, otherwise I never would have noticed it. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

Today's photos are all that the rain brings... We had 3-3/4 inch of rain, mostly last night. It was a good soak, just what we needed. Now this is only the 2nd driest year on record, after 2006-7. I was worried about our oak trees come the long, hot summer. Now I feel better.

On the other hand, climate (as opposed to weather) is all about averages. Our native flora has been through dry years before - that's why it's still here! This dry year has been a chance to see new adaptations of familiar plants.

(Update 8:45 pm - it's snowing!)

File.Name: c317b_c317-006.jpg
Notes:
6991643447 Here are new flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - with raindrops from our good rain last night. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

We had showers and bouts of hail today, but most of our 3-3/4 inch of rain came last night. It was a good soak, just what we needed. Now this is only the 2nd driest year on record! (After 2006-7)

File.Name: c317c_c317-010.jpg
Notes:
6991610935 These are beautiful "flowers" - really staminate catkins - of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Sometimes the catkins are yellow, other times they have more red. The combination of the colors in the woods is gorgeous! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

I can't say that the rain last night brought them out, but I sure don't remember seeing them yesterday!

File.Name: c317d_c317-019.jpg
Notes:
6845437186 I found this soggy mushroom in the woods today after our rain last night. Mushrooms are fast, but not that fast - but I swear it wasn't there yesterday. It must have already been in the soil as a "mushroom egg" all ready to roll.

It's definitely an Amanita mushroom with a "universal veil" cup at the base, but there's no sign of a "partial veil" ring on the stem. The cap is white. I'm not sure what it is. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

File.Name: c317e_c317-030.jpg
Notes: 6845406616 This is looking under the cap of the (I think) Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushroom in my [Previous] photo. I sliced the gills on the left, and you can see that it has watery white latex. They don't have the maple-syrup smell of L. fragilis, though they are still said to be good to eat. I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

File.Name: c317f_c317-032.jpg
Notes: 6991513301 It rained last night and today, and already there are new mushrooms in the woods! I believe this is a Rufous Candy Cap (Lactarius rufulus) mushroom in our garden. See my [Next] photo. They don't have the maple-syrup smell of L. fragilis, though they are still said to be good to eat. I haven't tried them. (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

File.Name: c317g_c317-016.jpg
Notes:
6991471459 These are tiny "umbrellas" of a Liverwort (Marchantiophyta) plant in the canyon - maybe the California Liverwort (Asterella californica), but I don't really know.

Compare my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6819575910

The "thallus" leaves of this liverwort has already greened up after our rain last night. These are real "resurrection plants". (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

File.Name: c317h_c317-026.jpg
Notes:
6991405141 This is a group of tiny Pixie Cup (Cladonia) lichens growing on a sandstone boulder in the canyon today. They are already waking up from the rain last night! (San Marcos Pass, 17 March 2012)

Lichens are composite organisms that are both fungi and algae. This makes reproduction difficult. The lichens in my photo seem to be covered in a greenish powder, which I reckon are soredia reproductive structures - tiny bundles of a few algal cells surrounded by a tangle of fungal hyphae. Wind and rain carry them away to make more lichens. Simple, but it works for them.

File.Name: c318a_c318-009.jpg
Notes:
6995481661 There's a demon face on these hindwings of the brilliant Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly I found along our road today! See my [Previous] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

We ended up with just under 4 inches of rain and snow from our storm this weekend. The real "March Miracle" rain happened in March 1991 when we got 21.5 inches of rain in one week. We've only had 12.4 inches of rain this year, but the latest storm helps a lot.

File.Name: c318b_c318-005.jpg
Notes:
6849307808 I found this brilliant Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly on our road today on a cold morning after a bit of snow. It looks like it wants to be pinned in a collection! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

I moved it to the oak leaves off the road. It must be newly emerged with those fabulous colors. It was a bad day for a coming out - with rain and snow, and a few moments of brilliant sunshine, like this. I came back after my hike, and it was gone.

If it looks a little weird, it's because I flipped the photo to look "more natural".

File.Name: c318c_c318-014.jpg
Notes: 6849286702 Here are new flowers of the Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing in the canyon. The common name is a mystery to me, but the species name makes sense, because most leaves are "truncate" - square on the ends. I prefer the name "Blunt-leaved Lupine". (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

File.Name: c318d_c318-017.jpg
Notes: 6849229032 These are beautiful new leaves on a frond of native Coffee Fern (Pellea andromedifolia) in the canyon today. I have no idea how this fern gets it's name. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

File.Name: c318e_c318-027.jpg
Notes: 6849208140 These are beautiful little sporophytes of a native Moss (Bryophyta) in the woods today. I don't think they could have fruited since our rain yesterday, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

File.Name: c318f_c318-028.jpg
Notes:
6995223903 I guess this is a Tricholoma mushroom in the woods. The notched gills are white, and they develop sordid reddish-brown spots as they mature. It has a faint "farinaceous" smell - like linseed oil. It may be part of the Tricholoma pessundatum group, not recommended for the table. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

This one has pushed up a non-native plant of Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family - doing it's part to eliminate weeds!
ScientificName: Tricholoma; Tricholoma pessundatum; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Tricholoma; Tricholoma pessundatum; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae

File.Name: c318g_c318-021.jpg
Notes:
6849022406 These are strange Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica) jelly mushrooms growing on a dead branch of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) on the ground. They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. They have a remarkable ability to revive after a rain. It rained yesterday, and here they are in the morning. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

The other fungus in the photo is a resupinate Crust Fungus - some kind of the genus Stereum. My book says that "Most Tremellas are frequently found with Stereum, suggesting some kind of relationship between the two.
CommonNames: Witch's Butter; Coast Live Oak; Crust Fungus

File.Name: c318h_c318-001.jpg
Notes: 6848980744 This is some kind of "Bracket Fungus" in the woods today - with tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects. I don't know what kind of fungus it is. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

File.Name: c319a_c319-026.jpg
Notes:
6998805985 I think there are laws about this in some U.S. states!

These tiny Weevils (Curculionidae) are doing *something* with this tiny fly (order Diptera) - but WTF are they doing? The fly sure seems to be presenting its "pig-nose" hindparts to the weevil. The fly had the clinging way of a Freeloader Fly (Milichiidae). My first thought was of "cleaner fish" in the ocean. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2012)

This scene is taking place on the shady-side of a flower bud of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family.

These critters are really tiny. I got another photo at 1:1 magnification, and I figure the size is just 390 pixels, about 2.15 mm, much less than 1/8 inch.

Tonight at 10:14 PDT is the real start of Spring! The vernal equinox happens at 5:14 GMT/UTC - that's tomorrow in England, but tonight here in California. The seasons change, life goes on...
Locality:
Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family.

These critters are really tiny. I got another photo at 1:1 magnification
CommonNames: Weevils; fly; Freeloader Fly; Wild Sweetpea
ScientificName: Curculionidae; Diptera; Milichiidae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Curculionidae; Diptera; Milichiidae; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c319b_c319-021.jpg
Notes: 6998784655 These tiny Weevils (Curculionidae) are doing *something* with this tiny fly (order Diptera) on the shady-side of a flower bud of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family - but what are they doing?? See my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2012)

File.Name: c319c_c319-025.jpg
Notes: 6852614692 These tiny Weevils (Curculionidae) are attracted to this tiny fly (order Diptera) on the shady-side of a flower bud of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. What are they doing?? See my [Next] two photos. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2012)

File.Name: c319d_c319-005.jpg
Notes: 6852549358 This is another "collector's plant" in our mountains - native Fairy Mist (Pterostegia drymarioides) in the Polygonaceae plant family. It grows close to the ground. It's an easy plant to miss, though the red foliage is attractive. I also missed the new "flower" left of center until I got this photo on my computer screen. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2012)

File.Name: c319e_c319-027.jpg
Notes: 6852529602 Here is a small "jelly-like" fungus growing from the end of a pine branch that I brought home from Figueroa Mountain a couple of months ago because it had interesting lichens. I don't know what the fungus is. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2012)

File.Name: c319f_c319-003.jpg
Notes: 6852478456 This is another "gelatinous blob" of Black Witch's Butter (Exidia glandulosa) in the Tremellales group of fungi. It's easy to miss this dark "mushroom" growing on dark fallen wood. (San Marcos Pass, 19 March 2012)

File.Name: c319g_c318-023.jpg
Notes:
6998550273 This is a "gelatinous blob" of Jelly Fungus (Tremelliales and allies) in the woods yesterday after our good rain. I suspect it's Witch's Butter (Tremella mesenterica), though it's interesting that sometimes it dries clear (like this) and sometimes it dries yellow. (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

They are said to be edible and tasteless - no surprise, they're mostly water. The other fungus in the photo is a resupinate Crust Fungus - some kind of the genus Stereum. My book says that "Most Tremellas are frequently found with Stereum, suggesting some kind of relationship between the two.
CommonNames: Jelly Fungus; Witch's Butter; Crust Fungus

File.Name: c319h_c318-003.jpg
Notes:
6998524295 This is how I found the beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly in my [Previous] two photos. It was foundering on the chip-seal road in the canyon. I picked it up by its forewings and moved it off the dangerous road to some nearby oak leaves - which happened to be in the sun for better photos! (San Marcos Pass, 18 March 2012)

I came back later, and the butterfly was gone. I reckon my rescue operation worked.

File.Name: c320a_c320-032.jpg
Notes:
7001865609 This is a fantastic furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today. It's quite white, and a bit over-exposed. It was visiting flowers of Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) in the Ericaceae plant family. Maybe it's genus Bombylius, but it's different from our usual brown species. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

I've been at a disadvantage tonight trying to write comments while a couple of my favorite classic movies are on TV in the same room - "Gold Diggers Of 1933" and "My Man Godfrey"! (TCM of course.)
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ericaceae; Bombylius
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ericaceae; Bombylius

File.Name: c320b_c320-017.jpg
Notes:
6855713710 I found this Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly this morning perched on a shrub of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's trying to warm up on a cold morning. Look at its long proboscis! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

I wonder if this is the same butterfly I rescued from the road two days ago? See my photo at:
   www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6998524295

File.Name: c320c_c320-034.jpg
Notes:
6855681154 This is not the best photo, but it's the first Brown Elfin (Callophrys augustinus) butterfly I've seen this year. It's been visiting flowers of 54% normal rainfall in the Ericaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

It's a hairstreak butterfly without tails, though it still rubs its hind wings together like any other hairstreak. It looks brown from a distance, but up close it looks more like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches (including violet) that combine to make an eye-catching brown.

This is an an 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo - taken from some distance away. I tried to get closer, but it took off. New butterflies are especially camera-shy!
Locality:
including violet) that combine to make an eye-catching brown.

This is an an 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo - taken from some distance away. I tried to get closer

File.Name: c320d_c320-029.jpg
Notes: 7001748217 This is a tiny "micro-moth" (order Lepidoptera) resting on a leaflet of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family growing in our canyon. It has beautiful gold colors, and "blue dunn" hackles on its neck. I don't know what kind of moth it is - maybe family Gelechiidae? (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

File.Name: c320e_c320-040.jpg
Notes: 7001710687 This is a cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on a leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) in the Fagaceae plant family in the canyon this morning. It's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

File.Name: c320f_c320-026.jpg
Notes: 7001691355 These are Honey Bees (Apis mellifera) working a new flower of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family this morning. The bee on the left has working away, when the other bee flew in and knocked it away! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

File.Name: c320g_c320-013.jpg
Notes:
6855514978 This is just a fly... but it has remarkable form and structure.

I believe it is a "Root-Maggot Fly" in the Anthomyiidae family of the order Diptera. It's perched on a pad of Prickly Pear (Opuntia) cactus in the garden, trying to warm up after a cold night. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

File.Name: c320h_c320-002.jpg
Notes: 6855480754 Here is a beautiful gall on a twig of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. It's the gall of a Beaked Twig Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis plumbella) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. This one is especially interesting because it's mostly green, not red. (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Beaked Twig Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Disholcaspis plumbella; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c321a_c321-003.jpg
Notes: 6858854910 This is a handsome little Snipe Fly (family Rhagionidae, order Diptera) - probably genus Rhagio - trying to warm up on an oak leaf in the woods this morning. Fortunately, it's not one of those biting snipe flies (genus Symphoromyia) that bug us later in the summer! (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2012)

File.Name: c321b_c321-046.jpg
Notes: 7004952179 This is the alate winged form of an aphid (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera). They are quite large for aphids - at least 1/8 inch. I call them "lupine aphids" because I only find them on native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family, like this. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2012)

File.Name: c321c_c321-044.jpg
Notes: 7004897629 I call these "lupine aphids" because I only find them on native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family, like this. They are definitely aphids (family Aphididae in the order Homoptera). They are quite large for aphids - at least 1/8 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 21 March 2012)

File.Name: c321d_c320-021.jpg
Notes:
7004860357 These are tiny native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) doing *something* on a bud of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I guess they are collecting honey dew from the bud, though we don't usually associate bitter oaks with honey! (San Marcos Pass, 20 March 2012)

I posted a similar photo a few days ago at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6971422293

It's interesting that these are a different kind of ant. The ants in my previous photo were (I think) genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae with two spines on their thorax and an interesting attachment to their abdomen. But these ants have a single "nodal bump" and their abdomen is fixed. I don't know what they are.

Ant ecology is so interesting!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: c321e_c321-024.jpg
Notes:
7004819161 Here are beautiful new flowers of native Blue Dick (Dichelostemma capitatum) - aka Common Brodiaea for the squeamish - in the Liliaceae plant family. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 March 2012)

I had some time today before my afternoon class at Dunn Middle School, so I stopped by my favorite place by the lake on my way to school.

File.Name: c321f_c321-023.jpg
Notes: 7004791853 This is a tiny new flower of native Common Eucrypta (Eucrypta chrysanthemifolia) in the Hydrophyllaceae family. It's growing as a fire-follower on ground that burned 10+ by Lake Cachuma. This native plant has a distinctive strong smell - it stinks! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 March 2012)

File.Name: c321g_c321-031.jpg
Notes:
7004743747 These are new flowers of native "Squaw Bush" - aka Basket Bush, Skunkbush, Three-leaved Sumac, and Chiquihuite! To a botanist, it's Rhus trilobata in the Anacardiaceae plant family. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 March 2012)

Maybe you can judge the importance of a plant by the number of common names it has! This plant was very important to the native Chumash people for several kinds of their fabulous baskets.
CommonNames: Squaw Bush; Basket Bush; Skunkbush; Three-leaved Sumac; Chiquihuite

File.Name: c321h_c321-010.jpg
Notes:
7004717953 This is a spent flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The flowers are the size of an avocado seed. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The foliage is as handsome as the flowers. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 21 March 2012)

This flower is a bit over the hill, and it's been hit by our drought this year. You can see the stamens falling away, revealing the large pistils that will bear seeds. Those strange orange "candy corn" things around the base of the flower are part of the nectary disk. I got this photo in the woods near Lake Cachuma in a place that burned at least 10 years ago. It's a "fire follower" plant that lingers.

The perennial roots were once collected for export to China as a medicinal herb. I'd rather find the flowers.

File.Name: c322a_c322-020.jpg
Notes:
7007554049 I found this furry Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2012)

I took my after-school Hike Club kids to "Lizard's Mouth" this afternoon. It's a fun place in the mountains with endless rock and caves to explore. I got home late, and I'm tired. Two photos will do just fine tonight.

File.Name: c322b_c322-004.jpg
Notes:
6861384158 We're being invaded by Ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) at home. These are the winged adults perched on a basket handle in the bathroom this morning. The worker ants are much smaller, and they have a ferocious bite/sting. I don't know what kind of ants they are. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2012)

I'll try to get a photo of the workers tomorrow. See my photos from last year at:
   http://www.fotolog-us.com/treebeard/67234239/

File.Name: c323a_c323-019.jpg
Notes:
7010050347 These flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family are bowed down by the weight of the raindrops they carry. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2012)

It didn't really rain last night, but we did have enough "fog drip" to collect 0.04 inch in the rain gauge this morning. And there's another real storm coming this weekend!

I had another busy evening with the Dunn Middle School science fair tonight. Fun student projects. This was the first time in 29 years that I didn't do a project, but I'm retired now - and I reckon my real on-going project is this photo page.

File.Name: c323b_c323-010.jpg
Notes:
6863909786 Here are raindrops on leaves and new flowers of Collar Lupine (Lupinus truncatus) in the Fabaceae family growing in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2012)

It didn't really rain last night, but we did have enough "fog drip" to collect 0.04 inch in the rain gauge this morning.

File.Name: c323c_c323-007.jpg
Notes: 6863899352 These are tiny raindrops on sporophytes of a native Moss (Bryophyta) in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2012) It takes a special sort of rain to make these perfect drops. It didn't really rain at all last night, but we did have 0.04 inches of fog drip in our rain gauge this morning. Perfect!

File.Name: c323d_c323-002.jpg
Notes:
6863874948 Here are raindrops on a palmate leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2012)

It takes a special sort of rain to make these perfect drops. It didn't really rain at all last night, but we did have 0.04 inches of fog drip in our rain gauge this morning. Perfect!

File.Name: c324a_c324-043.jpg
Notes:
7012892995 These aren't raindrops like my [Previous] photo, but "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on a palmate leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. I guess the small "raindrops" that they are pushing around like Sisyphus are drops of honeydew, though it's interesting that there are no ants tending them. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2012)

We've been in the clouds all day - cold and gloomy. A storm is moving in that may bring real rain tonight!

File.Name: c324b_c324-035.jpg
Notes:
6866761734 These are raindrops on a palmate leaf of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. No plant hangs onto raindrop like lupines. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2012)

It didn't really rain last night, but we did have enough "fog drip" to collect another 0.02 inch in the rain gauge this morning. The forecast is for real rain tonight!

File.Name: c324c_c324-046.jpg
Notes:
7012844931 This is an uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family. The buds are about ready to open. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2012)

This plant is common in Oregon - aka Oregon Grape - but it's uncommon in our mountains behind Santa Barbara. I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines, and I've never seen a berry. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on against climate change.

File.Name: c324d_c324-050.jpg
Notes: 7012829517 These are beautiful new flowers of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. They are growing on a shady bank along the road today. I was surprised to find it in this dry year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2012)

File.Name: c324e_c324-037.jpg
Notes:
6866692402 This is the most obscure wildflower I know in our mountains. These really are the flowers. I took me years to figure this out.

This is native Bowlesia incana in the Apiaceae plant family. The Apiaceae were once known as the Umbelliferae - because the flowers usually come in umbrella-like "umbels" with lots of flowers, but this is an umbel of one. I think of it as a limit thing - like a circle is a limit of an ellipse, so this flower is the limit of an umbel. It has attractive foliage, especially with raindrops. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2012)

File.Name: c324f_c324-027.jpg
Notes:
866649134 We have Banana Slug weather today!

This is another large hand-sized Pacific Banana Slug (Ariolimax columbianus) prowling through the woods today. They're 6-8 inches long! This is the largest terrestrial mollusk in our mountains, and we only see them after rains. They have two pairs of retractile "feelers" on their heads. The upper pair have eye spots, while the lower air provides a tactile sense and smell. (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2012)

File.Name: c324g_c323-025.jpg
Notes: 7012696133 This is a small flower head of native Plectritus ciliosa ssp. insignis in the unusual (for us) Valerianaceae plant family. I suspect it is common, but you have to get down on your hands and knees to see how pretty it is. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2012)

File.Name: c324h_c323-032.jpg
Notes: 7012664727 These are beautiful new cream-colored flowers of Chaparral Honeysuckle (Lonicera subspicata var. denudata) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. It's an interesting native shrub that is not quite a vine, but still has some vining tendencies when it clambers into other shrubs. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 23 March 2012)

File.Name: c325a_c325-012.jpg
Notes:
7016106087 I found this odd "elfin cup" fungus in the woods today - maybe the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin)? You can see the ribs on the outside of the cap. (See my [Previous] photo for a better look.) I put my cheap "digging knife" into the photo for scale - it's about 2-3/4 inches long. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2012)

I did a rain walk today, with a plastic garbage bag to protect my camera. It started raining pretty hard when I got this photo, so I hurried home just before the hard rain started.

We've had about 2 inches of rain so far today. That's on the low side of the 2-4 inches predicted, but I'm not complaining in this dry rain-year!

File.Name: c325b_c325-013.jpg
Notes: 7016064351 This is looking at the inverted "elfin cup" fungus in my [Next] photo - maybe the Brown Ribbed Elfin Cup (Helvella acetabulum or kin)? When I tried to pick it, the cup broke from the stem. The ribs of the cup extend almost to the cap margin, which is the key character. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2012)

File.Name: c325c_c325-010.jpg
Notes: 6869872988 I found this "Cup Fungus" in the woods today - maybe Peziza sylvestris or kin? The underside has a texture, and is not ribbed like the Helvella in my [Next] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2012)

File.Name: c325d_c325-002.jpg
Notes:
7015937205 This is the pale yellow form of native Indian Paintbrush (Castilleja affinis) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family growing on the hillside across from our mailbox today. The yellow "petals" are mostly leafy bracts, and the real flowers are the slender yellow tubes with protruding pistils. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2012)

Compare the usual red flowers at:
   www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6974718607/

These yellow flowers are unusual, but not uncommon. You can sometimes find them if you look for them where the plants are growing.

File.Name: c325e_c324-039.jpg
Notes:
7015874435 Here are beautiful "flowers" - really staminate catkins - of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Sometimes the catkins are yellow, other times they have more red like these. The combination of the colors in the woods is gorgeous! (San Marcos Pass, 24 March 2012)

Does anyone else remember the "catkin scene" in Ken Russell's "Women in Love" (1969) movie?

File.Name: c325f_c322-012.jpg
Notes:
7015789155 I mistook this wasp for a bee - though it sure does look like a large Carpenter Bee or kin. It seems pretty clumsy, almost too big to fly.

I'm now sure it's a Cimbicid Sawfly (Cimbicidae) in the order Hymenoptera, probably in the genus Abia. (Thanks for the ID terraincognita96!) One indication is that it has no hairs on it's legs like a real bee. It has no sign of a "wasp waist", but sawflies in the "Symphyta" group are like that.

This one is perched on a leaf of California Bay (Umbellularia californica) tree in the Lauraceae plant family, but the nearby Lonicera in the Caprifoliaceae plant family may well be its host plant. (San Marcos Pass, 22 March 2012)
CommonNames: wasp; bee; Carpenter Bee; Cimbicid Sawfly; California Bay
ScientificName: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Lonicera; Caprifoliaceae
associatedTaxa: Cimbicidae; Hymenoptera; Abia; Symphyta; Umbellularia californica; Lauraceae; Lonicera; Caprifoliaceae

File.Name: c325g_c323-020.jpg
Notes: 7015744567 This pretty little spider has found shelter from the storm under a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe from the web that it's a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae). (San Marcos Pass, 23 March 2012)
CommonNames: spider; Coast Live Oak; Sheetweb Spider

File.Name: c325h_c325-008.jpg
Notes:
6869608108 I think all lupines catch raindrops on their leaves, but different lupine catch them in different ways.

I believe these are young plants of a Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius in the Fabaceae plant family. I found a colony of a dozen or more plants in the woods today, like a bird dropped a seed pod. The leaves are soft and furry. (San Marcos Pass, 25 March 2012)

File.Name: c326a_c326-045.jpg
Notes:
7019803271 Them!

I found this large Ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) on our porch railing this afternoon. I'm sure its a Carpenter Ant in the genus Camponotus. It's nearly 1/2 inch long! They look so robotic. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

I often find these ants in the woodpile when I split firewood, and we have them in our home. Was this one scouting out our porch as a food source? BugGuide says that they "do not eat wood, only nest in it, and usually only after fungi have softened it." I'm not sure I believe that.

File.Name: c326b_c326-001.jpg
Notes:
7019769839 This is another cute little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) watching me from its perch on a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the canyon this morning. It's tiny! (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

This is an 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo, taken at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens.

File.Name: c326c_c326-020.jpg
Notes:
7019749097 This is a tiny Planthopper in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera. Those skinny wings can't be useful for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? They have amazing eyes. I guess they need them to see where they're hopping. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

I found this critter on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family today. I rotated the photo almost 90 degrees so it would look more "natural".

File.Name: c326d_c326-010.jpg
Notes:
7019687159 This is a new integral gall forming on a stem of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe it's the lumpy gall of the Tapered Stem Gall Wasp (Andricus spectabilis) in the in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

This is an "integral gall" that is not detachable like so many of our galls. You can't snap it off without braking the stem.
CommonNames: Scrub Oak; Tapered Stem Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus spectabilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus spectabilis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c326e_c326-013.jpg
Notes:
6873537126 The remarkable native Indian Warrior (Pedicularis densiflora) plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family are still flowering! Maybe they got a boost from the recent rains. I got this photo with the sun behind to illuminate the flowers. You can see that they have two stamens and a single exserted pistil. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

This plant is a root-parasite of several different chaparral shrubs, probably Big-berry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. It comes up mushroom-like, ready to flower.

The flower heads of these Indian Warrior plants are sold on the Internet "legal high" market. For more info, see my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6884345173/
Locality: Arctostaphylos glauca) in this place. It must have a fungal connection with its host plant. It comes up mushroom-like

File.Name: c326f_c326-040.jpg
Notes: 6873516834 These are beautiful new flowers of a native Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) tree in the Ericaceae plant family. They have little cup flowers, like the manzanitas in the same family. Often the flowers are too high to photograph up close, but these were at tripod level. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

File.Name: c326g_c326-029.jpg
Notes: 7019587653 These are beautiful native Goldback Ferns (Pentagramma triangularis) growing with moss in the woods today. I turned a leaf over for the photo, but it doesn't look very powdery yet. You can make pretty "fern tattoos" by pressing the ripe spore-bearing leaves to your skin. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

File.Name: c326h_c326-027.jpg
Notes:
6873417786 This mushroom sure looks like the Destroying Angel (Amanita ocreata), but look closer. It's too chunky, it has attached gills rather than free, no ring to speak of, and it lacks a saclike volva at the base. I'm pretty sure it's the Western Woodland Amanita (Amanita silvicola). (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

Don't even think about eating it! It looks too much like the deadly Amanita ocreata.

We've finally had recent rains, but mushrooms are still scarce in the woods. Maybe their fruiting time is over?
ScientificName: Amanita ocreata; Amanita silvicola; Amanita ocreata
associatedTaxa: Amanita ocreata; Amanita silvicola; Amanita ocreata

File.Name: c327a_c327-004.jpg
Notes:
7022878403 These are new manzanita-like flowers of uncommon native Wild Huckleberry (Vaccinium ovatum) in the Ericaceae plant family, growing in the shady north-slope Madrone and Tanoak woodland near home. This shrub is not common here. I rarely find fruit, and the birds always get them first. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2012)

These plants are part of the "relict association" of plants in our mountains like Madrone and Tanoak that really belong with the redwoods further north. But here they are.

I was really looking for mushrooms and slime mold today, so I went to a deep corner of the woods where no one else ever goes. *Sigh* no mushrooms, but I found these unusual flowers.

File.Name: c327b_c327-012.jpg
Notes:
6876741904 Here are the fruiting bodies of the well-named "Split-Gill" (Schizophyllum commune) fungus in our woods on a fallen Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree encrusted with lichens. They look like they have snow on their caps, but it's just white hairs. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2012)

They look like a Polypore with gills. Each gill is actually composed of two adjacent plates which can open and close. I've read that specimens sealed in a tube in 1911, then moistened 50 years later, unrolled their gills and began shedding spores!

You can guess that I'm hard up for "Phungus Photos". Despite the recent rains, there are few new mushrooms in the woods. I guess the 'shrooms have their time, and it is over.

File.Name: c327c_c327-014.jpg
Notes: 7022772531 This is an odd Crampball (Daldinia grandis or kin) fungus growing on a dead Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. It looks and feels like a lump of charcoal, but you can see some structure in the broken ball on top. The lumps on the surface are the spore-bearing "perithecia" of this odd member of the Pyrenomycetes group of the fungi. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2012) It gets its name apparently from an old folk belief that carrying one around in you armpit will cure cramps!
ScientificName: Daldinia grandis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; perithecia; Pyrenomycetes
associatedTaxa: Daldinia grandis; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; perithecia; Pyrenomycetes

File.Name: c327d_c327-016.jpg
Notes: 7022755091 This handsome yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is waiting for prey on a Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) shrub in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2012)

File.Name: c327e_c327-026.jpg
Notes: 6876607146 I found this pretty little Click Beetle (family Elateridae in the order Coleoptera) on a blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on our driveway today. They have their thorax drawn out to points that can *click* against the wing covers. (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2012)

File.Name: c327f_c327-020.jpg
Notes:
7022688855 This is bad grass! These are old flowering stems of non-native Smilo Grass (Piptatherum miliaceum). (San Marcos Pass, 27 March 2012)

This has become the worst invasive weed in our mountains. It grows along roads like other weeds, but it's also getting established in the woods and along the river. I've seen it get worse over the years. It's deceptive, because it really does look like a native grass if you don't look too close.

Kill it if you see it. Don't let it get established!

File.Name: c327g_c326-024.jpg
Notes:
7022652379 I believe these are new flowers of Turkey Pea (Sanicula tuberosa) in the Apiaceae plant family. It has finely dissected leaves and tuberous roots. It's the first of our native Sanicles that I've found in flower this year. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

Sanicles are another "collectors plant" in our mountains. We have 9 different species according to my flora. They all have similar flowers in little umbel-balls.

File.Name: c327h_c326-018.jpg
Notes:
7022561045 I believe this odd critter is a tiny Whitefly pupa - family Aleyrodidae in the old order Homoptera. It's tiny. This is an 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo, taken at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. I figure the size is 150 pixels, about 0.82 mm long. Really tiny! It looks like it belongs in the ocean. (San Marcos Pass, 26 March 2012)

Looking at BugGuide, this might be the Crown Whitefly (Aleuroplatus coronata) which favors oaks - but I have my doubts. That species is introduced, and I found this critter well of the beaten path in the mountains on a leaf of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family.
Locality: Aleuroplatus coronata) which favors oaks - but I have my doubts. That species is introduced
ScientificName: Aleyrodidae; Homoptera; Aleuroplatus coronata; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae
associatedTaxa: Aleyrodidae; Homoptera; Aleuroplatus coronata; Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae

File.Name: c328a_c328-017.jpg
Notes:
6879641836 These are impressive Weevil beetles in the family Curculionidae of the insect order Coleoptera. I found them today under a new leaf of native deciduous Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in the Fagaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. Look at those impressive jaws! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 28 March 2012)

It was overcast today as the dry tail-end of a storm moved through. No rain yet, but it was nice low-contrast light for photos.

File.Name: c328b_c328-004.jpg
Notes:
6879605744 I found this chubby little Wasp in the order Hymenoptera perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family this morning. Could it be a Cynipid Gall Wasp (Cynipidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2012)

I got this photo at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens, so I can figure its size with my photo. It's about 625 pixels long, and my camera sensor is 23.6 mm wide. So the size of the critter is 23.6x625/4288 = 3.4 mm, a bit more than 1/8 of an inch. Sounds right.
CommonNames: Wasp; Canyon Sunflower; Cynipid Gall Wasp

File.Name: c328c_c328-001.jpg
Notes:
6879579276 I found this small caterpillar in the woods this morning weaving a web between leaves of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) (left) and Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) (right). (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2012)

I watched it for a while, and it kept moving it's head-end back and forth between the new leaves, anchoring he webbing on both sides. I guess that these caterpillars make their webbing from the front end rather than the rear. I didn't know that.

File.Name: c328d_c328-023.jpg
Notes:
6879535674 This *could* have been a great photo, but you don't always get what you want - especially photographing insects. At least my photo shows the action.

Here is a furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera working a flower of Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia). These little flies are very camera shy! (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 28 March 2012)

File.Name: c328e_c328-020.jpg
Notes:
7025600469 This must be a new leaf gall because the leaves are new on this native deciduous Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in the Fagaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. I don't recognize the gall in my book by Ron Russo on Plant Galls of California and other Western States (2006). I assume that it's the gall of a Cynipidae wasp. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 28 March 2012)

It was overcast today as the dry tail-end of a storm moved through. No rain, but nice low-contrast light for photos.
CommonNames: Blue Oak; Plant Galls of California and other Western States

File.Name: c328f_c328-006.jpg
Notes:
6879480560 This is another fantastic spent flower of native California Peony (Paeonia californica) in the Paeoniaceae plant family. The flowers are the size of an avocado seed. It's a beautiful perennial plant that comes up mushroom-like with early flowers. The foliage is especially handsome. The flowers literally fall apart as they age. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 28 March 2012)

You can see the stamens falling away, revealing the large carpels that will bear seeds. Those strange orange "candy corn" things around the base of the flower are part of the nectary disk.

I got this photo today in the woods near Lake Cachuma in a place that burned at least 10 years ago. It's a "fire follower" plant that lingers.

File.Name: c328g_c328-024.jpg
Notes:
7025553933 These are new shiny new leaves and first open flowers of native Poison Oak (Toxicodendron diversilobum) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 28 March 2012)

The leaves contain the oil urushiol that causes an allergic skin reaction in many people. It's an allergen, not a poison. Enjoy, but don't touch - at least know your limits!

I'm one of the lucky ones who doesn't usually get the rash. It's a good thing - considering how much time I spend in the woods!

File.Name: c328h_c328-003.jpg
Notes: 6879434720 These are new flowers of non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. But this plant with whorled leaves is so well established in the woods that it may as well be native! We also have a number of native species with similar 4-petaled flowers. I hope to get photos of them all as the season goes on. (San Marcos Pass, 28 March 2012)

File.Name: c329a_c329-033.jpg
Notes:
6882382876 Here are a mating pair of March Flies (family Bibionidae) in order Diptera. They might well be the species Bibio xanthopus. I believe that's the female on top and the male underneath. Male and female look remarkably different! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)

I know, it's not quite in focus - but it's an action shot!

File.Name: c329b_c329-026.jpg
Notes: 6882353960 This handsome little Wild Bee (Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) is "sticking its neck out" for a flower of (I guess) Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceous) in the Cucurbitaceae family in the mountains today. It doesn't have "Pollen baskets" on its hind legs, and I see some hair under its abdomen, so I suspect it's in the family Megachilidae. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)
ScientificName: Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Marah fabaceous; Cucurbitaceae; Megachilidae
associatedTaxa: Apoidae; Hymenoptera; Marah fabaceous; Cucurbitaceae; Megachilidae

File.Name: c329c_c329-008.jpg
Notes:
6882331116 Watch out little bees, there is danger in the flowers!

Here is a yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) waiting for prey under a flower of non-native Black Mustard (Brassica nigra) in the Brassicaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)

File.Name: c329d_c329-011.jpg
Notes:
6882308762 This handsome yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) is a veteran - missing all but one leg on its left side. That must make it tough for a predator that pinches its prey. I found this spider today perched on foliage of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)

This is a common disability with these spiders - who try to grab prey bigger than they can hold. I wonder if the missing legs will regenerate on its next molt?

File.Name: c329e_c329-002.jpg
Notes: 6882280742 This is a new flower of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family. Did you notice the little Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on the left petal of the flower? I didn't either, until I got the photo up on my computer screen! (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)

File.Name: c329f_c329-025.jpg
Notes:
7028360185 D'oh!
rjadams55 sent email last night that this "jumping" spider is actually a lynx spider (family Oxyopidae). I think of lynx spiders as big green critters, but the bristly legs and the pattern of eyes gives it away - not at all like a jumping spider. He suggests a male Western Lynx Spider (Oxyopes scalaris) which is reported in California. Thanks a lot rjadams55!

Original comment:
"I guess this is a different Jumping Spider (Salticidae) on a leaf of (I guess) Wild Cucumber (Marah fabaceous) in the Cucurbitaceae family in the mountains today. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)

"This spider isn't as cute as the other Jumping Spiders I've been finding lately.
CommonNames: lynx spider; Western Lynx Spider; Jumping Spider; Wild Cucumber
ScientificName: Oxyopidae; Oxyopes scalaris; Salticidae; Marah fabaceous; Cucurbitaceae
associatedTaxa: Oxyopidae; Oxyopes scalaris; Salticidae; Marah fabaceous; Cucurbitaceae

File.Name: c329g_c329-028.jpg
Notes:
7028360185 This is an odd "foliose gall" on native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)

I believe this is the gall of a Gall Midge in the family Cecidomyiidae of the order Diptera - maybe the species Ceanothus Gall Midge (Asphondylia ceanothi or kin?
CommonNames: Greenbark Ceanothus; Gall Midge; Ceanothus Gall Midge
ScientificName: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi
associatedTaxa: Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Asphondylia ceanothi

File.Name: c329h_c329-043.jpg
Notes:
7028278207 Here is a Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) on the rim of a clay pot in our garden. It's showing mating colors. (San Marcos Pass, 29 March 2012)

Mentally draw a line between its eyes, and draw another line from the front of its face and up over its back... behind where they cross, you can see the greenish "third eye" of this lizard!

File.Name: c330a_c330-048.jpg
Notes:
7031121287 I found this amazing Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) in the woods today. It was maybe ovipositing on roots of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass when I found it. This photo shows it hanging upside-down on a leaf of non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

Maybe it's genus Eutreta? It has amazing eyes and dark moth-like wings.

Julie and I *might* take off for a few days tomorrow, but the weather may turn funky and windy. We'll see what happens.
CommonNames: Tephritid Fruit Fly; California Brome; Common Bedstraw
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Bromus carinatus; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae; Eutreta
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Bromus carinatus; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae; Eutreta

File.Name: c330b_c330-028.jpg
Notes: 6885003506 This furry little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera is taking "water from the rock" - a sandstone outcrop with some moisture. Maybe it's genus Bombylius? (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

File.Name: c330c_c330-010.jpg
Notes:
7031042435 The ants are totally in control!

The little ants aren't attacking the large winged ant because they are really the same thing. This is amazing dimorphism. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

The damn ants bite and sting! It was a personal sacrifice to get this photo today. I got "butt-nekkid" down on my knees and grabbed a fast 50 photos while the ants attacked me, and then stomped and swiped and took a shower!

Do you know the word "formication"? Said properly, it means "the sensation that insects or snakes are crawling over the skin". Yipes, I'm formicating!

I hope they're not Fire Ants - I don't think they've been recorded here - but the name fits. They never bother us except for this week or two in the spring.

File.Name: c330d_c330-038.jpg
Notes:
7031016821 These are more tiny native ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) gathering honeydew from new buds of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

I believe these ants are genus Crematogaster in the subfamily Myrmicinae. Look close at the middle ant on the left. They have two spines on their thorax and an articulated attachment to their abdomen. I like them!
ScientificName: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae
associatedTaxa: Formicidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae; Crematogaster; Myrmicinae

File.Name: c330e_c330-032.jpg
Notes: 7030991925 This is a moth-like Common Ringlet (Coenonympha tullia) butterfly perched on a grass blade in the canyon. It's a real butterfly, with knobby antennae and soccer-ball eyes - though I admit it is kind of dull. This is the first one I've managed to capture an image of this year, and I had to stalk it for some time! (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

File.Name: c330f_c330-016.jpg
Notes: 6884879940 I found this pretty caterpillar on a new flower bud of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family today. It is "butts up" in the flowers - the arrows point to its head! I don't know whether it's moth or butterfly. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

File.Name: c330g_c330-020.jpg
Notes:
7030954319 This is the tiny cocoon of a Ribbed Casemaker Moth (genus Bucculatrix, family Bucculatricidae) in the order Lepidoptera. I found it today on native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

The larvae are host-plant specific,and there are three that feed on Coyote Bush. This is one of those.
ScientificName: Bucculatrix; Bucculatricidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Bucculatrix; Bucculatricidae; Lepidoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae

File.Name: c330h_c330-021.jpg
Notes:
7030921465 This is a new gall forming on a stem of native Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. You can see a normal stem in the background.

I suspect this is the gall of the Baccharis Stem Gall Moth (Gnorimoshema baccharisella) in the family Gelechiidae (Lepidoptera), but there is also a Coyote Bush Stem Gall Midge (Rhopalomyia baccharis) in the order Diptera. Time will tell. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)
CommonNames: Coyote Bush; Baccharis Stem Gall Moth; Coyote Bush Stem Gall Midge
ScientificName: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Rhopalomyia baccharis; Diptera
associatedTaxa: Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Gnorimoshema baccharisella; Gelechiidae; Lepidoptera; Rhopalomyia baccharis; Diptera

File.Name: c331a_c331-023.jpg
Notes:
7033783645 I found this cute little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) today snuggling in the weed stems at an old homestead site in the mountains. These frogs are color-shifters from green to tan, but they always have a dark eye-stripe. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

It was odd to find them here, because there's no standing water nearby that I know of. Maybe there's an old cistern at the site that I don't know about?

We had gloomy weather today, and it finally rained - almost 1/2 inch, better than advertised by the NWS. Every bit helps with these late-season storms in a drought year! (We're still well under 50% of normal rainfall.)

File.Name: c331b_c331-030.jpg
Notes:
7033757235 I've just about given up on mushrooms in this drought year, but here is a new Coccora (Amanita calyptrata) mushroom growing under a non-native Fir tree (Abies) by an old homestead in the mountains today. There are also Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) and Madrone (Arbutus menziesii) trees nearby. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

Despite the Italian name, the Coccora is only known from the west coast of the US. It has a thick white "volva" - the cup at the base of the stem - and an equally thick volval patch on the cap. It usually has a distinct partial veil ring on the stem, but not this specimen - though you can still see a thin membrane still covering the gills.

This mushroom is said to be edible, but I generally avoid eating all Amanita mushrooms because some are deadly poison. But this really is a distinctive mushroom that I should try. My book describes it as having a "fishy" taste, which isn't encouraging.
CommonNames: Coccora; Fir; Coast Live Oak; Madrone
ScientificName: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Amanita
associatedTaxa: Amanita calyptrata; Abies; Quercus agrifolia; Arbutus menziesii; Amanita

File.Name: c331c_c331-033.jpg
Notes:
6887623230 This is a good-sized coiled "millipede" (class Diplopoda, Myriapoda) that I found in the woods when I lifted the mushroom in my [Newer] photo. It's maybe two inches long - not a giant, but still respectable. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

Millipedes are "thousand leggers" differing from the "hundred legger" centipedes - they have two pair of legs on most body segments. Our larger millipedes are often parasitized.

File.Name: c331d_c331-006.jpg
Notes: 7033696957 This is a beautiful coiled "fiddlehead" of Coastal Wood Fern (Dryopteris arguta) in the woods near home. It looks like it's still being unwrapped. New as it is, you can make out small sporangia in the lower-left. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

File.Name: c331e_c331-013.jpg
Notes:
6887549444 It's small world!

This is a tiny wasp gall on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe they are the galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? The galls are only a few mm long. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

Heh - the stamens on the catkin look like bunches of little bananas!
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: c331f_c331-003.jpg
Notes:
6887519590 These are beautiful new flowers of native Hummingbird Sage (Salvia spathacea) in the Lamiaceae plant family. I think my favorite part of them is the pinhead-dotted calyces and bracts in the whorls of the flowers. These flowers have two long-exserted stamens and a single long-exserted pistil. The leaves are fragrant and make good tea. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

There's nothing like a gloomy day to bring out the colors of these flowers. You lose a bit of brilliance, but gain it back with saturation.

File.Name: c331g_c331-010.jpg
Notes:
7033592621 These are new developing "samara" seed of a native Bigleaf Maple (Acer macrophyllum) in the Aceraceae) plant family. They are very pretty in the low light of a cloudy day. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

Note how the seeds are clustered at the top of the flower raceme. They will become the characteristic wings that flutter down like little helicopters when you shake them from the branches in autumn. See my photo at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6978227999/

File.Name: c331h_c330-049.jpg
Notes:
7033550909 Here is another photo of the remarkable Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) that I found in the woods yesterday. It has amazing technicolor eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 30 March 2012)

Maybe it is genus Eutreta? It's perched on a leaflet of non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. See my [Older] photo.
ScientificName: Tephritidae; Diptera; Eutreta; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae
associatedTaxa: Tephritidae; Diptera; Eutreta; Galium aparine; Rubiaceae

File.Name: c401a_c401-016.jpg
Notes:
7037496057 I found this native ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) in the woods today. It seems to be grooming itself. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2012)

It was very windy today - hard to get any photos, though it helped to be close to the ground like this. At least I tried.

It was too windy for photos, so I spent some time this afternoon listening and watching the fabulous Alan Lomax Collection on the web at: www.culturalequity.org/. What a treasure trove of American folk music!

File.Name: c401b_c401-022.jpg
Notes:
6891369522 I guess this is a Clitocybe mushroom with white spores and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk, but I don't know what kind it is. It has a dry funnel-shaped cap. (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2012)

Mushrooms were about the only thing not blowing in the wind today!

File.Name: c401c_c401-017.jpg
Notes: 7037446307 These are little dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus) mushrooms coming up in the woods today. They look quite different from one another, but they change over time. The final step of their transformation is that they "deliquesce" - literally digest themselves into black slime! (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2012)

File.Name: c401d_c401-008.jpg
Notes: 6891282502 These are galled leaves of native Nuttall's Snowberry (Symphoricarpos mollis) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. Maybe they are the galls of the Snowberry Gall Midge (Dasineura, Cecidomyiidae, Diptera) - or the Snowberry Sawfly (Blennogeneris spissipes, Hymenoptera)? (San Marcos Pass, 1 April 2012)
CommonNames: Nuttall's Snowberry; Snowberry Gall Midge; Snowberry Sawfly
ScientificName: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Dasineura; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Blennogeneris spissipes; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Symphoricarpos mollis; Caprifoliaceae; Dasineura; Cecidomyiidae; Diptera; Blennogeneris spissipes; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c401e_c331-014.jpg
Notes:
6891236798 Here on the left is a furry Bumblebee (family Bombus in the order Hymenoptera). It's perched on a "witches' broom" gall on native Toyon (Heteromeles arbutifolia) in the Rosaceae plant family. It was cold and overcast, but it seems an odd place to warm up. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

Witches' brooms are a dense collection of shoots coming from one place on a plant stem. They involve bacteria, fungi, mistletoes, or mites.

File.Name: c401f_c331-028.jpg
Notes:
7037264825 This caterpillar sure looks like a skinny "woolly bear" - a caterpillar of a Tiger Moth (family Arctiidae in the order Lepitoptera). It's either stretching its back, or it's preparing to pupate into a moth. It's hanging from an old weed stem. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

I expect to see these caterpillars in late summer, but I've seen them before in spring.

File.Name: c401g_c331-017.jpg
