Notes: 7037170623 This is another perfect new flower of native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) wildflower in the Iridaceae plant family growing in the woods near home. This is the only native iris that grows in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

File.Name: c401h_c331-025.jpg
Notes:
7037126631 This is a small Petty Spurge (Euphorbia peplus) in the Euphorbiaceae plant family. This odd little plant isn't native, but it persists as a weed at the site of an old homestead on the pass. (San Marcos Pass, 31 March 2012)

If you look at the bottom of my photo, you can see the odd monoecious flowers, with separate male and female flowers on the same plant. The male staminate flowers look like they belong in the ocean; the female pistillate flowers look like little fruit.

File.Name: c402a_c402-029.jpg
Notes:
7040845773 I found this perfect little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera in the canyon today - maybe genus Bombylius. It's resting on a dead leaf of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Usually these flies are camera-shy, but this one let me get very close and posed for me. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

"I don't mind the weather so the wind don't blow..."
- Holy Modal Rounders, "Hop High Ladies"

Yesterday was VERY WINDY, but today was calm and felt like spring.
ScientificName: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Bombyliidae; Diptera; Bombylius; Quercus agrifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c402b_c402-007.jpg
Notes:
6894716872 This is a beautiful little wasp (Hymenoptera) that I found perched on a leaf of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family this morning. It's quite small, maybe 3/8 inch long. I guess it's an Ichneumon Wasp (family Ichneumonidae). (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

I didn't appreciate how beautiful this little wasp was until I got this photo up on my computer screen!
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; austrolitoralis; Hydrophyllaceae; Ichneumonidae
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Phacelia ramosissima; austrolitoralis; Hydrophyllaceae; Ichneumonidae

File.Name: c402c_c402-059.jpg
Notes:
7040758187 This is the damnedest insect!

I guess it's a "true bug" in the order Hemiptera with a (faint) cross on its back and a round "coin-like" abdomen. But those furry antennae have me stumped. My first thought is family Reduviidae because of that abdomen. I found it today perched on a dead grass stalk on the wild side of our garden. It's quite small, about 1/4 inch. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

If you know what this critter is, please let me know!

Update: I think Aliceinwl1 pegged it as a Flat Bug (Aradidae). Thank you Alice!

File.Name: c402d_c402-044.jpg
Notes:
7040724735 I found this beautiful Pale Swallowtail (Papilio eurymedon) butterfly this morning working flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

Look how its proboscis is covered with yellow pollen from the flowers! This is one way that pollination happens.

File.Name: c402e_c402-035.jpg
Notes: 7040684555 I found another early female California Dogface (Colias eurydice) butterfly working flowers of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family today. These flowers have a "deep throat" that seems made for butterflies, but I don't think they offer much nectar. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

File.Name: c402f_c402-003.jpg
Notes: 6894553748 These are interesting galls on new leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. They are not listed in my book on "Plant Galls of California and Other Western States" by Ron Russo. I don't know what critter makes them. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

File.Name: c402g_c402-055.jpg
Notes: 7040614797 These are new open flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. You wouldn't make bouquets of these flowers, but they do the job for this plant. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

File.Name: c402h_c402-002.jpg
Notes: 6894494646 This is another handsome native ant (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) balancing on a twig in the woods today - maybe the same kind of ant as my [Older] photo. It's small, but not tiny. It has one nodal bump on its petiole. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

File.Name: c403a_c403-008.jpg
Notes: 6897723392 This is another beautiful little Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Hemiptera. It's hanging on a blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on our driveway. It has impossibly thin legs. If you look close, you can see its even-thinner proboscis folded under its head. See my [Older] photo for another shot of these amazing insects. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2012)

File.Name: c403b_c402-057.jpg
Notes:
7043769881 Here is a beautiful little Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Hemiptera. It's perched on a new leaf of California Honeysuckle (Lonicera hispidula) in the Caprifoliaceae plant family. These little bugs have impossibly thin legs. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

This one put its head down when I approached too close with my camera - as though it was trying to look even smaller!

File.Name: c403c_c403-013.jpg
Notes: 6897650746 This is a beautiful little Bee Fly (family Bombyliidae) in the order Diptera approaching an amazing flower in our garden. I guess the flower is non-native Mountain Bluet (Centaurea montana) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a relative of bad weeds, though this one doesn't seem to spread. The native insects like it. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2012)

File.Name: c403d_c403-018.jpg
Notes:
6897580094 This is a tiny Beetle (order Coleoptera) that I found today on a stem of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass above the retaining wall on our driveway. It seems to be flexing its wings. I'm not sure what it is - maybe a "seed weevil"? (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2012)

This is one of my favorite places to look for photos. It's a convenient height to look for and photograph tiny insects, my window into the woods.

This is another 800x800 crop from my 4288x2848 pixel original photo, taken at 1:1 magnification, as close as I can get with my macro lens. I figure the size is 460 pixels, about 2.6 mm long. Really tiny!

File.Name: c403e_c403-006.jpg
Notes:
7043639539 This is another different tiny Wild Bee (Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) resting on a petal of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. It has a hairy face unlike the bees in my two [Older] photos. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2012)

We have lots of these "little black bees" in our woods. (LBB's?)

File.Name: c403f_c403-003.jpg
Notes:
6897519704 This is a different tiny Wild Bee (Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This one has a white face without hairs, and a very long proboscis that is oddly forked at the end. (San Marcos Pass, 3 April 2012)

Compare my [Older] and [Newer] photos. We have many "little black bees" in our woods. (LBB's?)

File.Name: c403g_c403-021.jpg
Notes:
6897472674 This is a tiny Wild Bee (Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) working new flowers of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

We have many "little black bees" in our woods. (LBB's?) This one has a punctate body, no white on its face, and visible "pollen basket" hairs on its rear legs. Compare my two [Newer] photos.
Locality: LBB's?)  This one has a punctate body

File.Name: c403h_c402-025.jpg
Notes:
6897447752 "Beautiful aphids" sounds like an oxymoron, at least to a gardener, but these almost fit the bill!

These are blood-red aphids in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on a native shrub of Coyote Bush (Baccharis pilularis) in the Asteraceae plant family. They remind me of the bright orange Milkweed Aphids (Aphis nerii?) that infest the non-native milkweeds in our garden in late-summer. (San Marcos Pass, 2 April 2012)

Like many aphids, they do an "aphid twitch" - somehow all shifting position at the same time to scare off an intruder, like me with my camera.
ScientificName: Aphididae; Homoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Aphis nerii
associatedTaxa: Aphididae; Homoptera; Baccharis pilularis; Asteraceae; Aphis nerii

File.Name: c404a_c404-043.jpg
Notes:
7046816953 Perfect camouflage!

Look close to see the remarkable Crab Spider (Thomisidae) resting on top of this twig of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. The ant in my [older] photo didn't look close enough! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

I got this photo after the spider dropped the ant in my [Older] photo. It stretched out and almost disappeared against the oak bark.

Today started calm enough, but the winds have been picking up all day. *Sigh* The next couple of days are forecast to be very windy. I'll do what I can for new photos.

File.Name: c404b_c404-032.jpg
Notes:
7046777183 When I first saw this scene in the woods this morning, I thought "OMG it's a chimera!" Then I noticed the spider's eyes...

This is a remarkable Crab Spider (Thomisidae) with an ant (Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) for prey. The spider has perfect camouflage - see my [Newer] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

File.Name: c404c_c404-017.jpg
Notes:
6900658926 This is a fantastic little "Garbage Line Spider" (genus Cyclosa) in the Orb-Weaver Spiders (family Araneidae) on the inner circle of its web. I recognize that odd bottle-neck hump on it's back. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

It was breezy this morning, and I took *many* photos to get this almost-sharp shot.

File.Name: c404d_c404-010.jpg
Notes: 6900624050 Here is another little Jumping Spider (Salticidae) perched on the edge of a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus). *Don't Jump* Nah, this spider is a jumper, go ahead and jump! (He did.) (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

File.Name: c404e_c404-006.jpg
Notes:
7046693213 This beautiful little spider has an abdomen like cracked porcelain. I guess from its eyes that it's an Orb-Weaver Spider in the family Araneidae, though it has no web to speak of. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

There are several of these spiders snuggled into leaves of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family in our yard. They haven't moved much in the last week.

File.Name: c404f_c404-057.jpg
Notes: 6900559098 Here is the tiny nymph of a Long-Horned Grasshopper (suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera) - no doubt a Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae. It's perched on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. It has lobster-like antennae! (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)
CommonNames: Long-Horned Grasshopper; Katydid; Bush Monkey Flower
ScientificName: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae
associatedTaxa: Ensifera; Orthoptera; Tettigoniidae; Mimulus longiflorus; Scrophulariaceae

File.Name: c404g_c404-045.jpg
Notes: 7046631709 I guess this is a Propertius Duskywing (Erynnis propertius) butterfly taking "water from the rock" on a sandstone outcrop. It's a "Spread-wing Skipper" (subfamily Pyrginae of the Hesperiidae) without a white boarder on the hindwing. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

File.Name: c404h_c404-021.jpg
Notes: 7046615325 These are social caterpillars of the Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly in our woods. They favor "Scrophs" - plants in the Scrophulariaceae plant family, like this Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia). The caterpillars are web-spinners, and you can see some webbing in the photo. Sometimes they shroud the penstemon leaves like "tent caterpillars", but they are not the bad kind. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

File.Name: c405a_c405-014.jpg
Notes:
7049713743 This is a beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

Up close like this, it looks like a pointillist painting by Georges Seurat - many color swatches combine to make that green!

Today started breezy and the wind grew to gale force by afternoon. I took *many* photos to get each one of these keepers. *Sigh* more of the same is expected tomorrow, and it's been cold, with a frost warning tonight.
ScientificName: Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; pointillist
associatedTaxa: Callophrys dumetorum; Lotus scoparius; Fabaceae; pointillist

File.Name: c405b_c405-011.jpg
Notes: 7049687115 This beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly is ovipositing (laying eggs) on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

File.Name: c405c_c405-051.jpg
Notes:
6903569268 This is a small Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly that I found along my trail today. It's heads-down and butts-up among the little non-native Filaree (Erodium) plants that grow everywhere. It's trying to act like a solar collector pointing at the sun on a cold day in the woods today. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

These butterflies really are blue above and when they fly. You can see a bit of blue on the trim of the upper forewing.

File.Name: c405d_c405-007.jpg
Notes:
6903525244 First I was excited to find a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) on this native flower of Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. Then I noticed that it's head was upside down!

Look close at the tip of the fly's abdomen to see a bit of the Crab Spider (Thomisidae) that has captured it. Great camouflage! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Bush Poppy; Crab Spider
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae; Thomisidae
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Dendromecon rigida; Papaveraceae; Thomisidae

File.Name: c405e_c405-018.jpg
Notes:
6903458192 For the scant week or three that it flowers, we have no finer plant in our mountains than this native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family! The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. It was windy today, but these were the first flowers I've seen this year. I'll have better photos! (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

It used to be called Styrax officinalis var. redivivus - but it's so strange that a species native to southern Europe and the middle east is also a native shrub of Southern California. The name has been put right, but it's still an interesting shrub.

This is an example of the "Mediterranean-American disjunction", and there are more examples - like maybe native Yerba Buena (Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family which is related to the Mediterranean summer and winter savory plants, but also grows in our canyon.

There's a web page about this by Arthur C. Gibson:
   http://www.botgard.ucla.edu/html/membgnewsletter/Volume5number2/Styraxofficinalis.html

Gibson concludes that: "The only reasonable conclusion is that this genus was once widely distributed across the Northern Hemisphere and became restricted to certain smaller zone."

This plant also has a curious local distribution. It's very common on San Marcos Pass in both woodland and chaparral, but where else do you find it in Santa Barbara County?
Locality: Satureja douglasii) in the Lamiaceae plant family which is related to the Mediterranean summer and winter savory plants
ScientificName: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae
associatedTaxa: Styrax redivivus; Styracaceae; Styrax officinalis; redivivus; Satureja douglasii; Lamiaceae

File.Name: c405f_c405-053.jpg
Notes:
6903401146 These are leaves of the Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family. It is quite rare in our mountains, and it's not having a good time in this drought year, with only about 45% normal rainfall so far. I only found two plants, but this one had flower buds. I'll give it another week or two to open. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

This uncommon lily is endemic to our local mountains - rated CNPS 1B.2 - "Plants Rare, Threatened, or Endangered in California and Elsewhere". This canyon trail is the only place I've found it near home. There were just two plants, hard to photograph in the shade and wind today.

File.Name: c405g_c405-005.jpg
Notes:
6903381176 The Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius in the Fabaceae plant family) is starting to flower on the pass. I found these out on Fremont Ridge today. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

They are typical lupines, only larger - to head-high or so. They have silvery foliage, though it looks more green in my photo. Beautiful!

File.Name: c405h_c405-044.jpg
Notes: 7049446851 These are new native flowers of Bush Poppy (Dendromecon rigida) in the Papaveraceae plant family. It's a fantastic head-high (or more) native shrub that has these poppy flowers much of the year. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

File.Name: c406a_c406-035.jpg
Notes:
6906392666 This is a gorgeous native flower of a Larkspur in the genus Delphinium of the Ranunculaceae plant family in my [Previous] photo. Maybe this is D. patens ssp. hepaticoideum with 3-lobed cauline leaves? (But cp. D. parryi.) Some critter has munched a sepal and petal away, so you can look inside! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

This is the first larkspur I've seen in the canyon this year, and it may be the only one. Our drought year is taking a toll on wildflowers.
ScientificName: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; hepaticoideum; D. parryi
associatedTaxa: Delphinium; Ranunculaceae; D. patens; hepaticoideum; D. parryi

File.Name: c406b_c406-013.jpg
Notes: 6906363532 This ferocious-looking wasp in the order Hymenoptera is trying to get warm in the sun on a leaf this morning. It's quite large. I don't know what kind of a wasp it is - family Vespidae? I like those notched eyes that fit its antennae. See my [Older] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

File.Name: c406c_c406-011.jpg
Notes:
7052421587 I found this large wasp in the order Hymenoptera trying to get warm in the sun this morning on a leaf of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. I don't know what kind of a wasp it is - family Vespidae? (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

It was cold last night - not quite freezing, but close enough!

File.Name: c406d_c406-023.jpg
Notes:
7052379719 I found this small Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata) in the woods today. It's perched on a dead grass stem. Sure, it's a tight crop. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

I've seen a few damselflies before, but this is the first one I've managed to capture a photo of this year.

File.Name: c406e_c405-056.jpg
Notes: 7052351433 Here is a tiny fly (order Diptera) perched on a succulent leaf near the front porch. Nice whiskers! You can make out the ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up its compound eyes in this photo. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

File.Name: c406f_c406-040.jpg
Notes: 7052333467 Here are a pair of tiny flies (order Diptera) working a new flower of native Miner's Lettuce (Claytonia perfoliata) in the Portulacaceae) plant family in the woods today. I have no idea what kinds of flies they are. (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

File.Name: c406g_c404-051.jpg
Notes: 6906214306 This is an odd small hairy lupine growing in bare dirt along the road in our canyon. It sure looks like the Bajada Lupine (Lupinus concinnus) in the Fabaceae plant family with those hairy leaves. I usually find these hairy lupines in the desert, so isn't it surprising here by the coast? (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

File.Name: c406h_c405-032.jpg
Notes:
7052294117 This is a different view of Cold Springs Bridge on Highway 154 over the Santa Ynez Mountains, from the high point of Fremont Ridge across the canyon. You can see the new "suicide barrier" along the sides of the bridge. *Sigh* It remains a beautiful bridge, light and airy. (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

The bridge has appeared in many commercials, and (my favorite) the Disney movie "The Cat from Outer Space" (1978).

People have climbed that arch that rises 600+ feet above the canyon! There used to be names and dates written in pencil at the base of the east buttress, but they were painted over during earthquake-recontruction on the bridge. I've hiked the creek under the bridge. My favorite find was HALF of a bowling ball that bounced!
Locality:
1978).

People have climbed that arch that rises 600+ feet above the canyon! There used to be names and dates written in pencil at the base of the east buttress

File.Name: c407a_c407-029.jpg
Notes:
6909602114 This is looking into the heart of a new flower of Chaparral Clematis (Clematis lasiantha in the Ranunculaceae plant family. You can see male stamens around the bottom and the female pistils on the crown above. You can also see what will become the plumose hairs of the seeds. I don't think I've ever noticed this before! (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2012)

Today was a bit breezy, but beautiful!

File.Name: c407b_c407-034.jpg
Notes:
6909562212 This is a native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family. It's a variety of "Cobweb Thistle" with white flowers, but there are also violet and bright red versions. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2012)

This particular flower is inhabited by green aphids in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera.
ScientificName: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: c407c_c407-018.jpg
Notes:
6909535890 This is a pretty little Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly ovipositing on a native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plant in the Fabaceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2012)

I'm still waiting to get a photo of these butterflies showing their pretty blue upper wings.

File.Name: c407d_c407-024.jpg
Notes: 6909495056 This pretty peach-colored spider has captured a small beetle on a leaf of Goldencup Oak aka Canyon Live Oak (Quercus chrysolepis) in the Fagaceae plant family. I believe from its eyes and web that it's an Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). I love those eyes (and eyes and eyes...) (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2012)
CommonNames: spider; Goldencup Oak; Canyon Live Oak; Orb-Weaver Spider

File.Name: c407e_c407-027.jpg
Notes:
6909495056 Here are new flowers of native Sanicula arguta in the Apiaceaeplant family. It's not a flower you would cut for your dining table, but it is native and it does have its charms. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2012)

The Sanicles are another "collector's genus". We have 5 to 7 species in our mountains. The flowers all look the same, but the foliage is different.

File.Name: c407f_c407-036.jpg
Notes:
7055463357 These are pretty flowers of Asphodel aka Onionweed (Asphodelus fistulosus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It's growing along Highway 154 on my way to Santa Barbara today. (Santa Ynez Mountains, 7 April 2012)

It's pretty, but it's a federally listed noxious weed. I first noticed it in the 1970s when I started learning our native plants and couldn't find it in the Munz Flora. I've been watching it spread over the mountains ever since. Don't encourage it.

I got this photo this afternoon on my beer run to town after JMDN posted a photo of the same plant growing in Spain.

File.Name: c407g_c407-005.jpg
Notes:
7055437163 These are pretty new "flowers" of the native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. It's one of the native grasses I can recognize by sight.

This clump of native bunch-grass was surrounded by non-native grasses, but it seems to be doing fine. I also find it in our woodland at home. (San Marcos Pass, 7 April 2012)

File.Name: c407h_c406-049.jpg
Notes:
6909277428 This is a close photo of a new leaf blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family - and a little lesson on grass anatomy. I need help with grasses too - I know a few grasses, but I'm mostly a forb guy! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

You see the central stem or culm of the grass and the new leaf blade going off to the left. Below, you can see the sheath of the new leaf, which surrounds the stem like a tube split down one side. At the junction of the sheath and the blade, you can make out the thin membranous ligule which joins the two, like underwear. Some grasses also have auricles - little appendages at the base of blade, but not this grass.
CommonNames: California Brome; forb; culm; blade; sheath; ligule; auricles

File.Name: c408a_c408-023.jpg
Notes:
6913006118 I found this tattered Painted Lady (Vanessa cardui) butterfly perched on a leaf of native California Thistle (Cirsium occidentale var. californicum) in the Asteraceae plant family in the canyon - which happens to be one of its host plants. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)

Some years, these butterflies emigrate north in large numbers from Mexico. This butterfly has come from *somewhere* and it looks like its been a hard trip!

I've been seeing these butterflies for a while, but this is the first I've managed to get a photo of.

It was a tough day for new photos with the steady breeze. It wasn't terribly windy, but it was just enough to keep everything in motion. There's a 90% chance of rain on Tuesday night!
ScientificName: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae
associatedTaxa: Vanessa cardui; Cirsium occidentale; californicum; Asteraceae

File.Name: c408b_c408-011.jpg
Notes:
6912972882 I found this beautiful large Grass-Skipper (genus Hesperia, family Hesperiidae) butterfly working a flower of native Prickly-Phlox (Leptodactylon californicum) in the Polemoniaceae plant family by the creek today. Usually I don't try to ID skippers (I just "skip her"), but this one is probably another early Umber Skipper (Poanes melane). (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)

If you look close, you can see pollen on her rolled-up proboscis. Thus pollination happens.
ScientificName: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Poanes melane
associatedTaxa: Hesperia; Hesperiidae; Leptodactylon californicum; Polemoniaceae; Poanes melane

File.Name: c408c_c408-007.jpg
Notes:
7058996931 This is another photo I'm not proud of, but it's a record shot.

This is the first California Sister (Adelpha bredowii) butterfly that I've seen in the canyon this year. It's perched high in a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)

This is an 800x800 crop from my original 4288x2848 pixel original photo taken with my 105 mm macro lens - the only lens I usually carry on my walks in the woods.

File.Name: c408d_c408-016.jpg
Notes:
7058957025 Here is a large black male Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera. It's working a new flower of native Black Sage (Salvia mellifera) in the Labiatae plant family in the canyon. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)

I know it's overexposed and not quite sharp. It was breezy today, and I only got one click for this photo before the bee flew away. It happens.
ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia mellifera; Labiatae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Salvia mellifera; Labiatae

File.Name: c408e_c408-024.jpg
Notes: 7058907087 This is another impossibly thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Hemiptera perched on a plant of Golden Yarrow (Eriophyllum confertflorum) in the Asteraceae plant family. For scale, there's a little black aphid on the upper-left border. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)

File.Name: c408f_c408-029.jpg
Notes:
7058839367 I first noticed this nest many months ago on a native Beavertail Cactus (Opuntia basilaris) in the Cactaceae plant family that we potted many years ago from a piece we collected in the desert. I've been thinking all along that it was a spider nest. Well, I'm wrong!

This nest has brought forth tiny caterpillars! Looking closer at my photo, the nest seems more like wood "excelsior" than spider silk.

I guess these are the hatchling caterpillars of the Western Tussock Moth - family Lymantriidae in the order Lepidoptera, maybe Orgyia vetusta or kin. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)
ScientificName: Opuntia basilaris; Cactaceae; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta
associatedTaxa: Opuntia basilaris; Cactaceae; Lymantriidae; Lepidoptera; Orgyia vetusta

File.Name: c408g_c408-030.jpg
Notes:
7058794091 This is like the dream of a spider...

I found it perched under a thin leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family today, with the sun behind the leaf - like a natural light tent. I believe it is a Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae), but it hardly matters. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)

File.Name: c408h_c408-026.jpg
Notes: 7058711855 This is a pretty little weed growing on a bank in our canyon. I believe it is the Cutleaf Geranium (Geranium dissectum) in the Geraniaceae plant family. It doesn't seem like the bad sort of weed that invades the woods, but here it is just a few feet away from the Delphinium patens plant I showed the day before yesterday. The flowers are tiny, less than 1/4 inch across. (San Marcos Pass, 8 April 2012)

File.Name: c409a_c409-032.jpg
Notes:
6917038570 This cute little Pacific Tree Frog (Pseudacris regilla aka Hyla regilla) looks like it's ready to take of on a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) leaf board! Actually, it's trying to get away from me, and it succeeded. I love its tiny feet. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

I found this frog at the same old homestead site in the mountains as my previous frog photo. There's no standing water nearby that I know of, but maybe there's an old cistern at the site that I don't know about? This frog looks moist in a dry year.

These frogs are color-shifters from green to tan - but I can't recall seeing red before. They always have a dark eye-stripe.

Our frogs at home are going off tonight. Maybe they can sense that we have a 90% chance of rain tomorrow!
CommonNames: Pacific Tree Frog; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Quercus agrifolia
associatedTaxa: Pseudacris regilla; Hyla regilla; Quercus agrifolia

File.Name: c409b_c409-006.jpg
Notes:
6916973498 This pretty little spider has just pulled its legs free from the old "shell" that it has just shed. It looks like its wiggling its new toes in joy! You can see the old skin up above. It's hard to believe those long new legs fit into it a few minutes ago. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

This spider is hanging from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family this morning. I believe it's a little Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). It looks like something from the ocean.

Soon after this, the spider started twirling around on the end its web. I don't believe the breeze was enough to do this. Maybe it was drying its new skin out?
Locality:
family Araneidae). It looks like something from the ocean.

Soon after this

File.Name: c409c_c409-001.jpg
Notes:
7063017091 I found this pretty little spider hanging from a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family this morning. It's shedding its skin, just about to pull its legs free from their old-skin "socks"! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

I believe it's a little Orb-Weaver Spider (family Araneidae). It was already breezy, so I had to bump up my ISO a notch to capture this image. It's interesting that those long new legs fit into that short old skin. See my [Newer] photo.

File.Name: c409d_c409-072.jpg
Notes: 6916890556 I found this rather large yellow Crab Spider (Thomisidae) on a flower stem of native Tauschia arguta in the Apiaceae plant family today. It's looking right at me! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

File.Name: c409e_c406-003.jpg
Notes: 7062956459 I guess this is a small Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) hanging from it's web on the wild edge of our garden. What great climbers/acrobats they are - look at that footwork on the web! (San Marcos Pass, 6 April 2012)

File.Name: c409f_c409-013.jpg
Notes:
7062895141 Here are fantastic tiny wasp galls on a staminate catkin of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. Maybe they are the galls of a Two-horned Gall Wasp (Dryocosmus dubiosus in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera? The galls are only a few mm long. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

Catkins seem such a temporary place to build a house, but these galled catkins usually stay on the tree long past blooming. The tiny gall insects change the behavior of the abscission as well as the stamens.
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Two-horned Gall Wasp

File.Name: c409g_c409-047.jpg
Notes:
7062867655 These are new leaf galls on a Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) shrub in the Ericaceae plant family. They are caused by an aphid rather than a wasp - the Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid (Tamalia coweni) in the family Aphididae of the order Homoptera). If you peel one of the galls open, you can see the tiny aphids inside. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

It's interesting (and confusing!) that both aphids and a fungus cause red leaves on this manzanita. See my [Older] photo.
CommonNames: Bigberry Manzanita; Manzanita Leaf Gall Aphid
ScientificName: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera
associatedTaxa: Arctostaphylos glauca; Ericaceae; Tamalia coweni; Aphididae; Homoptera

File.Name: c409h_c405-045.jpg
Notes: 7062821253 These are bright red leaves on an otherwise normal green shrub of native Bigberry Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glauca) in the Ericaceae plant family. Pretty, but I suspect it is the beginning of a fungus infection - maybe the Manzanita Leaf Fungus(Exobasidium vacinii)? (San Marcos Pass, 5 April 2012)

File.Name: c410a_c410-010.jpg
Notes:
7066244985 This is a pretty little flower of native Mission Star (Lithophragma cymbalaria) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. You can see the goodies inside, like one of those fancy easter eggs that you look into. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2012)

This Lithophragma cymbalaria has little opposite leaves on its flower stalks, but we also have the similar Woodland Star (L. affine) with alternate leaves on the stem. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year.

It was really too windy for photos today as our new storm blows in - but I kicked up the ISO and tried. The surface winds have been coming from the north, but the upper-level winds are bringing clouds in from the south. This is a good sign for our chances of rain.

Our best winter storms come from the north but pull clouds from the south - which meets our east-west mountains head on, producing an "orographic enhancement". At least that's what I hope will happen.
Locality:
L. affine) with alternate leaves on the stem. These little differences help make our local plants interesting year after year.

It was really too windy for photos today as our new storm blows in - but I kicked up the ISO and tried. The surface winds have been coming from the north
ScientificName: Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria; L. affine
associatedTaxa: Lithophragma cymbalaria; Saxifragaceae; Lithophragma cymbalaria; L. affine

File.Name: c410b_c410-003.jpg
Notes:
6920136458 Here are flowers of native California Saxifrage (Saxifraga californica) in the Saxifragaceae plant family. I found them growing on a shady bank along Stagecoach Road this morning. This mature flower has lost its pretty orange stamens, but the pistils are more interesting. They turn bright red as the seeds mature. (San Marcos Pass, 10 April 2012)

It was really too windy for photos today as our new storm blows in - but I kicked up the ISO and tried!

File.Name: c410c_c409-063.jpg
Notes:
7066183215 Here is a bee (Hymenoptera) caught in the air flying into the open throat a flower of native Pitcher Sage (Lepechinia calycina) flower in the Lamiaceae plant family. Note the little beetle occupying the landing platform! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

This bee looks awfully dark for a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera). Maybe something else?
ScientificName: Hymenoptera; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae; Apis mellifera
associatedTaxa: Hymenoptera; Lepechinia calycina; Lamiaceae; Apis mellifera

File.Name: c410d_c409-035.jpg
Notes: 6920061720 This is a little Plume Moth (family Pterophoridae in the order Lepidoptera) perched on a dead weed stem in the woods. Their outstanding feature is their "cruciform" (cross-like) wings. More than 30 species are recorded in California! I wish I knew more about them. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

File.Name: c410e_c409-018.jpg
Notes: 7066112199 This is a mass of rather large "Lupine Aphids" (my name) in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera on stem of native Canyon Lupine (Lupinus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. They pack the stems and do some damage to the plants. I haven't found any ants tending them. They manage on their own! (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

File.Name: c410f_c409-030.jpg
Notes: 7066097883 Here is a beautiful grouping of flowers of native Blue-Eyed Grass (Sisyrinchium bellum) - not a real grass, but a native iris in the Iridaceae plant family. This is the only native iris that grows in our mountains. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

File.Name: c410g_c409-052.jpg
Notes:
6919978382 This native Dodder plant - probably California Dodder (Cuscuta californica) in the Cuscutaceae family - is taking over a branch of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It has small but rather pretty flowers. (San Marcos Pass, 9 April 2012)

Dodder is a parasitic plant without chlorophyll that gets nutrients from another green plant like this. It doesn't seem choosy. The early stems are bright orange, but they lose some color as they mature. You can see the bumpy "haustoria" - the structures that will penetrate the host's tissue.
ScientificName: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; haustoria
associatedTaxa: Cuscuta californica; Cuscutaceae; Ceanothus spinosus; Rhamnaceae; haustoria

File.Name: c410h_c404-050.jpg
Notes: 7066039363 This is native Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) in the Poaceae plant family growing in the woods near home. You wouldn't want it for a lawn, but it's a real native grass that sometimes forms a grassy understory in our woods. Native grasses are uncommon. I like it a lot. (San Marcos Pass, 4 April 2012)

File.Name: c411a_c411-032.jpg
Notes:
6923602752 Here is a blushing new flower of native Catalina Mariposa Lily (Calochortus catalinae) in the Liliaceae plant family, with raindrops. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

I was looking for plants in the Liliaceae plant family on Figueroa Mountain today. I only found two these, hiding in the non-native grasses along the road. I found no flowering Zigadenus or Fritillaria, which is what I was really looking for, though I did find vegetative plants. I guess one plant trick for dealing with a drought year is simply not to flower.

We got 1.83 inches of rain from this storm, mostly last night. That brings our total rainfall to 16.8 inches, still only 1/2 normal.

I had a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society board meeting tonight. I'm tired. Two photos will do just fine today. You can learn more about our natural history society here: www.syvnature.org/
CommonNames: Catalina Mariposa Lily; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society
ScientificName: Calochortus catalinae; Liliaceae; Liliaceae; Zigadenus; Fritillaria
associatedTaxa: Calochortus catalinae; Liliaceae; Liliaceae; Zigadenus; Fritillaria

File.Name: c411b_c411-014.jpg
Notes:
7069653279 These are beautiful flowers of the native Crinkled Onion (Allium crispum) in the Liliaceae plant family. Maybe it's not the best photo - it was breezy so I had to bump up my ISO, and it started to rain, so I had to shoot fast and run. But look closer - the flower in the lower-left is full of rain, and you see see the grassland in that raindrop! (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

I went looking for plants in the Liliaceae plant family on Figueroa Mountain today. I only found a few of these, but hopefully they are just starting. See my [Newer] photo.

File.Name: c412a_c412-013.jpg
Notes:
6925944980 These little sow bugs aka woodlice are "doing it" - making baby woodlice - under a leaf of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) for their umbrella. These critters are terrestrial crustaceans in the suborder Oniscidea of the order Isopoda of the subphylum Crustacea. They are often called pill bugs or roly-polys, but I'm pretty sure that these critters can't roll up into little balls, so most of the names are inappropriate. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2012)

I'm posting early (and just two photos again) because Julie and I are stepping out to see the Kronos Quartet perform an all Steve Reich program tonight at UCSB!

File.Name: c412b_c412-002.jpg
Notes: 6925929236 I found little "looper" type caterpillar (Lepidoptera) today on a leaflet of non-native Common Bedstraw (Galium aparine) in the Rubiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2012)

File.Name: c413a_c413-003.jpg
Notes:
6929271550 Our creek is flowing after 1.5+ inches of rain today! It's not flowing much, but this is real water running down between the rocks. It rained most of the day - thunder and lightning this morning, then showers and hail and drizzle.

We had a brief interlude when the sun came out this afternoon, so I went for a walk and got this photo - but it was a 20 minute walk in a 15 minute break in the rain. Now the wind is kicking up. I hope it won't blow all the new moisture from the ground! (San Marcos Pass, 13 April 2012)

We still have only about 50% normal rainfall, but these late-season rains are helping. I feel better now about the prospects for summer flowers.

File.Name: c413b_c412-005.jpg
Notes:
6929192858 This is a family group of Mule Deer (Odocoileus hemionus) on the wild hillside beyond our garden. That's the mama deer on the right, with her two "teenage" yearlings. Mama is browsing in a clump of native Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus). Our coastal deer are much smaller than the mule deer in eastern California and Oregon. (San Marcos Pass, 12 April 2012)

We've been seeing these native deer more and more lately since our beloved dog "Mojo" died a few years ago. The deer have been doing some damage to the garden. We need a new dog!

File.Name: c413c_c411-030.jpg
Notes: 7075246407 Here are brilliant new flowers of native Western Wallflower (Erysimum capitatum) in the Brassicaceae plant family growing on Figueroa Mountain this week. They are as orange as California Poppies. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

File.Name: c413d_c411-017.jpg
Notes:
6929133866 I originally thought this plant is a shy Calystegia malacophylla ssp. pedicellata in the Convolvulaceae plant family, but I'm wrong. It is really the South Coast Range Morning-glory (Calystegia collina ssp. venusta). The difference is that this plant has wavy leaves. Thank you again Lynn!

This morning-glory is growing on a serpentine rock outcrop on Figueroa Mountain. This is the only plant I found with flowers. Look close - the flowers are full of rain water! (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)
ScientificName: Calystegia malacophylla; pedicellata; Convolvulaceae; Calystegia collina; venusta
associatedTaxa: Calystegia malacophylla; pedicellata; Convolvulaceae; Calystegia collina; venusta

File.Name: c413e_c411-051.jpg
Notes: 6929097126 I believe this is a new flower of the very glandular Slender Tarweed (Madia gracilis) in the Asteraceae plant family. The pretty composite flowers open at night and close at dawn, so I rarely see them - but they get confused on rainy days and stay open. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

File.Name: c413f_c411-029.jpg
Notes: 7075163651 These are new flowers and foliage of Common Lomatium (Lomatium utriculatum) in the Apiaceae plant family, growing with the grasses. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

File.Name: c413g_c411-056.jpg
Notes: 7075138433 This is a sky-blue Sky Lupine (Lupinus nanus) in the Fabaceae plant family, with raindrops on its leaves. They are starting to come out with the poppies on that one hillside on Figueroa Mountain that usually gets covered with lupine and poppy. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

File.Name: c413h_c411-067.jpg
Notes:
6929008360 I stand corrected - thanks to NatureShutterbug! These are flowers of the other "California Poppy" in our mountains - Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family.

The difference is that these poppies lack the collar below the flower that Eschscholzia californica has. I should have recognized it, but I think of Eschscholzia caespitosa as more yellow. See my old photo at:
   www.fotolog.com/treebeard/34445165/

And the lupine is really the Silver Lupine (Lupinus albifrons). L. longifolius is more uniformly blue and coastal. (I do better close to home!)

Original comment: "Poppies and lupines are such a beautiful combination! These are California Poppies (Eschscholzia californica, Papaveraceae) and Bush Lupine (Lupinus longifolius, Fabaceae). The poppies are all closed because it's lightly raining as I got this photo. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

"Did you spot that intrusive non-native Wild Oat (Avena fatua) grass on the right? It's the classic "Photographer's Dilemma" - do I alter the scene by removing it to make a better photo? Well, in this case the decision was easy - it was a steep hillside, and I would trample the poppies. Let it be.

"Hah - they are all buried under snow today!
Locality: Avena fatua) grass on the right? It's the classic "Photographer's Dilemma" - do I alter the scene by removing it to make a better photo? Well

associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia caespitosa; Lupinus albifrons; L. longifolius; Eschscholzia californica; Papaveraceae; Lupinus longifolius; Fabaceae; Avena fatua

File.Name: c414a_c414-017.jpg
Notes:
7078567059 These are colorful new Blewit (Clitocybe nuda) mushrooms in the woods - late, but always welcome! They are edible, but not incredible. The mass of mycelium and leaves at the base is distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2012)

Be careful though - these mushrooms start blue, but they soon fade to gray or tan. And we also have an inedible blue Cortinarius mushroom in the woods that looks very similar. Caveat emptor!

It started windy today, and grew to gale force. Not a good day for photos, but I got a few. I hope the wind will calm down tonight.

File.Name: c414b_c414-002.jpg
Notes:
7078521075 Any mushrooms are interesting this late in our rainy season - and I'm happy to find them!

I guess these are Clitocybe mushrooms with white spores and decurrent gills that grow down the stalk, but I don't know what kind they are. They have dry funnel-shaped caps. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2012)

File.Name: c414c_c414-011.jpg
Notes:
6932404006 This is another small Planthopper insect in the superfamily Fulgoroidea of the insect order Homoptera. I guess it's an Issid Planthopper (Issidae) - maybe Danepteryx robusta with those narrow wings? It's perched by new flower buds of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2012)

Those skinny wings can't be useful for flying, but maybe they help it glide when it hops? They have amazing eyes. I guess they need them to see where they're hopping.
ScientificName: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Danepteryx robusta; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae
associatedTaxa: Fulgoroidea; Homoptera; Issidae; Danepteryx robusta; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae

File.Name: c414d_c414-026.jpg
Notes: 7078440465 This is a handsome little Sheetweb Spider (Linyphiidae) hanging upside-down from it's web in the woods. This spider is a lady. You can see her "epigynum" in the middle, close to where her abdomen meets her cephalothorax. (San Marcos Pass, 14 April 2012)

File.Name: c414e_c411-057.jpg
Notes: 6932330878 I guess these are flowers of the Caterpillar Phacelia (Phacelia cicutaria var. hispida) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. They are growing on the poppy/lupine hillside on Figueroa Mountain. The reddish stems are handsome. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

File.Name: c414f_c411-059.jpg
Notes: 6932284338 This odd grass is Goldentop (Lamarckia aurea) in the Poaceae plant family. Unfortunately it's not native. It's an invader from the mediterranean that has found its way to California, but it is pretty. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

File.Name: c414g_c411-024.jpg
Notes: 6932252436 These are odd flowers of native California Plantain (Plantago erecta) in the Plantaginaceae) plant family. The lobes of the flower are scarious - thin, membranous, and dry. This one is growing on serpentine soil. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

File.Name: c414h_c411-062.jpg
Notes:
6932181308 I stand corrected - thanks to NatureShutterbug! These are flowers of the other "California Poppy" in our mountains - Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family. And so are the poppies in yesterday's photo. The flowers are closed because it was raining. (Figueroa Mountain, 11 April 2012)

The difference is that these poppies lack the collar below the flower that Eschscholzia californica has. I should have recognized it, but I think of Eschscholzia caespitosa as more yellow. See my old photo at:
   www.fotolog.com/treebeard/34445165/
ScientificName: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia caespitosa
associatedTaxa: Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; Eschscholzia californica; Eschscholzia caespitosa

File.Name: c415a_c415-041.jpg
Notes:
7082672299 I found this tiny spider hanging under a leaf blade of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass today. Looking at its eyes, I guess this is a little Crab Spider (Thomisidae). It looks so green and strange, almost like a SEM photo. That's because the grass leaf is being a natural "light tent" with the sun behind. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

It was still breezy today, but not too bad, and there were calm moments for photos.

File.Name: c415b_c415-024.jpg
Notes:
6936546724 I really enjoy taking photos of these tiny Jumping Spiders (Salticidae). They stand their ground! This one is raising its pedipalps to scare me off. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

This little spider is perched on a leaf of a Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family.

File.Name: c415c_c415-015.jpg
Notes:
6936523858 This is a gorgeous little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) working a single flower of non-native (but ubiquitous) Chickweed (Stellaria media) in the Caryophyllaceae plant family. That gives a sense of scale! (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

Darn, the focus isn't quite right. I always seem to focus a bit too far away.

File.Name: c415d_c415-020.jpg
Notes:
7082549033 This is a tiny Tephritid Fruit Fly (family Tephritidae, order Diptera) that I found in the woods today on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. They have amazing eyes. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

I think these flies might gall the flowers. See my photo (and follow the link) at:
   http://www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6889741123/

File.Name: c415e_c415-007.jpg
Notes:
6936365116 I believe these are furry new galls of the Round Gall Wasp (Besbicus conspicuus) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera - but cp. the Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp (Disholcaspis washingtonensis). They are growing on twigs of Scrub Oak (Quercus berberidifolia in the Fagaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

There's an old gall of the Tapered Stem Gall Wasp (Andricus spectabilis) in the background. That one is "integral" which means you can't detach it without breaking the stem. The new galls are "detachable" because you could easily break them off. Galls on galls!

Compare my older photo that might well show the same galls a month ago:
   www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6986357357/

These are the most common galls in the woods right now. The scrub oaks are "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once about now, then get new leaves at about the same time. I'm sure I'll find new leaf galls soon.
Locality:
Andricus spectabilis) in the background. That one is "integral" which means you can't detach it without breaking the stem. The new galls are "detachable" because you could easily break them off. Galls on galls!

Compare my older photo that might well show the same galls a month ago:
   www.flickr.com/photos/treebeard/6986357357/

These are the most common galls in the woods right now. The scrub oaks are "semi-deciduous" - they drop their leaves all at once about now
CommonNames: Round Gall Wasp; Fuzzy Oak Gall Wasp; Scrub Oak; Tapered Stem Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus spectabilis
associatedTaxa: Besbicus conspicuus; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Disholcaspis washingtonensis; Quercus berberidifolia; Fagaceae; Andricus spectabilis

File.Name: c415f_c415-037.jpg
Notes:
7082413459 These are spiky new fruit of native Bitter Gooseberry (Ribes amarum) in the Grossulariaceae plant family. The fruit don't ripen until late summer. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

The ripe fruit are edible and tasty if you can get past the spines. The trick is to carefully split the berry between your front teeth and then squeeze out the flesh inside.

File.Name: c415g_c415-013.jpg
Notes:
7082362215 This is a grass-like flower stalk of native Fairy Lantern (Calochortus albus) in the Liliaceae plant family - it's anything but a grass! These are growing by a cluster of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass, and you really have to look close to tell them apart. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

The big difference is that the grasses have leaves that sheath the stem, but these lilies don't. The lilies also have a large strap-like leaf at the base of the stem, but it's easy to miss.

File.Name: c415h_c415-036.jpg
Notes:
7082291211 This is looking into our rarest native habitat - it's a real native grassland under the oaks, with Thin Grass (Agrostis pallens) and Giant Rye Grass (Leymus condensatus) in the Poaceae plant family, and the odd dry-land Roundfruit Sedge (Carex globosa) in the Cyperaceae plant family.

The grasses and sedges form a real understory under the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. That bare patch in the center should be a small wetland, but it's been a dry year. There are Juncus rushes growing around it. (San Marcos Pass, 15 April 2012)

I call this the "little mushroom woods" - as opposed to the "big mushroom woods"! But the time for mushrooms seems to be over, despite the recent rains and a few oddballs. Of course, I'll keep looking.
CommonNames: Thin Grass; Giant Rye Grass; Roundfruit Sedge; Coast Live Oak
ScientificName: Agrostis pallens; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Juncus
associatedTaxa: Agrostis pallens; Leymus condensatus; Poaceae; Carex globosa; Cyperaceae; Quercus agrifolia; Juncus

File.Name: c416a_c416-001.jpg
Notes:
6939954562 Here is a pretty little Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae, order Diptera) perched on the edge of a new flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can clearly see the "spurious vein" on its wings if you know what to look for. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

I've been looking for signs of drought in this very dry winter - we stand at only 54% normal rainfall. One thing I've noticed is that native shrubs are flowering more spread out, rather than all at once. These blackberrys have been going since the beginning of March, and they're really just beginning, with lots of new buds. Eastwood Manzanita (Arctostaphylos glandulosa) and Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) are also still going strong. Of course it has everything to do with recent rains.
CommonNames: Syrphid Flower Fly; Wild Blackberry; Eastwood Manzanita; Greenbark Ceanothus
ScientificName: Syrphidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ceanothus spinosus
associatedTaxa: Syrphidae; Diptera; Rubus ursinus; Rosaceae; Arctostaphylos glandulosa; Ceanothus spinosus

File.Name: c416b_c416-032.jpg
Notes:
7085982733 These are gorgeous new sky-blue flowers of Chia Sage (Salvia columbariae) in the Lamiaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

They are uncommon in our coastal mountains, but they are a common desert plant. I guess I shouldn't be surprised to find them, even in a drought year.

File.Name: c416c_c416-018.jpg
Notes:
6939876408 This is an interesting fly (order Diptera) perched on a leaf of Fremont's Star Lily aka Chaparral Death Camas (Zigadenus fremontii var. inezianus) in the Liliaceae plant family. It looks like we will get some flowers this year! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

I was happy to find these flower buds. I'd just about given up on these interesting flowers in this drought year. Despite the recent rains, we're still only at 54% normal rainfall.
CommonNames: fly; Fremont's Star Lily; Chaparral Death Camas

File.Name: c416d_c416-029.jpg
Notes: 6939827386 I found these "Little Brown Mushrooms" growing in the grass along my trail today. They have attached gills and brown spores. They are pretty nondescript, but maybe they are the Spring Agrocybe (Agrocybe praecox) mushroom? (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

File.Name: c416e_c416-023.jpg
Notes: 6939812622 I found another little Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly ovipositing on a native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) plant in the Fabaceae plant family today. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

File.Name: c416f_c416-013.jpg
Notes:
7085826873 This is the first Grasshopper in the family Acrididae of the order Orthoptera that I've managed to capture this year. It has blue on its abdomen. Here it is on my stony trail today. It has "Pretty Good Camouflage". (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

I don't know what it is, but I'll bet aliceinwl1 does!

File.Name: c416g_c416-024.jpg
Notes:
7085791649 This is a handsome little Crab Spider (Thomisidae). I found it hanging in the shade under a white-haired leaf of endemic native Trask's Yerba Santa (Eriodictyon traskiae) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family. This spider has interesting wings on its Cephalothorax. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

These native shrubs have new buds. It won't be long until they start to flower.

File.Name: c416h_c416-015.jpg
Notes: 7085767381 This is the nest of an unusual (though locally common) spider in our woods. I think it's a Coneweb Spider aka Desertshrub Spider in the family Diguetidae. It has a nest like an inverted thimble and only 6 eyes. I'll keep trying to get a face shot. If you look close, you can make out the spider inside. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

File.Name: c417a_c417-034.jpg
Notes:
7089257141 This is a single flower of the rare Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family growing in our mountains. It's a beautiful lily-relative, maybe 3 feet tall. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2012)

I must have taken 100 photos of this flower today to get this one keeper, but it was worth it. I waited a long time for the sun to move to bring it some light - and even then, it was breezy and the petals act light a natural light tent which make everything too green. So be it.

I'm going to "stealth" the location of this wonderful flower, but I'll show the dirt road to walk down. (I also "stealth" our home, though I show the canyon.)

File.Name: c417b_c417-036.jpg
Notes:
6943156676 This is almost the whole plant of the Ojai Fritillary (Fritillaria ojaiensis) in the Liliaceae plant family - with a flower and buds. It's a beautiful lily-relative, maybe 3 feet tall. It is quite rare in our mountains, and it's not having a good time in this drought year, with only about 50% normal rainfall so far. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2012)

I've only found two plants (only this one with flowers) on San Marcos Pass, though it also grows along the Tequepis Trail and Refugio Road that I know of. All these sites are on the north-facing slopes of our east-west trending mountains.
Locality: only this one with flowers) on San Marcos Pass

File.Name: c417c_c417-021.jpg
Notes:
6943121026 Here are fantastic new flowers of native Snowdrop Bush (Styrax redivivus) in the Styracaceae plant family. The flowers have a strong, sweet fragrance like perfume. They seem almost too beautiful to be native chaparral plants, but here they are. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2012)

See my earlier photo for more info.

File.Name: c417d_c417-008.jpg
Notes: 6943103686 Here is a tiny "micro-moth" (Lepidoptera) perched on a leaf of (I think) Branching Phacelia (Phacelia ramosissima var. austrolitoralis) in the Hydrophyllaceae plant family this morning. I have no idea what kind of moth it is. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2012)

File.Name: c417e_c417-005.jpg
Notes:
7089122529 These are a family group of dark aphids in the family Aphididae in the order Homoptera. They are on a new leaf of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2012)

If you look close, it sure looks like the large aphid at the bottom of my photo is squeezing out a baby. I reckon the large aphids are the "fundatrices" for this colony - the first aphids that produce new aphids parthenogenetically. Start with Wikipedia for more info.

File.Name: c417f_c416-025.jpg
Notes:
6943018932 These are tiny Lace Bugs in the family Tingidae of the order Hemiptera working a leaf of native Greenbark Ceanothus (Ceanothus spinosus) in the Rhamnaceae plant family. The two look a bit different, so they may be male and female. They have a remarkable net-like lattice-work on their body that reminds me of shogun-era samurai armor - but it also looks like bubble-wrap! (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

I wonder if those tar-like black spots on the leaf are their eggs?

File.Name: c417g_c416-003.jpg
Notes:
7089073035 "It's a dirty job, but someone has to do it!"

This is a tiny Wild Bee (Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) taking a breather in a flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. This bee has collected some pollen in its pollen baskets, but it's mostly interested in the nectar at the base of the stamens. (San Marcos Pass, 16 April 2012)

File.Name: c417h_c417-002.jpg
Notes: 7089046463 I saw a couple of new migratory birds in the trees today - a Wilson's Warbler (Wilsonia pusilla) and (I think) an unusual Hermit Warbler (Dendroica occidentalis). I mounted my long lens and... scared them away. But I did get a photo of this sweet little House Wren (Troglodytes aedon). We have several pairs of them nesting in bird houses around our home. (San Marcos Pass, 17 April 2012)
CommonNames: Wilson's Warbler; Hermit Warbler; House Wren
ScientificName: Wilsonia pusilla; Dendroica occidentalis; Troglodytes aedon
associatedTaxa: Wilsonia pusilla; Dendroica occidentalis; Troglodytes aedon

File.Name: c418a_c418-047.jpg
Notes:
7095018683 This might be the only native Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Liliaceae plant family flowering on Figueroa Mountain this year! At least it's the only one I've found so far, and I've been looking. It's growing in a mass of weedy grasses. What's a nice native flower like you doing in this company? (Figueroa Mountain, 18 April 2012)

This flower has become almost an emblem of Figueroa Mountain in the past few years, but it's very scarce in this drought year.

I had my class at Dunn Middle School today, followed by a Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society public lecture by James Lawrence Powell on Global Warming. Very good lecture, but I was tired when I got home about 10 pm. I'm posting late and changing the date.
CommonNames: Chocolate Lily; Santa Ynez Valley Natural History Society

File.Name: c418b_c418-056.jpg
Notes: 7094989377 This is a new flower head of beautiful native Red-skinned Onion (Allium haematochiton) in the Liliaceae plant family. They are scarce this year, but I found a few today on the saddle between Figueroa Mountain and Ranger Peak. (Figueroa Mountain, 18 April 2012)

File.Name: c418c_c418-042.jpg
Notes:
6948868936 You never go wrong with poppies and lupines!

These are Sky Lupines (Lupinus nanus) in the Fabaceae plant family and Tufted Poppy (Eschscholzia caespitosa) in the Papaveraceae plant family growing together near the top of Figueroa Mountain. What can I say? (Figueroa Mountain, 18 April 2012)

They do get kind of boring to photograph after a while... you wonder, what next? (Answer - lots of flowers and critters!)

If you look close at the bottom of the flowers and the seed pods, you can see that these poppies lack the disk below the flowers. That makes them E. caespitosa rather that E. californica. I think of these as the real California Poppies since so many of the others come from seed packets.
Locality:
Answer - lots of flowers and critters!)

If you look close at the bottom of the flowers and the seed pods
ScientificName: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; E. caespitosa; E. californica; the real
associatedTaxa: Lupinus nanus; Fabaceae; Eschscholzia caespitosa; Papaveraceae; E. caespitosa; E. californica; the real

File.Name: c418d_c418-033.jpg
Notes:
6948842302 These are beautiful new flowers of native "Checker Bloom" (Sidalcea malviflora) in the Malvaceae plant family. I got this photo near Lake Cachuma. They're not quite ready to flower at home in the mountains yet. (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 April 2012)

I don't understand the common name. There's nothing checkered about these flowers!

File.Name: c419a_c419-020.jpg
Notes:
6949069340 Welcome to my world!

I'm sure this is an immature Katydid in the family Tettigoniidae of the suborder Ensifera, in the order Orthoptera. So cute and fragile! (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2012)

It was warm today - the the 80s, a taste of summer.

File.Name: c419b_c419-001.jpg
Notes:
7095109021 Here is a large black male Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera. It's working a native flower of Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2012)

This bee is running a cheat on the wildflower. Instead of going through the throat of the flower and helping pollination, the bee is taking a shortcut and going right through the calyx to get to the nectar. Many insects know this trick.
ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Lathyrus latifolius; Fabaceae

File.Name: c419c_c419-012.jpg
Notes: 7095074441 I guess this is the same kind of late-season mushroom that I showed a few days ago - probably genus Clitocybe with decurrent gills growing down the stem. Look at those little Drosophilid Flies (Drosophilidae, Diptera) on the mushroom cap. (San Marcos Pass, 19 April 2012)

File.Name: c419d_c418-049.jpg
Notes: 6948985050 This is almost the whole plant of a beautiful native Chocolate Lily (Fritillaria biflora) in the Liliaceae plant family flowering on Figueroa Mountain. It's growing in a mass of weedy grasses. (Figueroa Mountain, 18 April 2012)

File.Name: c420a_c420-022.jpg
Notes:
7097941213 This is a beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly ovipositing (laying eggs) on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. She perched here quietly for some time and let me get a photo. See my [Older] photo. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2012)

It was downright HOT today, 95∞ by my thermometer on the porch in the shade. I don't think I'm ready for this!

File.Name: c420b_c420-024.jpg
Notes: 6951857050 Here is a beautiful green Bramble Hairstreak (Callophrys dumetorum) butterfly ovipositing (laying eggs) on a stem of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. If you look close, you can see her egg emerging! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2012)

File.Name: c420c_c420-018.jpg
Notes: 6951840490 I found this beautiful Silvery Blue (Glaucopsyche lygdamus) butterfly perched on a leaf of Bush Monkey Flower (Mimulus longiflorus) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family in the canyon today. It sat here for a while so I could get the photo. I wish all butterflies would do that! (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2012)

File.Name: c420d_c420-001.jpg
Notes:
6951802248 These are the "window ants" in our bedroom tearing apart a Western Paper Wasp (Mischocyttarus flavitarsis or kin) in the family Vespidae of the order Hymenoptera this morning. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2012)

I know, why wouldn't the wasp just fly away? But I'm sure (first hand!) that the ants have some potent venom. These little ants (family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are really vicious. They bite, and it stings!
Locality: family Formicidae in the order Hymenoptera) are really vicious. They bite
ScientificName: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Formicidae; Hymenoptera
associatedTaxa: Mischocyttarus flavitarsis; Vespidae; Hymenoptera; Formicidae; Hymenoptera

File.Name: c420e_c420-030.jpg
Notes: 7097831101 I found this handsome Beetle (order Coleoptera) under a rock by our dry creek today. It has great antennae! It was flying actively like a Tiger Beetle (Cicindelidae), but the antennae are wrong. Maybe it's a Scarab Beetle ( Scarabaeidae)? (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2012)

File.Name: c420f-c418-016.jpg
Notes:
7097785813 I've been noticing migrating birds in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees lately, and this is an interesting one. I believe it's a beautiful adult male Nashville Warbler (Vermivora ruficapilla). They are uncommon in Santa Barbara County. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2012)

Migratory birds come over San Marcos Pass on their way north, just like motorists.

File.Name: c420g_c418-006.jpg
Notes:
7097746117 OK folks, I changed the name. I usually look down on the ground when I look for photos, so I get confused looking up. Thanks!

This is another little migrating yellow warbler that I found in the flowering Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree near our upstairs porch. It's got a worm in its mouth.

My first thought was Hutton's Vireo (Vireo huttoni), but it doesn't have the characteristic "broken eye-ring". Maybe it's an "Orange-crowned Warbler" (Vermivora celatat) or a Common Yellowthroat (Geothlypis trichas)? Whatever it is, it's a cute little bird! (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2012)
CommonNames: Coast Live Oak; Hutton's Vireo; Common Yellowthroat
ScientificName: Quercus agrifolia; Vireo huttoni; Geothlypis trichas
associatedTaxa: Quercus agrifolia; Vireo huttoni; Geothlypis trichas

File.Name: c420h_c418-011.jpg
Notes: 6951659348 I've been noticing migrating birds in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees lately. This is a beautiful male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi). They stay around for a while in the fall/winter, but they head off in spring. (San Marcos Pass, 18 April 2012)

File.Name: c421a_c421-025.jpg
Notes:
6954719066 This is a beautiful new Acmon Blue (Plebejus acmon) butterfly perched on a leaf of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It's the first one I've seen this year. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

Today was a few degree cooler than yesterday - 90∞ rather than 95∞. That's still too hot for comfort!

Julie and I may take off on a trip to the fabulous Carizzo Plains beyond the backcountry tomorrow. We'll be gone for a few days.

File.Name: c421b_c421-006.jpg
Notes:
6954692394 We don't usually think of grasses as having flowers, but here they are!

These are new flowers of native California Brome (Bromus carinatus) grass in the Poaceae plant family growing on the dry edge of our garden. You can see the yellow stamens that release pollen and feathery white pistils that receive it. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

This grass has short awns, which distinguishes it from weedy grasses like Ripgut Grass (Bromus diandrus) and Red Brome (Bromus rubens) and the rest of their kin that are trying to take over the woodland and grassland. We've been encouraging it around the house and it's having a good year. You have to know something about grasses to recognize it.
CommonNames: California Brome; Ripgut Grass; Red Brome
ScientificName: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens
associatedTaxa: Bromus carinatus; Poaceae; awns; Bromus diandrus; Bromus rubens

File.Name: c421c_c421-026.jpg
Notes: 6954675440 I'm always surprised to find a Moth (Ledpidoptera) in the woods in the daytime. This one is resting on a branch of native Deerweed (Lotus scoparius) in the Fabaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

File.Name: c421d_c421-032.jpg
Notes:
7100713519 I spotted this tiny Wild Bee (Apoidae, order Hymenoptera) on a damp rock along the trail to "The Playground" today. I have no idea what kind of bee it is. It kept coming back to the same place, like a dragonfly. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

Someone out there will surely know what kind of bee this is!

File.Name: c421e_c421-036.jpg
Notes: 6954605480 These are new flowers of (I believe) the Hairy Suncup (Camissonia hirtella) in the Onagraceae plant family. The stems keep producing new flowers from the front as they continue to grow. They are growing in a place that burned a few years ago in the Gap Fire which threatened homes on The Pass. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

File.Name: c421f_c421-029.jpg
Notes:
6954563302 This is a beautiful flower of Coastal Morning-Glory (Calystegia macrostegia ssp. cyclostegia) in the Convolvulaceae plant family. These flowers have the bracts close to the flower base, appearing like part of the calyx. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

These flowers have taken over on the "Gap Wildfire" burn a few years ago, but they are backing off this year. I don't know if it's the drought or just the natural cycle of fire-follower plant to bloom like crazy and then disappear, until the next wildfire.

File.Name: c421g_c420-037.jpg
Notes: 6954523040 This is another impossibly thin Stilt Bug in the family Berytidae of the order Hemiptera perched on a new flower of native California Figwort (Scrophularia californica) in the Scrophulariaceae) plant family. It's just starting to flower in our canyon. You can see the bug's slender procoscis. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2012)

File.Name: c421h_c418-026.jpg
Notes:
6954491580 This gorgeous fly (order Diptera) is perched on a leaf of deciduous Blue Oak (Quercus douglasii) in the Fagaceae plant family growing near Lake Cachuma. Maybe it's a Syrphid Flower Fly (family Syrphidae)? (Lake Cachuma, Santa Ynez Valley, 18 April 2012)

Look close, you can make out the ommatidia or "simple eyes" that make up its compound eyes. That's my hallmark for fly photos. "Get the eyes sharp and the rest will follow.
CommonNames: fly; Blue Oak; Syrphid Flower Fly
ScientificName: Diptera; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae; Syrphidae; ommatidia
associatedTaxa: Diptera; Quercus douglasii; Fagaceae; Syrphidae; ommatidia

File.Name: c422a_c422-007.jpg
Notes:
7104908563 This was a lucky photo. My camera happened to be set on self-timer with a 2 second delay from my previous photo, and this large bee flew into the photo after I pressed the button!

It's a female Carpenter Bee (maybe Xylocopa tabaniformis?) in the family Anthophoridae of the order Hymenoptera. She's on a cultivar of native Douglas iris (Iris douglasiana) in the Iridaceae plant family growing on the dry edge of our garden. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2012)

We didn't make it off to the Carizzo Plains today. Maybe next week...
ScientificName: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Iris douglasiana; Iridaceae
associatedTaxa: Xylocopa tabaniformis; Anthophoridae; Hymenoptera; Iris douglasiana; Iridaceae

File.Name: c422b_c422-003.jpg
Notes:
6958696556 I found this late-season Agaricus mushroom in the woods today. I won't try to guess what species it is. I don't trust any of these woodland Agaricus mushrooms to eat. (San Marcos Pass, 22 April 2012)

It has a broad flat cap which stains yellow, but the base of the stem does not stain yellow. (So it's not A. xanthodermis). The gills are quite gray. It tastes nutty at first, but that quickly turns to phenolic. It has a thin membranous partial veil ring. (So it's not A. hondensis.) I don't know what it is.
Locality: So it's not A. xanthodermis). The gills are quite gray. It tastes nutty at first

Notes:
7104529597 This is the other Miner's Lettuce in our mountains - Claytonia parviflora, not C. perfoliata, in the Portulacaceae plant family. It's growing in a place that didn't burn, but was recently cleared. I've been finding lots of interesting fire-follower plants here. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

This Small-flowered Miner's Lettuce resembles it's namesake, but it has narrower basal leaves. I often find plants that are reddish-colored.

File.Name: c422g_c421-013.jpg

7104496579 This is looking into a flowering cluster of the native grass Small-flowered Melic (Mellica imperfecta) in the Poaceae plant family. The small dark flowers are distinctive. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

It does well after wildfires and brush clearings, but it's also doing well in the woods around home, not quite so robust.

File.Name: c422h_c418-022.jpg
Notes:
6958353492 These are interesting tumor-like galls on flowers of Common Fiddleneck (Amsinkia menziesii var. intermedia - aka A. intermedia) in the Boraginaceae plant family. You can see a couple of stunted orange flowers. The plant is a native, but kind of weedy - in fact it takes over whole fields, and it is spreading around the world. (Santa Ynez Valley, 18 March 2010)

I believe this photo shows the gall of the Fiddleneck Gall Nematode (Anguina amsinckia) that induces the plant to form floral galls that replace the flowers. Notice that there are few flowers on the galled plant. I read on the Web that about 40,000 nematodes occur in an average 3/8-inch diameter gall!

The mind boggles... This might just be a way to naturally control this homebrew weed. There's more info at:
   http://ucce.ucdavis.edu/files/repositoryfiles/ca3907p4-62885.pdf
