7108487659 Here is a Honey Bee (Apis mellifera) working the newly opened flowers of native Sugar Bush (Rhus ovata) in the Anacardiaceae plant family. These native shrubs form their flower buds in autumn, and then wait months to open them. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

It didn't officially rain today, but we ended up with 1/10 inch of drizzle in our rain gauge by the end of the day! It was too wet for new photos. Real rain is likely tomorrow night!

File.Name: c423b_c420-016.jpg
Notes: 6962382596 This is a handsome little "true bug" in the woods. It seems to have 5-segmented antennae, so I guess it is a "stink bug" in the family Pentatomidae in the order Hemiptera. I found it perched on a leaf of native Canyon Sunflower (Venegasia carpesioides) in the Asteraceae plant family. I don't know anything about it, except here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 20 April 2012)

File.Name: c423c_c421-016.jpg
Notes:
7108405403 This is a wild mustard, but it's not one of those pesky (but showy) introduced mustards that have taken over California.

I believe this is California Mustard (Thelypodium lasiophyllum aka Guillenia lasiophylla) in the Brassicaceae plant family. It's a real native mustard that grows as a fire-follower on burns and clearings in our mountains. It has long seed-pods that spread out from the stem. It's not common. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

File.Name: c423d_c423-001.jpg
Notes:
7108311063 Where there's smoke there's fire, and where there's a pupating Woolly Bear caterpillar there will soon be a Tiger Moth! (See my photo of the caterpillar.)

Does the common name "vestal" mean springtime (like "vernal")? Most of our Tiger Moths arrive later in the summer.

I broke down and used flash for this photo, though I really don't like the too-sharp look. I couldn't get the white-balance right without it, and this pretty moth has to look white!

like "vernal")? Most of our Tiger Moths arrive later in the summer.

File.Name: c423g_c421-042.jpg
Notes:
6961963240 I have to learn more about Wild Blackberries...

These are flowers of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing along the trail to "The Playground" in our mountains. They looked strange and I took a photo, but I didn't realize what was strange until I got the photo up on my computer screen. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

These flowers are all pistillate, whereas our flowers at home are mostly staminate with a small central core of pistils. Sure enough, the Jepson Flora says that most flowers are unisexual. So what gives with our flowers at home, just a few miles away? (Maybe that's why I rarely find fruit?)

These familiar flowers have become strange and interesting!

File.Name: c423h_c421-015.jpg
Notes: 6961941768 These are more new open flowers of a native Holly-Leaved Redberry (Rhamnus ilicifolia) shrub in the Rhamnaceae plant family. It sort of looks like a scrub oak, but the 4-merous flowers are followed by bright red berries rather than acorns. (San Marcos Pass, 21 April 2012)

File.Name: c424a_c424-023.jpg
Notes:
7111578225 This is looking very close at the venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) that I show in my [Older] photo. What a wonderfully menacing face! It has its tongue out, tasting me from a distance. It didn't much like the taste, and moved away up the hillside out of sight. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2012)

Damn, I almost stepped on it! It was curled up near a flower of Agoseris grandiflora that I was trying to photograph. I walked all around the plant. I'll have a photo tomorrow, before I saw the rattlesnake!

By the way, don't wait to hear or see a rattlesnake's rattle. The real signs of a rattlesnake are the black color and the triangular head.

A new rainstorm is moving in tonight. I didn't expect much in the way of photos today, but I found: snakes, slime molds, mushrooms, flies, and new flowers, oh my! It was a good photo-day.

File.Name: c424b_c424-022.jpg
Notes:
6965472154 I found this wonderfully menacing venomous Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) - "la serpiente de cascabel" - along our road today. It was 3+ feet long, with 7 rattles on it's tail. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2012)

This snake never rattled. Maybe it was cold on a gloomy day? It checked me out (see my [Newer] photo) and moved away up the hillside.

I'm not particularly afraid of rattlesnakes, but I do worry about stepping on them. When I inadvertently step of our cat, she hisses - but if you step on a rattlesnake, they might strike! Really, the snakes do everything they can to avoid contact. Respect their wishes - "Don't tread on me!

File.Name: c424c_c424-003.jpg
Notes:
6965423256 Julie found this odd bubbly colony of Slime Molds (Myxomycetes or Myxogastria) in the garden this morning. They are NOT mushrooms or fungi, thought they look kind of fungus-like. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2012)

Slime Molds are remarkable critters that seem to be part fungus and part animal. They are Mycetozoa - which literally means "fungus-animals" - but they are really animals, not fungi.

Mycetozoa live as microscopic predatory single-celled amoeba-like protists in decaying wood and humus. When the weather is right, they get an "urge to merge" and stream together to form a visible multi-nucleate "plasmodium" without cell membranes. They end up as tiny mushroom-like structures like these that release spores that disperse to become new single-celled amoeba-like protists to colonize a new substrate. It's a perfect strategy to move from one bit of decaying wood to another.

These slime molds are still coming together, so I don't know what they will finally look like. I'll try for another photo tomorrow.

File.Name: c424d_c424-036.jpg
Notes:
6965404048 These are beautiful dark-spored Inky Cap (genus Coprinus) mushrooms coming up along our road. They are quite large - maybe 2 inches across. The caps are thinner than tissue paper and easier to tear. They look like diaphanous undersea creatures. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2012)

Mushrooms in this genus "deliquesce" - they literally digest themselves into black slime!

File.Name: c424e_c424-011.jpg

They are growing from an old stump of Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree in the Fagaceae plant family. They fruited here earlier this year, as early as November. It's odd to see them again this year. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2012)

These mushrooms are quite poisonous, and have a bitter taste to deter accidents.

Notes:
6965334602 This is an amazing Tachinid Fly in the family Tachinidae of the order Diptera working a flower of native Wild Blackberry (Rubus ursinus) in the Rosaceae plant family. You can see its proboscis deep in the flower where there is nectar. It's quite large, but not as large as a honeybee. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2012)

Note that this blackberry flower has stamens, quite different from the flowers in my photo yesterday.

D'oh - I took this photo at f/20 because I had peviously got a photo of a mushroom and I forgot to set it back. It's not quite sharp, but not too bad.

File.Name: c424h_c424-048.jpg
Notes:
6965257886 This is the uncommon native Shiny-Leaved Barberry (Berberis pinnata) in the Berberidaceae plant family - in full flower on a gloomy day. (San Marcos Pass, 24 April 2012)

This shrub is common in Oregon - aka Oregon Grape - but it's uncommon in our mountains behind Santa Barbara. I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines, and I've never seen a berry. I guess it's a remnant plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change. (The climate has been changing since the end of the Pleistocene!)

Wednesday is my busy day, so 4 photos will do just fine.

File.Name: c425b_c425-005.jpg
Notes:
7114461743 This isn't the best photo, but it shows something interesting. This small wasp (Hymenoptera) is carrying a much larger caterpillar (Lepidoptera) down a tendril of native Wild Sweetpea (Lathyrus latifolius) in the Fabaceae plant family. It was trying to get to the ground. After trying all the paths along the tendril, it carried its load back up and came back down a different stem. This time it made it, and disappeared in the undergrowth. (San Marcos Pass, 25 April 2012)

It was gloomy and breezy this morning, and both wasp and caterpillar were in motion. The photo is interesting because this wasp is carrying its prey, instead of laying its egg on the prey. Different wasps have different strategies.

File.Name: c426a_c426-041.jpg
Notes:
7117709583 This is a new flower of native Thimbleberry (Rubus parviflorus) in the Rosaceae plant family growing in our canyon. It's quite uncommon in our mountains behind Santa Barbara. I only find it in a few shady places where the sun never shines, and I've never found the edible (but thin) fruit here. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2012)

They are common and almost weedy in the Sierras and Oregon, but they are uncommon here in the Santa Barbara mountains. I guess it's a relict plant from wetter times in the past that's managed to hold on so far against climate change.

We ended up with a disappointing 0.37 inches of rain from our storm last night and today. That's not much, but every bit helps this late in a drought year. We stand at 18.65 inches of rain the year, just 54% of normal.

File.Name: c426b_c426-048.jpg
Notes:
7117685227 I believe this is a flower head of native Stinging Lupine (Lupinus hirsutissimus) in the Fabaceae plant family. It doesn't really sting like a nettle, but it does have long stiff hairs like one. They are quite common in our canyon at home. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2012)

Somehow I had the impression that Lupinus hirsutissimus was larger - but it's one of our small lupines.

File.Name: c426c_c426-050.jpg
Notes:
7117647047 I believe these are "Poison Pie" (Hebeloma crustuliniforme or kin) mushrooms in the woods today. They have viscid caps and brown spores. Look at the mushroom on the left - it's a field mark of these 'shrooms that they often are covered with tiny Springtail (order Collembola) insects that get stuck in the slime. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2012)

Like the common name suggests, don't eat them. They cause mild to severe gastrointestinal distress.

This is another mushroom that fruited way back in December and is coming back again because of our recent Spring rains.

File.Name: c426d_c426-032.jpg
Notes:
6971531742 I guess these impressive large mushrooms are the Felt-Ringed Agaricus (Agaricus hondensis) mushroom. They have a thick ring that stands out from the stalk and stains yellow, like the base. The cap tastes mild. But I don't know about those reddish colors on the stem or the lack of fibrils on the cap. They might well be something else. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2012)

They look delicious, but they are probably poisonous to many people. I don't trust any of our woodland agaricus mushrooms.

File.Name: c426e_c426-057.jpg
Notes:
6971498570 I found this Russula emetica (or kin) mushroom in the woods today growing from the base of a dead Ceanothus burl under Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2012)

These mushrooms fruited some time ago in February, but here they are again, after our spring rains. See my [Newer] two photo.

I've heard this mushroom called "The Sickener" - though it's not that bad! I like to nibble on it - it's spicy hot, like wasabi. Sure, don't eat a plate of them, but handling them (and nibbling) won't hurt you at all. It's one of our most beautiful creatures!

File.Name: c426f_c426-039.jpg

File.Name: c426g_c426-030.jpg
Notes: 7117528027 This is looking down on the caterpillar of a Variable Checkerspot (Euphydryas chalcedona) butterfly, with raindrops. It's on a stem of Climbing Penstemon (Keckiella cordifolia) in the Scrophulariaceae plant family. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2012)

File.Name: c426h_c426-015.jpg
Notes:
7117495055 I went back along the road today, and our Southern Pacific Rattlesnake (Crotalus viridis helleri) - "la serpiente de cascabel" - was there again, in the same place. I guess we should put up a *Warning* sign here. (San Marcos Pass, 26 April 2012)

It's a wonderfully menacing snake. Rattlesnakes are quite dangerous, though this one just wanted to be left alone on a drizzly day. It let me get quite close, then decided it had enough and went back up the hillside. Julie says this photo reminds her of the curl of a wave.

I got this photo with my 105 mm macro lens, which on my DX camera is like a 150 mm telephoto. I wasn't THAT close.

File.Name: c427a_c427-080.jpg
Notes:
7120348949 I found this Blue-belly Lizard aka Western Fence Lizard (Sceloporus occidentalis) perched on an old sun-bleached deer antler in the garden this afternoon. It has mating colors, and such a sweet smile! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

I was expecting serious wind today, but it was beautiful and the wind was not too bad. There's so much to see in the woods right now. There's no other place I would rather be!

Notes:
6974225566 Push, mama, push!

6974186676 I would never have noticed this critter on the ground , but I saw it land and kept it in view while I moved my tripod in for the photo!

It's a small Damselfly (suborder Zygoptera, order Odonata). It has desert colors, and its perched on a desert-colored background. There's no standing water nearby that I know of, but here it is. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

File.Name: c427e_c427-032.jpg
Notes:
7120213977 This photo looks like the peyote-dream of a mushroom, but it's really a new insect gall. This is the spring bisexual gall of the Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp (Callirhytis quercuspomiformis) in the family Cynipidae of the order Hymenoptera. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

In a month or two, male and female wasps will emerge (from different galls), mate, and form the large "oak apple" summer galls, which contain only females. These females will emerge next spring to parthenogenetically produce a new generation of these bisexual galls. It's complicated, but life is like that! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

Note that these Live Oak oak apples are quite different from the more familiar oak apples that occur on Valley Oak (Quercus lobata) and Scrub Oak (Q. berberidifolia). Those galls are caused by California Gall Wasp (Andricus quercuscalifornicus) which seem to lack males altogether!
CommonNames: Live Oak Apple Gall Wasp; Valley Oak; Scrub Oak; California Gall Wasp
ScientificName: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Q. berberidifolia; Andricus quercuscalifornicus
associatedTaxa: Callirhytis quercuspomiformis; Cynipidae; Hymenoptera; Quercus lobata; Q. berberidifolia; Andricus quercuscalifornicus

File.Name: c427f_c427-014.jpg
Notes:
7120173043 I finally got a photo of an uncommon male Hermit Warbler (Dendroica occidentalis) bird! It's perched in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree off the north-west corner of our upstairs deck. They are quite shy. They have a black and white body and a brilliant yellow head with black patches above and below. I've seen them before, but this is my first photo. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

These migrating warblers see to come over the mountain in "mixed flocks" with several different species. It's a treat while they're here, but they don't stay long.

File.Name: c427g_c427-003.jpg
Notes:
6974068518 This is a sweet little Wilson's Warbler (Wilsonia pusilla) in the still-flowering Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) tree off the north-west corner of our upstairs deck. They have a distinctive black cap on their heads. I can't call them uncommon since I see them every fall and spring as they fly here and there, but it's uncommon for me to get a photo! (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

These migrating warblers seem to come over the mountain in "mixed flocks" with several different species. It's a treat while they're here, but they don't stay long.

File.Name: c427h_c427-009.jpg
Notes:
6974068518 There are migrating birds in the Coast Live Oak (Quercus agrifolia) trees lately. Not every day, but every now and then a mixed flock of birds flies through.

This is a beautiful male Townsend's Warbler (Dendroica townsendi). They are the migrating warbler that I see most often. (San Marcos Pass, 27 April 2012)

Sure it's a bit overexposed. My other birds today stayed in the shade, so I had my exposure kicked up a notch. I got this photo (and most of my bird photos) from our upstairs deck at home.
